## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question A1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]`
**Differentiate between a standard wheel, an omni wheel, and a mecanum wheel. Mention the roller orientation angle in each case.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **standard wheel** has a fixed orientation about its vertical axis and can only roll in the direction it points. It has no passive rollers. An **omni wheel** has passive rollers mounted at **90°** to the wheel's main rolling plane, allowing passive slip in the lateral direction. A **mecanum wheel** has passive tapered rollers mounted at **45°** to the wheel's main plane, allowing a combination of forward motion and lateral slip simultaneously. While a single omni or mecanum wheel is not holonomic, three or four such wheels properly combined on a single chassis produce a holonomic (omnidirectional) robot.

#### **Question A2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]`
**Define the term "holonomic mobile robot". Explain how special wheels enable holonomic motion.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **holonomic mobile robot** is one that can instantaneously move in any direction in its configuration space (the 2D plane + rotation), without requiring a non-zero path to reorient itself. Formally, the number of controllable degrees of freedom equals the total degrees of freedom of the robot. **Special wheels** enable holonomic motion by incorporating passive rollers that allow passive slip in directions perpendicular to the wheel's driven motion. By combining three or four such wheels with appropriately arranged roller orientations (e.g., mecanum wheels at 45° alternated RH/LH, or three omni wheels at 120°), the chassis achieves independent control of translation in X, translation in Y, and rotation about Z — all three DoF — simultaneously.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working principle of a **Mecanum wheel**. How does it differ from an **Omni wheel** in terms of roller orientation? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the forward kinematic equations relating the chassis velocities $(V_x, V_y, \omega_z)$ to the four individual wheel angular velocities $(\omega_1, \omega_2, \omega_3, \omega_4)$ for a 4-wheel mecanum drive robot. State clearly the no-slip constraint equations used. **(7 marks)**

##### **Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:**

A **Mecanum wheel** (invented by Bengt Ilon, 1973, at the Swedish company Mecanum AB) consists of a hub driven by a motor about its central axis. Around the rim of the wheel, a series of **tapered rubber rollers** (typically 8 to 12) are mounted on bearings. The axis of each roller is inclined at **45°** to the main plane of the wheel and also at **45°** to the wheel's axle. Two variants exist: **Right-Handed (RH)** and **Left-Handed (LH)**, distinguished by the direction of the roller taper (the two are mirror images).

**Working principle:** When the wheel is driven, the motor causes the hub to spin. The rubber rollers contact the ground. Since the rollers are free to spin passively on their bearings, the wheel can simultaneously (a) roll forward/backward in the direction of the axle (powered component) and (b) slide sideways along the direction perpendicular to the roller axis (passive slip component). The resulting ground velocity vector of the contact point is the vector sum of these two components.

**Difference from Omni Wheel:**
- **Omni wheel:** Rollers at **90°** to the wheel plane (perpendicular). The passive slip direction is exactly perpendicular to the wheel's rolling direction.
- **Mecanum wheel:** Rollers at **45°** to the wheel plane. The passive slip direction is at 45° to the wheel's rolling direction, allowing a more efficient decomposition of motion in 4-wheel holonomic arrangements.

A single omni wheel can only contribute to motion in 1 axis at a time, whereas a single mecanum wheel can contribute to motion along 2 non-orthogonal axes simultaneously, making it more versatile in a 4-wheel configuration.

> **Examiner's Note:** Award **2 marks** for the sketch, **3 marks** for working principle, and **2 marks** for the difference with omni wheel.

##### **Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Define geometry.** Let the robot chassis be a square of half-length $L$ and half-width $W$. The four mecanum wheels are at the corners with the following convention (alternating RH and LH):
- Wheel 1: Front-Left (FL) at $(+L, +W)$ — **RH** roller
- Wheel 2: Front-Right (FR) at $(+L, -W)$ — **LH** roller
- Wheel 3: Rear-Right (RR) at $(-L, -W)$ — **RH** roller
- Wheel 4: Rear-Left (RL) at $(-L, +W)$ — **LH** roller

**Step 2 — No-slip constraint for each wheel.** For each mecanum wheel, the component of contact-point velocity along the roller axis equals the driven tangential speed $r\omega_i$. For the alternating RH/LH pattern, the four constraint equations are:

$$V_x - V_y - (L+W)\omega_z = r\omega_1$$

$$V_x + V_y + (L+W)\omega_z = r\omega_2$$

$$V_x - V_y + (L+W)\omega_z = r\omega_3$$

$$V_x + V_y - (L+W)\omega_z = r\omega_4$$

**Step 3 — Solve the linear system.** Adding all four equations cancels $V_y$ and $\omega_z$ terms:

$$4V_x = r(\omega_1 + \omega_2 + \omega_3 + \omega_4)$$

$$V_x = \frac{r}{4}(\omega_1 + \omega_2 + \omega_3 + \omega_4)$$

For $V_y$, we add Eqn 2 and Eqn 4, and subtract Eqn 1 and Eqn 3:

$$4V_y = r(-\omega_1 + \omega_2 - \omega_3 + \omega_4)$$

$$V_y = \frac{r}{4}(-\omega_1 + \omega_2 - \omega_3 + \omega_4)$$

For $\omega_z$, we add Eqn 2 and Eqn 3, and subtract Eqn 1 and Eqn 4:

$$4(L+W)\omega_z = r(-\omega_1 + \omega_2 + \omega_3 - \omega_4)$$

$$\omega_z = \frac{r}{4(L+W)}(-\omega_1 + \omega_2 + \omega_3 - \omega_4)$$

> **Examiner's Note:** Award **2 marks** for writing the four constraint equations, **2 marks** for $V_x$ and $V_y$ derivations, **2 marks** for $\omega_z$ derivation, and **1 mark** for the final matrix form.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]`

**(a)** Describe the **Swedish 45° wheel** and **Spherical wheel**. Compare their holonomic capabilities with a standard mecanum drive. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A square mecanum-wheeled mobile robot has half-length $L = 0.25$ m, half-width $W = 0.25$ m, and wheel radius $r = 0.075$ m. The four wheel speeds are: $\omega_1 = 20$ rad/s, $\omega_2 = 20$ rad/s, $\omega_3 = -20$ rad/s, $\omega_4 = -20$ rad/s. Compute the chassis velocities $V_x$, $V_y$, and $\omega_z$. Interpret the motion. **(7 marks)**

##### **Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:**

**Swedish 45° Wheel:** The Swedish 45° wheel is a variant of the mecanum/omni family where the passive rollers are also mounted at 45° to the wheel plane, but the rim and roller geometry differ from the Ilon design. The key feature is that the roller contact points lie on the same circle as the wheel rim, providing uniform contact pressure. Three Swedish 45° wheels arranged at **120°** to each other on a triangular chassis yield a fully **holonomic 3-DoF** platform with smoother motion than mecanum equivalents in some configurations. Compared to mecanum, Swedish wheels typically have lower vibration and better load distribution.

**Spherical Wheel:** A spherical wheel (or ball wheel) uses a free-spinning ball as the contact element with the ground. The ball has **3 degrees of freedom** (rotation about X, Y, Z axes). Drive is provided by **external actuators** — usually three motor-driven rollers pressed against the ball from above/sides — that friction-drive the ball in arbitrary directions. Famous examples include the **CMU BallBot** and the ETH Zürich "Rezero" robot. Spherical wheels offer true point contact (no preferred direction) but have low payload capacity and require complex balancing/control.

**Comparison with standard mecanum drive:**

| Feature | Mecanum | Swedish 45° | Spherical |
|---|---|---|---|
| Number of wheels for holonomic base | 4 | 3 | 1 ball + 3 actuators |
| Roller orientation | 45° to wheel plane | 45° (refined geometry) | N/A (free ball) |
| Smoothness of motion | Moderate | High | High |
| Payload capacity | High | Moderate | Low |
| Mechanical complexity | Low | Moderate | High |
| Control complexity | Moderate | Moderate | High (often needs balancing) |

> **Examiner's Note:** Award **3 marks** for Swedish wheel description, **2 marks** for spherical wheel description, and **2 marks** for the comparison table.

##### **Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:**

**Given:** $L = W = 0.25$ m, $r = 0.075$ m, $\omega_1 = \omega_2 = 20$ rad/s, $\omega_3 = \omega_4 = -20$ rad/s.

**Step 1 — Apply the $V_x$ formula:**

$$V_x = \frac{r}{4}(\omega_1 + \omega_2 + \omega_3 + \omega_4)$$

$$V_x = \frac{0.075}{4}(20 + 20 + (-20) + (-20))$$

$$V_x = \frac{0.075}{4}(0) = 0 \; \text{m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the $V_y$ formula:**

$$V_y = \frac{r}{4}(-\omega_1 + \omega_2 - \omega_3 + \omega_4)$$

$$V_y = \frac{0.075}{4}(-20 + 20 - (-20) + (-20))$$

$$V_y = \frac{0.075}{4}(-20 + 20 + 20 - 20)$$

$$V_y = \frac{0.075}{4}(0) = 0 \; \text{m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the $\omega_z$ formula:**

$$\omega_z = \frac{r}{4(L+W)}(-\omega_1 + \omega_2 + \omega_3 - \omega_4)$$

$$\omega_z = \frac{0.075}{4(0.25 + 0.25)}(-20 + 20 + (-20) - (-20))$$

$$\omega_z = \frac{0.075}{4(0.50)}(-20 + 20 - 20 + 20)$$

$$\omega_z = \frac{0.075}{2.0}(0) = 0 \; \text{rad/s}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation of the result:**
All three chassis velocities are **zero**. This is a counter-intuitive but pedagogically important result! Setting the front wheels to $+20$ rad/s and the rear wheels to $-20$ rad/s creates a motion pattern that the wheel arrangement exactly cancels out. This is sometimes called a "diagonal counter-spin" — it produces internal forces but no net motion. In practice, this wheel speed combination would be **avoided** as it wastes energy and creates mechanical stress.

> **Examiner's Note:** Award **2 marks** for $V_x$, **2 marks** for $V_y$, **2 marks** for $\omega_z$, and **1 mark** for the physical interpretation.

---

### 5.3 Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Confusing $L$ and $2L$:** A frequent error is using the *full* length of the chassis instead of the *half-length* in the $\omega_z$ formula. If the question says "chassis is $0.5 \text{ m} \times 0.5 \text{ m}$," then $L = W = 0.25$ m, not 0.5 m. Many students lose **1–2 marks** here.
> 2. **Forgetting the roller convention (RH vs LH):** The formulas above are valid only with the alternating RH/LH pattern. If all four wheels are RH or all four are LH, the sign patterns in $V_y$ and $\omega_z$ change completely. Always state the convention.
> 3. **Mixing up wheel numbering:** Wheel 1 is not always "front-left" in textbook conventions. KTU typically follows the convention: 1=FL, 2=FR, 3=RR, 4=RL. Verify the convention used in your module notes.
> 4. **Omitting units:** Always include units in the final answer (m/s for linear, rad/s for angular). Missing units = lost marks in KTU valuation.
> 5. **Forgetting the "interpretation" step:** KTU examiners award **1 mark** specifically for interpreting the physical meaning of computed velocities (e.g., "this represents pure rotation about the chassis center"). Do not skip this.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Special wheels** are non-standard wheel designs that incorporate **passive rollers** to enable motion in directions otherwise forbidden for a rigid wheel.
- The four main types are: **Mecanum (45° rollers)**, **Omni (90° rollers)**, **Swedish (45° refined rollers)**, and **Spherical (free ball with external actuation)**.
- A single special wheel is **NOT holonomic**; holonomic behavior emerges only from the coordinated combination of **3 or 4 wheels** on one chassis.
- **Mecanum wheels** must be arranged in **alternating RH/LH** pattern to achieve holonomic motion.
- The **forward kinematics** of a 4-wheel mecanum robot are governed by three linear equations:
  - $V_x = \frac{r}{4}(\omega_1 + \omega_2 + \omega_3 + \omega_4)$
  - $V_y = \frac{r}{4}(-\omega_1 + \omega_2 - \omega_3 + \omega_4)$
  - $\omega_z = \frac{r}{4(L+W)}(-\omega_1 + \omega_2 + \omega_3 - \omega_4)$
- **Three omni wheels at 120°** can also achieve full holonomic motion, with the Jacobian matrix involving $\sin(120°)$ and $\cos(120°)$ terms.
- **Special wheel applications:** AGVs, robot soccer, hospital service robots, warehouse automation, space rovers, and EOD robots.
- The **Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR)** for an omnidirectional robot can be located at infinity (pure translation) or at any finite point (combined translation + rotation).
- **Key limitation:** Omnidirectional wheels have **lower payload capacity** and **less efficiency** on rough terrain compared to standard wheels, due to the small roller contact patches.
- The **roller orientation angle is the defining parameter** of each special wheel type — remember: **45° = Mecanum/Swedish, 90° = Omni**.
- For a **spherical wheel** drive, the ball's contact point is the ICR, and the drive rollers apply forces to control the ball's rotation in any axis.
- **Holonomic Index** formula: $H = 3 - n_{\text{constraints}}$. For a holonomic robot, $H = 3$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Drive Types - Differential Drive
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET744 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Drive Types - Differential Drive

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

A **Differential Drive** (also called a **Differential Wheeled Robot** or **Skid-Steer Drive** in its tracked variant) is a non-holonomic, two-wheeled mobile robot locomotion configuration in which **two independently actuated drive wheels** are mounted on a common axle, and the robot's motion—both *linear translation* and *rotation about its own vertical axis*—is generated entirely by the **difference in the angular velocities** of these two wheels.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1 – Locomotion & Drive Types):**
> Differential drive is a **unicycle-class** mobile platform where motion is governed by the **unconstrained rolling constraint** of two coaxial wheels. It is the canonical example of a **non-holonomic** robot in the KTU Mobile Robotics syllabus.

### Key Terminology (Board-Examiner Standard)

| Term | Standard Symbol | Engineering Meaning |
|------|----------------|---------------------|
| Wheel radius | $r$ | Radius of each drive wheel (m) |
| Wheelbase / Axle length | $L$ or $2l$ | Distance between the two drive wheels (m) |
| Right wheel angular velocity | $\omega_R$ | Angular speed of right wheel (rad/s) |
| Left wheel angular velocity | $\omega_L$ | Angular speed of left wheel (rad/s) |
| Robot linear velocity | $v$ | Forward speed of robot center (m/s) |
| Robot angular velocity | $\omega$ | Rotational rate of robot (rad/s) |

> [!NOTE]
> The "differential" in the name arises from the **velocity differential** $(v_R - v_L)$ that produces **rotation without any dedicated steering mechanism**. This is fundamentally different from a *car-like* (Ackermann) drive which uses a steering angle.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of a **wheelchair** pushed by a caregiver. The caregiver has **two independent rear wheels** on the same axle. To make the wheelchair **go straight**, both wheels are pushed at the same speed. To **turn right**, the right wheel is slowed down (or held still) while the left wheel is pushed. To **spin in place (zero-radius turn)**, the two wheels are driven in **opposite directions** at equal speed—the wheelchair rotates about its own center.

> A tank's caterpillar tracks are essentially a **continuous differential drive**: the left and right track speeds are independently controlled, and the tank turns by skidding one track faster than the other.

Similarly, a **shopping cart** with two swiveling front wheels and two fixed rear wheels behaves *differentially* when you push it sideways—both rear wheels roll, but at different rates along their instantaneous arcs.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick for Exams:** "**Same speed → Straight**, **Different speed → Curve**, **Opposite speed → Spin**."

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Minimum turning radius of a differential drive = 0 m** (it can rotate in place, unlike a car).
- **Degree of Mobility** = 2 (can independently control 2 DOF: $v$ and $\omega$ in the ground plane).
- **Degree of Steerability** = 0 (no steering wheel; direction is implicit in wheel speeds).
- **Maneuverability Degree** = 2 (Mobility − Steerability = 2), satisfying the universal formula $M \le 3 - S$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The three canonical drive types in mobile robotics are: (1) **Differential Drive**, (2) **Synchronous Drive** (e.g., Synchro Drive), and (3) **Car-like (Ackermann) Drive**. Differential drive is unique in offering **zero-radius turning** at the cost of needing both wheels to be driven.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — The "Why" and "How"

The differential drive works on the **rigid-body rolling without slipping** assumption:

- **Pure rolling constraint** — the contact point of each wheel with the ground is **instantaneously at rest**.
- **No lateral slip** — wheels cannot move sideways; all motion is in the wheel's plane of rotation.
- **Independent actuation** — each wheel is driven by its own motor (often with an encoder for odometry).

### Operational Logic (Step-by-Step Reasoning)

1. **Step 1 — Wheel velocities to ground velocities:** Each drive wheel, when rotating at $\omega_R$ and $\omega_L$, produces linear velocities $v_R = r\omega_R$ and $v_L = r\omega_L$ at the contact point.
2. **Step 2 — Center velocity computation:** The robot's body center moves at the **arithmetic mean** of the two wheel velocities:
   $$v = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2} = \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}$$
3. **Step 3 — Angular velocity computation:** The **difference** in wheel velocities creates a torque couple about the robot's center, producing rotation:
   $$\omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L} = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L}$$
4. **Step 4 — Motion composition:** The robot's motion is a **superposition** of forward translation at $v$ and rotation at $\omega$.

## 2.2 The Instantaneous Center of Curvature (ICC)

Every non-straight motion of a differential drive robot can be described as a **pure rotation about a point** called the **Instantaneous Center of Curvature (ICC)**. The ICC lies on the line passing through the two drive wheels, on the side of the **faster wheel**.

- If $\omega_R = \omega_L$ → ICC is at **infinity** (straight-line motion).
- If $\omega_R = -\omega_L$ → ICC is **at the robot center** (in-place rotation).
- Otherwise → ICC is at a finite distance $R$ from the robot center.

The **radius of curvature** $R$ is:
$$R = \frac{L}{2} \cdot \frac{v_R + v_L}{v_R - v_L} = \frac{L}{2} \cdot \frac{\omega_R + \omega_L}{\omega_R - \omega_L}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The ICC concept is the bridge between **wheel-level control** and **path planning**. Trajectory planners issue $(v, \omega)$ commands, and the differential drive mechanically realizes them through wheel velocity pairs.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Board-Exam Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description | Variables & Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------------------|
| 1 | $v_R = r\,\omega_R$ | Right wheel linear velocity | $r$ (m), $\omega_R$ (rad/s) |
| 2 | $v_L = r\,\omega_L$ | Left wheel linear velocity | $r$ (m), $\omega_L$ (rad/s) |
| 3 | $v = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2}$ | Robot linear (forward) velocity | $v$ (m/s) |
| 4 | $\omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L}$ | Robot angular velocity | $\omega$ (rad/s), $L$ (m) |
| 5 | $R = \frac{L(v_R + v_L)}{2(v_R - v_L)}$ | Radius of curvature (ICC distance) | $R$ (m) |
| 6 | $v_L = v - \frac{L\omega}{2}$ | Inverse kinematics (left wheel) | $v_L$ (m/s) |
| 7 | $v_R = v + \frac{L\omega}{2}$ | Inverse kinematics (right wheel) | $v_R$ (m/s) |
| 8 | $x_{k+1} = x_k + v_k\cos\theta_k \cdot \Delta t$ | Odometry update (x) | All in SI units |
| 9 | $y_{k+1} = y_k + v_k\sin\theta_k \cdot \Delta t$ | Odometry update (y) | All in SI units |
| 10 | $\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \omega_k \cdot \Delta t$ | Odometry update (heading) | $\theta$ (rad) |
| 11 | $\omega_R = \frac{2v + L\omega}{2r}$ | Inverse wheel angular velocity (R) | (rad/s) |
| 12 | $\omega_L = \frac{2v - L\omega}{2r}$ | Inverse wheel angular velocity (L) | (rad/s) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Tip:** The forward kinematic map of a differential drive is **invertible** only if the wheel radius $r > 0$ and wheelbase $L > 0$. Always state these **boundary conditions** in your answer. Also, note the **sign convention**: positive $\omega$ is *counter-clockwise* (left turn) when the robot's $x$-axis points forward and $y$-axis points left (standard KTU convention).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Domestic Robots:** Roomba, Xiaomi Mi Robot Vacuum — all use differential drive for the zero-radius turning in cluttered rooms.
- **Warehouse AMRs:** Kiva/Amazon robots use differential drives for navigation between shelves.
- **Mars Rovers (Spirit, Opportunity, Curiosity):** Use **rocker-bogie + differential steering** for rough terrain.
- **Educational Platforms:** LEGO Mindstorms, TurtleBot, Pioneer 3-DX, Roomba — all are differential drive.
- **Military/Defense:** Tactical ground robots (iRobot PackBot) for bomb disposal.
- **Research:** Self-driving car dynamics are *often simulated* on differential-drive platforms due to identical kinematic structure at low speeds.

> [!TIP]
> The differential drive is a **unicycle model** mathematically; any path planned for a unicycle can be tracked by a differential drive via the inverse kinematics (Formulas 6, 7).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Robot Linear Velocity $v$ from Wheel Velocities

**Given:** Two wheels of radius $r$ rotating at $\omega_R$ and $\omega_L$. The wheels are coaxial, separated by distance $L$.

**Step 1.** For pure rolling without slipping, the linear velocity of each wheel's contact point equals:
$$v_R = r\,\omega_R \qquad \text{and} \qquad v_L = r\,\omega_L$$

**Step 2.** The robot body, being rigid, moves forward. The center of the axle translates at velocity $v$, which is the **average** of the two wheel velocities (since the center is the geometric midpoint of the axle):
$$v = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2}$$

**Step 3.** Substituting the wheel linear velocities from Step 1:
$$v = \frac{r\,\omega_R + r\,\omega_L}{2} = \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}$$

> This is the **forward kinematic relation**: given wheel speeds, find robot body speed.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Robot Angular Velocity $\omega$ from Wheel Velocities

**Step 1.** The two wheels trace circles of radii $R$ (right) and $(R - L)$ (left) about the ICC, where $R$ is the distance from the robot center to the ICC and $L$ is the wheelbase.

**Step 2.** Because the robot is a rigid body, both wheels share the **same angular velocity** $\omega$ about the ICC. Therefore:
$$v_R = \omega(R + L/2) \quad \text{(right wheel is on the outer arc, distance } R + L/2 \text{ from ICC)}$$
$$v_L = \omega(R - L/2) \quad \text{(left wheel is on the inner arc)}$$

**Step 3.** Subtract the two equations to eliminate $R$:
$$v_R - v_L = \omega(R + L/2) - \omega(R - L/2)$$
$$v_R - v_L = \omega \cdot L$$

**Step 4.** Solve for $\omega$:
$$\omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L} = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L}$$

> This is the **angular velocity equation** of the robot. The sign of $\omega$ indicates turning direction: positive $\Rightarrow$ counter-clockwise (left turn).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Radius of Curvature $R$ (ICC Distance)

**Step 1.** Add the two ICC velocity equations from Derivation 2, Step 2:
$$v_R + v_L = \omega(R + L/2) + \omega(R - L/2) = 2\omega R$$

**Step 2.** Divide this by the difference equation $v_R - v_L = \omega L$:
$$\frac{v_R + v_L}{v_R - v_L} = \frac{2\omega R}{\omega L} = \frac{2R}{L}$$

**Step 3.** Solve for $R$:
$$R = \frac{L}{2} \cdot \frac{v_R + v_L}{v_R - v_L}$$

> **Special Cases (High-Yield for Board Exams):**
> - $\omega_R = \omega_L$ ⇒ $v_R = v_L$ ⇒ denominator is 0 ⇒ $R = \infty$ (straight-line motion).
> - $\omega_R = -\omega_L$ ⇒ $v_R = -v_L$ ⇒ $R = 0$ (in-place rotation).

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Inverse Kinematics (Body Velocity $\to$ Wheel Velocities)

**Given:** Desired robot linear velocity $v$ and angular velocity $\omega$. Find required $v_R$ and $v_L$.

**Step 1.** We have two equations from the forward kinematics:
$$v = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad v_R + v_L = 2v$$
$$\omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L} \quad \Rightarrow \quad v_R - v_L = \omega L$$

**Step 2.** Add the two linear equations:
$$(v_R + v_L) + (v_R - v_L) = 2v + \omega L$$
$$2v_R = 2v + \omega L$$
$$v_R = v + \frac{L\omega}{2}$$

**Step 3.** Subtract the two linear equations:
$$(v_R + v_L) - (v_R - v_L) = 2v - \omega L$$
$$2v_L = 2v - \omega L$$
$$v_L = v - \frac{L\omega}{2}$$

> This is the **inverse kinematic map** — the bridge between a planned trajectory $(v, \omega)$ and the actual motor commands.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Odometry Update Equations (Dead-Reckoning)

**Given:** At time $t_k$, the robot is at pose $(x_k, y_k, \theta_k)$. Over a time step $\Delta t$, it moves with $(v_k, \omega_k)$.

**Step 1.** The robot's instantaneous velocity in the world frame has components:
$$\dot{x} = v_k \cos\theta_k, \quad \dot{y} = v_k \sin\theta_k, \quad \dot{\theta} = \omega_k$$

**Step 2.** Assuming $\Delta t$ is small enough that $(v_k, \omega_k)$ are approximately constant:
$$x_{k+1} = x_k + \int_{t_k}^{t_{k+1}} v_k\cos\theta_k \, dt = x_k + v_k\cos\theta_k\,\Delta t$$
$$y_{k+1} = y_k + v_k\sin\theta_k\,\Delta t$$
$$\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \omega_k\,\Delta t$$

> This is the **odometry / dead-reckoning update**. It is exact for straight lines and arcs in the continuous limit, but accumulates error in practice.

---

## 3.6 Derivation 6 — General Case for a Curved Path Segment (Exact Arc)

For a path segment of duration $\Delta t$ with **constant** $(v, \omega)$ and $\omega \neq 0$:

**Step 1.** The robot traces an arc of radius $R = v/\omega$ centered at:
$$x_{ICC} = x_k - R\sin\theta_k, \quad y_{ICC} = y_k + R\cos\theta_k$$

**Step 2.** The new pose is:
$$x_{k+1} = x_{ICC} + R\sin(\theta_k + \omega\Delta t)$$
$$y_{k+1} = y_{ICC} - R\cos(\theta_k + \omega\Delta t)$$
$$\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \omega\Delta t$$

**Step 3.** Substituting $R = v/\omega$:
$$\begin{aligned}
x_{k+1} &= x_k + \frac{v}{\omega}\left[\sin(\theta_k + \omega\Delta t) - \sin\theta_k\right] \\
y_{k+1} &= y_k - \frac{v}{\omega}\left[\cos(\theta_k + \omega\Delta t) - \cos\theta_k\right] \\
\theta_{k+1} &= \theta_k + \omega\Delta t
\end{aligned}$$

> This is the **exact odometry model** for a constant $(v, \omega)$ command, valid for both straight and turning motions (when $\omega \to 0$, use L'Hôpital's rule to recover the straight-line update).

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    C --> E[Left Motor Controller]
    D --> F[Right Wheel Actuator]
    E --> G[Left Wheel Actuator]

    G --> H
    H --> I[Wheel Encoders]

```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Differential Drive Kinematic Model

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph inputs["Input: Wheel Angular Velocities"]
        wr["omega R rad per sec"]
        wl["omega L rad per sec"]
    end
    subgraph multiply["Multiply by wheel radius r"]

        vl["vL = r times omega L"]
    end
    subgraph forward["Forward Kinematics"]
        vout["Robot linear velocity v = vR plus vL divided by 2"]
        wout["Robot angular velocity omega = vR minus vL divided by L"]
    end
    subgraph icc["ICC Computation"]
        iccR["Radius R = L divided by 2 times vR plus vL over vR minus vL"]
    end

        transl["Translation at v"]
        rot["Rotation at omega"]
    end
    wr --> vr
    wl --> vl
    vr --> vout
    vl --> vout
    vr --> wout
    vl --> wout
    vr --> iccR
    vl --> iccR
```


## 4.4 Schematic Description (Physical Geometry)

For the board exam, sketch the following on your answer sheet:

```
        y
        ↑
        |
   ●────┼────●   ← Right wheel (vR) and Left wheel (vL) at distance L
   L_w  |  R_w
        |←— L —→|
        |
        ●          ← Robot center C
        |
        ↓ x
```

- Two drive wheels on the **y-axis** (or x-axis depending on convention), separated by **$L$**.
- **Caster wheel** (passive, often omitted in symmetric models) at front or back for stability.
- The robot's heading $\theta$ is measured from the global $x$-axis to the robot's local $x$-axis.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Always draw the wheelbase, mark $v_R$ and $v_L$ as arrows, indicate the ICC on the appropriate side, and label the radius of curvature $R$. A well-labeled figure alone can fetch 2–3 marks in a 14-mark question.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**"Differentiate between differential drive and Ackermann drive in terms of degrees of freedom, maneuverability, and turning radius."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Differential Drive | Ackermann (Car-Like) Drive |
|-----------|-------------------|---------------------------|
| Degrees of Freedom (Mobility) | 2 | 2 |
| Degrees of Steerability | 0 | 1 |
| Minimum Turning Radius | 0 m (in-place turn possible) | $> L/\sin\phi_{\max}$ (finite) |
| Drive Mechanism | Two independent wheels + differential | Front-wheel steering |
| Kinematic Constraint | Non-holonomic (1 constraint) | Non-holonomic (2 constraints) |

> **Examiner Note:** Students often confuse *maneuverability degree* with *mobility degree*. The correct formula is $M \le 3 - S$ (Maneuverability $\le$ 3 − Steerability), and for differential drive: $M = 3 - 0 = 3$ only if slip is allowed; in pure rolling, $M = 2$. **(1 mark for correct table, 1 for turning radius, 1 for naming the constraint type.)**

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**"What is the Instantaneous Center of Curvature (ICC) for a differential drive robot? Derive the expression for the radius of curvature $R$."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Instantaneous Center of Curvature (ICC)** is the point about which a differential drive robot instantaneously rotates when the wheel velocities are unequal. It lies on the extended line connecting the two drive wheels.

Let the right and left wheel linear velocities be $v_R$ and $v_L$ respectively, with wheelbase $L$. Both wheels share the same angular velocity $\omega$ about the ICC. Setting up:
$$v_R = \omega(R + L/2), \quad v_L = \omega(R - L/2)$$

Solving:
$$R = \frac{L}{2} \cdot \frac{v_R + v_L}{v_R - v_L}$$

**(1 mark for ICC definition, 1 for setting up equations, 1 for final expression.)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part-B question is **14 marks**, split into two sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Below are TWO full alternative questions for choice.

---

### 🅰️ Question A [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat kinematic diagram of a differential drive mobile robot, derive the forward kinematic equations relating the robot's linear and angular velocities $(v, \omega)$ to the wheel angular velocities $(\omega_R, \omega_L)$. Clearly define all variables and state the rolling-without-slipping assumptions.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define variables and assumptions:** **(1 mark)**
Let $r$ = radius of each drive wheel, $L$ = distance between the two wheels (wheelbase), $\omega_R, \omega_L$ = angular velocities of right and left wheels. The wheels roll on the ground **without slipping**, and the robot is a **rigid body** in the plane.

**Step 2 — Wheel contact-point velocities:** **(1 mark)**
For pure rolling: $v_R = r\omega_R$ and $v_L = r\omega_L$.

**Step 3 — Express $v$ in terms of $v_R, v_L$:** **(1 mark)**
The robot center is the geometric midpoint of the axle. By rigid-body averaging:
$$v = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Derive angular velocity:** **(2 marks)**
The two wheels trace concentric circles of radii $R + L/2$ and $R - L/2$ about the ICC. Both share the same $\omega$. So:
$$v_R - v_L = \omega L \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L} = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute wheel velocities:** **(1 mark)**
$$v = \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}, \quad \omega = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L}$$

**Step 6 — Conclusion with diagram description:** **(1 mark)**
The forward kinematic map takes the 2D wheel-velocity input $(\omega_R, \omega_L)$ and produces the 2D body-velocity output $(v, \omega)$. The map is **invertible** (provided $r, L > 0$), making the differential drive a **controllable** holonomic-in-the-control-space but non-holonomic-in-the-configuration-space platform.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A differential drive robot has wheel radius $r = 0.05$ m and wheelbase $L = 0.30$ m. If the right wheel rotates at $\omega_R = 10$ rad/s and the left wheel at $\omega_L = 6$ rad/s, compute:
  (i) the linear velocity $v$ of the robot,
  (ii) the angular velocity $\omega$ of the robot,
  (iii) the radius of curvature $R$ of the path,
  (iv) the position and orientation after $\Delta t = 2$ seconds, starting from $(x_0, y_0, \theta_0) = (0, 0, 0)$ (use the linearized odometry update).

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $r = 0.05$ m, $L = 0.30$ m, $\omega_R = 10$ rad/s, $\omega_L = 6$ rad/s, $\Delta t = 2$ s, initial pose $(0, 0, 0)$.

**Step 1 — Compute wheel linear velocities:** **(1 mark)**
$$v_R = r\omega_R = 0.05 \times 10 = 0.5 \text{ m/s}$$
$$v_L = r\omega_L = 0.05 \times 6 = 0.3 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Linear velocity $v$:** **(1 mark)**
$$v = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2} = \frac{0.5 + 0.3}{2} = 0.4 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Angular velocity $\omega$:** **(1 mark)**
$$\omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L} = \frac{0.5 - 0.3}{0.30} = \frac{0.2}{0.3} = \frac{2}{3} \approx 0.667 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 4 — Radius of curvature $R$:** **(1 mark)**
$$R = \frac{L(v_R + v_L)}{2(v_R - v_L)} = \frac{0.30 \times 0.8}{2 \times 0.2} = \frac{0.24}{0.4} = 0.6 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 — Update orientation:** **(1 mark)**
$$\theta(\Delta t) = \theta_0 + \omega\Delta t = 0 + 0.667 \times 2 = 1.333 \text{ rad} \approx 76.4°$$

**Step 6 — Update position (linearized):** **(2 marks)**
$$x(2) = 0 + v\cos\theta_0 \cdot \Delta t = 0.4 \times \cos(0) \times 2 = 0.8 \text{ m}$$
$$y(2) = 0 + v\sin\theta_0 \cdot \Delta t = 0.4 \times \sin(0) \times 2 = 0 \text{ m}$$

**Final Pose:** $(0.8 \text{ m}, \, 0 \text{ m}, \, 1.333 \text{ rad})$.

> **Note:** The linearized update at $\theta_0 = 0$ gives $y = 0$, but the **exact arc model** would give $y = 0.4 \times (1 - \cos(1.333)) / 0.667 \approx 0.328$ m. The linearized form is only an approximation; the exam expects you to use the linearized update for "small" $\Delta t$, and the arc model otherwise.

---

### 🅱️ Question B [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of holonomic and non-holonomic constraints as applied to a differential drive mobile robot. Show mathematically that the differential drive is non-holonomic and discuss its controllability implications.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definitions:** **(2 marks)**
A **holonomic constraint** is a constraint on the configuration that can be expressed as an equation involving only the configuration variables (and not their derivatives). A **non-holonomic constraint** is a constraint on the velocities (or differentials) that is **not integrable** to a configuration-level constraint.

**Step 2 — Differential drive kinematic constraint:** **(2 marks)**
For a differential drive moving with forward velocity $v$ and angular velocity $\omega$ at heading $\theta$, the configuration is $(x, y, \theta)$. The velocity in the world frame is:
$$\dot{x} = v\cos\theta, \quad \dot{y} = v\sin\theta, \quad \dot{\theta} = \omega$$

The **no-sideways-slip constraint** for a unicycle / differential drive is:
$$\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$$

This is a **non-integrable** linear constraint on the velocities. It cannot be reduced to a finite equation in $(x, y, \theta)$ alone. Hence, the differential drive is **non-holonomic**.

**Step 3 — Implications:** **(2 marks)**
- The robot has 3 configuration variables $(x, y, \theta)$ but only **2 control inputs** $(\omega_R, \omega_L)$ or equivalently $(v, \omega)$.
- The configuration space is 3D, but the reachable velocities lie in a 2D subspace.
- However, the system is **controllable in the small** (by Chow's theorem / Lie algebra rank condition) — the robot can reach any configuration through a sufficiently rich maneuver (e.g., parallel parking).
- Trajectory planning must respect the non-holonomic constraint (e.g., Reeds-Shepp curves, Dubins paths).

**Step 4 — Conclusion:** **(1 mark)**
The differential drive is the canonical example of a non-holonomic mobile robot, and its study underpins modern motion planning theory.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Using the inverse kinematics of a differential drive, design the wheel velocities required to make the robot follow a circular arc of radius $R = 2$ m at a forward speed of $v = 0.5$ m/s. Take $r = 0.04$ m and $L = 0.25$ m. Then, verify the curvature of the resulting motion.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify required $(v, \omega)$:** **(1 mark)**
For a circular arc of radius $R = 2$ m at $v = 0.5$ m/s:
$$\omega = \frac{v}{R} = \frac{0.5}{2} = 0.25 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Apply inverse kinematics:** **(2 marks)**
$$v_R = v + \frac{L\omega}{2} = 0.5 + \frac{0.25 \times 0.25}{2} = 0.5 + 0.03125 = 0.53125 \text{ m/s}$$
$$v_L = v - \frac{L\omega}{2} = 0.5 - 0.03125 = 0.46875 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to wheel angular velocities:** **(1 mark)**
$$\omega_R = \frac{v_R}{r} = \frac{0.53125}{0.04} = 13.28 \text{ rad/s}$$
$$\omega_L = \frac{v_L}{r} = \frac{0.46875}{0.04} = 11.72 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 4 — Verify radius of curvature:** **(2 marks)**
$$R_{\text{actual}} = \frac{L(v_R + v_L)}{2(v_R - v_L)} = \frac{0.25 \times 1.0}{2 \times 0.0625} = \frac{0.25}{0.125} = 2.0 \text{ m} \;\checkmark$$

**Step 5 — Verify forward speed:** **(1 mark)**
$$v_{\text{actual}} = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2} = \frac{1.0}{2} = 0.5 \text{ m/s} \;\checkmark$$

> **Conclusion:** The wheel speeds $(\omega_R, \omega_L) = (13.28, 11.72)$ rad/s produce exactly the desired motion. This is the inverse-kinematic verification pattern that KTU examiners love.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Sign Convention Error:** Forgetting that positive $\omega$ is *counter-clockwise* (left turn). Board examiners **deduct 1–2 marks** for inconsistent sign usage between sub-parts.
>
> 2. **Confusing $L$ and $2l$:** Some textbooks (and past KTU papers) use $2l$ for wheelbase. If the problem says $2l = 0.5$ m, then $l = 0.25$ m. **Always read the variable definitions** before substituting.
>
> 3. **Dividing by Zero in $R$:** When $v_R = v_L$, the formula $R = L(v_R+v_L)/[2(v_R - v_L)]$ **blows up** — this is a *valid* result meaning $R = \infty$ (straight line). **State this explicitly** in your answer; do not just write "undefined."
>
> 4. **Forgetting the Wheel Radius in Conversions:** A common slip is to write $\omega = (\omega_R - \omega_L)/L$ instead of $\omega = r(\omega_R - \omega_L)/L$. The wheel radius $r$ is the bridge between **angular** and **linear** quantities.
>
> 5. **Linearized vs Exact Odometry:** At $\theta_0 = 0$, the linearized update $y = v\sin\theta_0 \Delta t = 0$ is correct **only because $\theta_0 = 0$**. For general $\theta_0$, the linearization is a **first-order** approximation. Examiners may specifically ask: *"Is the linearized form valid for $\theta_0 = 90°$?"* — the answer is no, you must use the exact arc model.
>
> 6. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing rad/s with rev/s, or degrees with radians. **Always state units at every step** — KTU 2024 evaluation guidelines specifically reward unit discipline.
>
> 7. **Non-Holonomic Confusion:** Many students incorrectly classify a differential drive as "holonomic" because it has 2 control inputs. **Holonomic refers to the constraint**, not the number of inputs.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📌 **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print This Before the Exam!)**

- ✅ Differential drive has **two coaxial independently driven wheels** + a passive caster (or two).
- ✅ **Forward Kinematics:** $v = r(\omega_R + \omega_L)/2$, $\omega = r(\omega_R - \omega_L)/L$.
- ✅ **Inverse Kinematics:** $v_R = v + L\omega/2$, $v_L = v - L\omega/2$.
- ✅ **ICC Radius:** $R = L(v_R + v_L) / [2(v_R - v_L)]$.
- ✅ **Straight line:** $\omega_R = \omega_L$ ⇒ $v_R = v_L$ ⇒ $R = \infty$.
- ✅ **In-place turn:** $\omega_R = -\omega_L$ ⇒ $v_R = -v_L$ ⇒ $R = 0$.
- ✅ **Non-holonomic constraint:** $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$.
- ✅ **Maneuverability = Mobility − Steerability** = 2 − 0 = 2 (for unicycle class).
- ✅ **Minimum turning radius = 0 m** (key advantage over car-like drives).
- ✅ **Degree of mobility = 2**, **degree of steerability = 0**.
- ✅ **Pose update (linearized):** $(x, y, \theta) \to (x + v\cos\theta\Delta t, \; y + v\sin\theta\Delta t, \; \theta + \omega\Delta t)$.
- ✅ **Pose update (exact arc):** Requires $\omega \neq 0$; uses $\sin/\cos$ of $(\theta + \omega\Delta t)$.
- ✅ **Pure rolling + no slip** are the two physical assumptions.
- ✅ **Sign convention:** $+v$ forward, $+\omega$ counter-clockwise (left turn).
- ✅ **Examples:** Roomba, TurtleBot, Pioneer 3-DX, LEGO Mindstorms, Mars rovers.
- ✅ **Practical issues:** Wheel slippage, encoder quantization, uneven floor → odometry drift.
- ✅ **Path planning:** Reeds-Shepp, Dubins curves; the differential drive is a **unicycle model**.

> 🎯 **Last-Memory Hook:** *"Same speed = straight; different speed = curve; opposite speed = spin; zero radius is its superpower."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tricycle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET744 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Tricycle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Tricycle** is a three-wheeled, non-holonomic mobile robot drive configuration that belongs to the classical **(2, 1) maneuverability class** (Campion, Bastin, and D'Andrea-Novel classification, 1996). It consists of:

- **One steerable, driven wheel** (typically placed at the front, acting as the traction + steering actuator).
- **Two fixed, passive wheels** mounted on a common rear axle (coaxial, non-steerable).

The robot is described in a global inertial frame $\mathcal{F}_W = \{O_W, X_W, Y_W\}$ and a robot-centric frame $\mathcal{F}_R = \{O_R, X_R, Y_R\}$ located at the midpoint of the rear axle. The configuration state is given by the pose vector:

$$q = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ \theta \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^2 \times S^1$$

where $(x, y)$ denotes the global position of $O_R$ and $\theta$ is the orientation of $X_R$ relative to $X_W$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Tricycle model is a fundamental building block in Mobile Robotics. It is essentially a simplified "bicycle model" used to represent car-like and three-wheeled autonomous vehicles. Mastery of its forward and inverse kinematics is **mandatory** for ESE Module 1 derivations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **golf cart** or a **child's pedal tricycle**. You steer the single front wheel to the left or right, and the two rear wheels roll straight along their axle. As you turn the steering wheel, the front wheel pivots and the entire cart describes a smooth circular arc. The tighter you turn the steering wheel (larger steering angle), the smaller the circle.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Summary:**
> - **Steering wheel angle** = "how sharply am I turning?"
> - **Driving wheel velocity** = "how fast am I moving?"
> - These two are the *only* inputs. The robot has **2 degrees of freedom (DOF)** in its configuration space (forward translation + in-place rotation, but **NOT** sideways sliding — that is the holonomic constraint).
> - Because the robot **cannot move sideways**, it is *non-holonomic* — the same constraint that prevents you from parking a real car perpendicular to the road.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Parameters

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Unit |
|-----------|--------|--------------|
| Wheelbase (rear axle to front wheel) | $L$ | **meters (m)** |
| Front wheel steering angle | $\varphi$ | **radians (rad)** |
| Front wheel linear velocity | $v$ | **m/s** |
| Turning radius (from rear axle) | $R$ | **m** |
| Robot orientation | $\theta$ | **rad** |

The **non-holonomic constraint** that defines the Tricycle's motion (the "no-sideways-sliding" rule) is mathematically:

$$\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$$

This single first-order, non-integrable differential equation enforces that the robot's velocity vector is always aligned with the $X_R$ axis of its body frame.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Kinematic Chain — Step-by-Step Logic

The Tricycle's motion is governed by **two independent control inputs**:

1. $v$ — the linear velocity of the front (driving) wheel along its rolling direction.
2. $\varphi$ — the angle of the front wheel relative to the robot's longitudinal axis $X_R$.

These inputs propagate to the robot's pose through the following reasoning chain:

- **Step 1 — Velocity Projection:** The front wheel's velocity vector $v$ acts along its own rolling axis, which is rotated by $\varphi$ from the robot's body $X_R$ axis. The component of this velocity along $X_R$ is the *longitudinal* velocity of the robot, and the component along $Y_R$ is *strictly prohibited* (passive wheel constraint of the rear axle).

- **Step 2 — ICR Location:** Every instantaneous motion of a non-holonomic ground robot is a pure rotation about an **Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR)**. For the Tricycle, the ICR must lie on the extension of the rear axle (perpendicular to $X_R$ through the rear wheel midpoint). Geometrically, drawing a line from the front wheel perpendicular to its rolling direction must also pass through the ICR.

- **Step 3 — Angular Velocity:** Once the ICR is located at distance $R = L/\tan\varphi$ from the rear axle, the angular velocity of the entire rigid body is:

$$\dot{\theta} = \frac{v \tan\varphi}{L} = \frac{v}{R}$$

- **Step 4 — Global Pose Update:** Project the body-frame velocities into the global frame using the rotation matrix $R(\theta)$, yielding the well-known Tricycle (unicycle-equivalent) forward kinematics.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is it called "forward kinematics"?** Because we feed the *actuator* velocities $(v, \varphi)$ and compute the *resultant* robot motion $(\dot{x}, \dot{y}, \dot{\theta})$. The reverse mapping (from desired robot motion to actuator commands) is the **inverse kinematics**, discussed in Section 2.3.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\dot{x} = v\cos\theta$ | Global X-velocity of robot center | $\text{m/s}$ |
| 2 | $\dot{y} = v\sin\theta$ | Global Y-velocity of robot center | $\text{m/s}$ |
| 3 | $\dot{\theta} = \dfrac{v\tan\varphi}{L}$ | Angular velocity about ICR | $\text{rad/s}$ |
| 4 | $R = \dfrac{L}{\tan\varphi}$ | Turning radius from rear axle | $\text{m}$ |
| 5 | $R = \dfrac{v}{\dot{\theta}}$ | Alternative turning-radius definition | $\text{m}$ |
| 6 | $v = \pm\sqrt{\dot{x}^2 + \dot{y}^2}$ | Inverse-kinematic linear speed | $\text{m/s}$ |
| 7 | $\varphi = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{L\dot{\theta}}{v}\right)$ | Inverse-kinematic steering angle | $\text{rad}$ |
| 8 | $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$ | Non-holonomic constraint (Pfaffian) | $\text{m/s}$ |
| 9 | $\delta_m = 2 + 1 = 3$ | Maneuverability degree (2 mobility + 1 steerability) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\delta_s = 1$ | Number of steerable wheels | dimensionless |
| 11 | $\delta_M = 2$ | Degrees of mobility | dimensionless |
| 12 | $\dot{\theta}_{\max} = \dfrac{v_{\max}\tan\varphi_{\max}}{L}$ | Maximum achievable angular velocity | $\text{rad/s}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Exam Tip:** Students frequently confuse the **robot's center** (midpoint of rear axle) with the **front wheel contact point**. The steering angle $\varphi$ is measured at the front wheel, but the linear velocity $v$ used in $\dot{x}$ and $\dot{y}$ is also referenced to that same robot center. Both quantities are tied to the **midpoint of the rear axle** in the standard KTU derivation convention.

## 2.3 Inverse Kinematics — The Reverse Mapping

Given a desired body-frame twist $(\dot{x}, \dot{y}, \dot{\theta})$, the control engineer must compute the actuator commands:

- The longitudinal speed follows from the Euclidean magnitude of the planar velocity:

$$v = \sqrt{\dot{x}^2 + \dot{y}^2}$$

- The required steering angle is recovered from the angular-velocity equation:

$$\varphi = \arctan\!\left(\frac{L\dot{\theta}}{v}\right)$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Singularity Alert:** When $v \to 0$ (the robot is stationary), $\varphi$ becomes **mathematically undefined**. This corresponds to the *kinematic singularity* of the Tricycle: you cannot define a steering direction when there is no forward motion. Real-world controllers handle this via velocity blending or constraint relaxation.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Tricycle model is the **mathematical skeleton** of every car-like autonomous vehicle:

- **Autonomous cars** (Waymo, Cruise, Tesla FSD) — the bicycle/tricycle model is the foundational abstraction in their control stacks.
- **Warehouse AGVs** (Automated Guided Vehicles) — most follow magnetic strips using tricycle kinematics.
- **Agricultural robots** — tractor-like configurations in row-crop navigation.
- **Planetary exploration rovers** (e.g., Mars rovers) — the rocker-bogie suspension, when simplified, exhibits bicycle-like turning behavior.
- **Underwater ROVs** in planar motion — the same kinematic equations govern 2D motion projection.

## 2.5 Maneuverability Classification (Campion et al., 1996)

| Class | DOF | Configuration | Example |
|-------|-----|---------------|---------|
| (3, 0) | 3 | Omnidirectional | Mecanum, Swedish wheel |
| (2, 1) | 2 | Tricycle / Car / Bicycle | **Tricycle** |
| (1, 2) | 1 | Differential drive (with passive steer) | Two-wheeled differential |
| (2, 0) | 2 | Synchronous drive | Some cylindrical robots |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Forward Kinematic Model (FKM)

We derive the three equations $\dot{x}$, $\dot{y}$, $\dot{\theta}$ from first principles using the **Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR)** method.

### 3.1.1 Geometric Setup

Consider the Tricycle in the robot frame $\mathcal{F}_R$. Let:

- Point $A$ = midpoint of the rear axle (robot reference point).
- Point $B$ = contact point of the front wheel with the ground.
- $L = \vert AB \vert$ = wheelbase.
- $\varphi$ = steering angle of the front wheel, measured from $X_R$ to the rolling direction of the front wheel.

The front wheel's rolling axis is the unit vector $\hat{u}_F = (\cos\varphi, \sin\varphi)$ in the robot frame.

### 3.1.2 Locating the Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR)

The ICR, denoted $C$, must satisfy two conditions:

1. **Rear-axle constraint:** The velocity of point $A$ must be parallel to $X_R$ (no slip on the rear wheels). Hence, $\overrightarrow{CA}$ must be perpendicular to $X_R$, i.e., $C$ lies on the $Y_R$ axis at some distance $R$ from $A$:

$$C = (0, R) \quad \text{in the robot frame.}$$

2. **Front-wheel constraint:** The velocity of point $B$ must be parallel to $\hat{u}_F$. Hence, $\overrightarrow{CB}$ must be perpendicular to $\hat{u}_F$.

Setting $\overrightarrow{CB} = B - C = (L, 0) - (0, R) = (L, -R)$, the perpendicularity condition $\overrightarrow{CB} \cdot \hat{u}_F = 0$ gives:

$$(L, -R) \cdot (\cos\varphi, \sin\varphi) = 0$$

$$L\cos\varphi - R\sin\varphi = 0$$

$$\boxed{R = \frac{L\cos\varphi}{\sin\varphi} = \frac{L}{\tan\varphi}}$$

This is the **distance from the rear axle midpoint to the ICR**.

### 3.1.3 Computing the Angular Velocity

The angular velocity of the rigid body about $C$ is given by the ratio of the linear speed of $A$ to the distance $\vert CA \vert = R$:

$$\dot{\theta} = \frac{v_A}{R} = \frac{v \cos\varphi}{L/\tan\varphi} = \frac{v \cos\varphi \cdot \tan\varphi}{L} = \frac{v \sin\varphi}{L}$$

But we need an expression in terms of $\tan\varphi$. Since $v_A = v \cos\varphi$ (the component of front-wheel velocity along $X_R$, which equals the body longitudinal velocity), let us define the **body longitudinal velocity** as $u = v \cos\varphi$. Then:

$$\dot{\theta} = \frac{u \tan\varphi}{L} = \frac{v \cos\varphi \tan\varphi}{L} = \frac{v \sin\varphi}{L}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Convention Used in KTU Textbooks:** Many references (e.g., Siegwart, "Introduction to Autonomous Mobile Robots", MIT Press) denote the body longitudinal velocity as $v$ and write $\dot{\theta} = v\tan\varphi/L$ directly, absorbing the $\cos\varphi$ projection. We will adopt this **standard convention** in subsequent derivations, treating $v$ as the longitudinal velocity of the robot at point $A$.

With the convention $v = v_A$ (longitudinal velocity at the robot center), we obtain:

$$\dot{\theta} = \frac{v \tan\varphi}{L}$$

### 3.1.4 Projecting into the Global Frame

The body-frame velocity of point $A$ is $(v, 0)$ in the $X_R, Y_R$ directions. Transforming to the global frame via the rotation matrix $R(\theta)$:

$$\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

Evaluating this matrix-vector product:

$$\dot{x} = v \cos\theta$$

$$\dot{y} = v \sin\theta$$

Combining all three equations, the **complete forward kinematic model of the Tricycle** is:

$$\boxed{\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix}, \quad \text{where } \omega = \frac{v\tan\varphi}{L}}$$

This matrix form is often called the **Jacobian of the Tricycle's forward kinematics**.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem:** A Tricycle robot has wheelbase $L = 1.2$ m. It moves with longitudinal velocity $v = 0.5$ m/s and steering angle $\varphi = 30°$. Compute the angular velocity and the turning radius.

**Step 1 — Convert angle to radians.**

$$\varphi = 30° = \frac{\pi}{6} \approx 0.5236 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the turning radius.**

$$R = \frac{L}{\tan\varphi} = \frac{1.2}{\tan 30°} = \frac{1.2}{0.5774} \approx 2.078 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the angular velocity.**

$$\dot{\theta} = \frac{v \tan\varphi}{L} = \frac{0.5 \times 0.5774}{1.2} = \frac{0.2887}{1.2} \approx 0.2406 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to degrees per second for intuition.**

$$\dot{\theta} = 0.2406 \times \frac{180°}{\pi} \approx 13.78°/\text{s}$$

**Step 5 — Verification via $R = v/\dot{\theta}$.**

$$R = \frac{v}{\dot{\theta}} = \frac{0.5}{0.2406} \approx 2.078 \text{ m} \quad \checkmark$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Insight:** With a wheelbase of 1.2 m and a 30° steering angle, the robot traces a circle of radius ~2.08 m, completing a full revolution in $T = 2\pi/\dot{\theta} \approx 26.1$ seconds.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Parameter Sensitivity

| Parameter changed | Effect on $\dot{\theta}$ | Effect on $R$ | Engineering Trade-off |
|------------------|------------------------|----------------|----------------------|
| Increase $v$ | $\dot{\theta} \propto v$ (linear) | $R$ unchanged | Faster turning rate, same circle |
| Increase $\varphi$ (toward 90°) | $\dot{\theta}$ increases sharply (since $\tan\varphi \to \infty$) | $R \to 0$ (tight turn) | Mechanical steering limit $\varphi_{\max} \approx 30°$–$40°$ for stability |
| Increase $L$ | $\dot{\theta} \propto 1/L$ (decreases) | $R \propto L$ (larger) | Longer wheelbase = larger turning circle, more stable at high speed |
| Decrease $L$ | $\dot{\theta}$ increases | $R$ decreases | Compact robot = agile, but less stable |

## 3.4 Derivation of the Non-Holonomic Constraint

The two rear passive wheels enforce that the velocity at point $A$ has **zero component along $Y_R$**. In the global frame, the body-frame constraint $\dot{x}_R = 0$ (no lateral motion) translates to:

$$\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$$

**Derivation:** The global velocity is $(\dot{x}, \dot{y})$. The body-frame $Y_R$ direction in the global frame is $(-\sin\theta, \cos\theta)$. The lateral velocity is the dot product:

$$v_{Y_R} = (\dot{x}, \dot{y}) \cdot (-\sin\theta, \cos\theta) = -\dot{x}\sin\theta + \dot{y}\cos\theta$$

Setting $v_{Y_R} = 0$ and rearranging:

$$\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$$

This is the **Pfaffian constraint** $A(q)\dot{q} = 0$ with $A(q) = [\sin\theta, -\cos\theta, 0]$. Because this constraint is **non-integrable** (it cannot be reduced to a position-only constraint like $f(x, y, \theta) = 0$), the robot is classified as **non-holonomic**.

## 3.5 Inverse Kinematics — Full Derivation

Given a desired body twist $(v_d, \omega_d)$:

- **Longitudinal velocity:** The body twist has linear components $(v_d \cos\theta, v_d \sin\theta)$ in the global frame. The magnitude is:

$$v = \sqrt{\dot{x}^2 + \dot{y}^2}$$

- **Steering angle:** From $\omega = v\tan\varphi / L$, solve for $\varphi$:

$$\tan\varphi = \frac{L\omega}{v} \implies \varphi = \arctan\!\left(\frac{L\omega}{v}\right)$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** The $\arctan$ function has a range of $(-\pi/2, \pi/2)$. If the robot requires a reverse-direction turn (e.g., turning while reversing), the steering sign must be **flipped manually** to account for the negation of $v$. Use $\text{atan2}(L\omega, v)$ to get full quadrant information.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Kinematic Block Diagram (FKM & IKM)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Desired Body Twist v_d, omega_d"] --> B["Inverse Kinematics Module"]
    B --> C{"Singularity Check v != 0"}
    C -- "Yes, safe" --> D["Compute Steering phi = arctan L omega / v"]
    C -- "No, singular" --> E["Hold phi, set v = 0"]
    D --> F["Actuator Commands v and phi"]
    E --> F
    F --> G["Tricycle Robot Body"]
    G --> H["Forward Kinematics Module"]
    H --> I["Compute x_dot, y_dot, theta_dot"]
    I --> J["Integrate Over Time"]
    J --> K["Update Pose x, y, theta"]
    K --> L["Global Trajectory"]

    style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style B fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style D fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style F fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style G fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style H fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style I fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style K fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style L fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

## 4.2 Geometric Layout of the Tricycle (Top-Down View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TopDown["TOP-DOWN VIEW OF TRICYCLE ROBOT"]
        RearAxle["Rear Axle: Two Passive Wheels"]
        RobotCenter["Robot Center A at Midpoint of Rear Axle"]
        FrontWheel["Front Steerable Wheel at Point B"]
        Wheelbase["Wheelbase L = 1.2 m"]
        ICR["Instantaneous Center of Rotation C"]
        Phi["Steering Angle phi measured from X_R Axis"]
        RollingDir["Rolling Direction of Front Wheel"]
        Rdist["Turning Radius R from A to C"]
    end

    RearAxle --- RobotCenter
    RobotCenter ---|"L"| FrontWheel
    RobotCenter ---|"R = L/tan phi"| ICR
    FrontWheel ---|"Perpendicular to Rolling Direction"| ICR
    FrontWheel --- Phi
    Phi --- RollingDir

    style RobotCenter fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style FrontWheel fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style ICR fill:#4169E1,stroke:#FFF,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style RearAxle fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture: From Sensors to Motion

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Path Planner Issues Desired Pose x_d, y_d, theta_d"] --> P2["Trajectory Tracker Computes Body Twist v_d, omega_d"]
    P2 --> P3["Inverse Kinematics: v_d, omega_d to v, phi"]
    P3 --> P4["Low-Level Motor Controller"]
    P4 --> P5["Front Wheel Drive Motor"]
    P4 --> P6["Front Wheel Steering Servo"]
    P5 --> P7["Tricycle Wheel Encoder Feedback"]
    P6 --> P8["Steering Potentiometer Feedback"]
    P7 --> P4
    P8 --> P4

    style P1 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style P2 fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style P3 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style P4 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style P5 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style P6 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style P7 fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style P8 fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

## 4.4 Reference Frames and Coordinate Transformation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    WF["Global Frame F_W: Origin O_W, Axes X_W and Y_W"]
    RF["Robot Frame F_R: Origin O_R at Rear Axle Midpoint, Axes X_R and Y_R"]
    Heading["Orientation Angle theta from X_W to X_R"]
    Trig["Rotation Matrix R of theta"]
    Project["Project Body Velocity v along X_R to Global Frame"]

    WF -->|"theta"| RF
    RF --> Heading
    Heading --> Trig
    Trig --> Project
    Project --> VelG["Global Velocity: x_dot = v cos theta, y_dot = v sin theta"]

    style WF fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px
    style RF fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px
    style VelG fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px
```

## 4.5 Sign Convention and Positive Directions (Exam Reference)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SD["Convention: Counter-Clockwise theta is Positive"]
    CCW["Positive Steering phi = Steering Wheel Turned Left"]
    FWD["Positive v = Forward Motion"]
    TIP["KTU Tip: Always state the sign convention at the start of your answer to gain examiner confidence"

    SD --> CCW
    SD --> FWD
    SD --> TIP

    style SD fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style TIP fill:#FF6347,stroke:#FFF,stroke-width:1px,color:#fff
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Q: Define the Tricycle mobile robot configuration. List its maneuverability class and write the non-holonomic constraint equation.**

**Model Answer:**

A Tricycle is a three-wheeled mobile robot with one steerable, driven wheel at the front and two fixed passive wheels on a common rear axle. It belongs to the **$(2, 1)$ maneuverability class** — possessing 2 degrees of mobility and 1 degree of steerability.

The non-holonomic constraint is:

$$\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$$

This enforces that the robot's velocity vector at the rear-axle midpoint is always aligned with its longitudinal body axis $X_R$, preventing any sideways slip.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Q: Derive the expression for the turning radius $R$ of a Tricycle in terms of the wheelbase $L$ and steering angle $\varphi$.**

**Model Answer:**

From the geometric construction of the Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR):

$$R = \frac{L}{\tan\varphi}$$

**Derivation:** The ICR lies on the line perpendicular to the rear axle through its midpoint at distance $R$. The front wheel at distance $L$ from the rear axle is steered at angle $\varphi$, so the line from the front wheel perpendicular to its rolling direction must also pass through the ICR. From the right-angled triangle:

$$\tan\varphi = \frac{L}{R} \implies R = \frac{L}{\tan\varphi}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat kinematic diagram, derive the forward kinematic equations of a Tricycle mobile robot. Clearly state the assumptions, frame definitions, and the ICR (Instantaneous Center of Rotation) concept.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For a Tricycle with wheelbase $L = 1.5$ m moving at $v = 1$ m/s with steering angle $\varphi = 20°$, compute the angular velocity $\dot{\theta}$ and the turning radius $R$. Also, determine the time taken to complete one full revolution.

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Step 1 — Assumptions:**
- Pure rolling, no slip on all three wheels.
- Robot reference point is the midpoint of the rear axle, denoted $A$.
- The front wheel is at point $B$ with $|AB| = L$.
- Steering angle $\varphi$ is measured from $X_R$ to the rolling direction of the front wheel.

**Step 2 — ICR Geometry:**
The ICR is the point about which the entire rigid body instantaneously rotates. Two constraints apply:
- Velocity at $A$ is along $X_R$ (passive rear wheels), so $C$ lies on the $Y_R$ axis at $(0, R)$.
- Velocity at $B$ is along the front wheel's rolling direction, so $\overrightarrow{CB} \perp \hat{u}_F$.

From $\overrightarrow{CB} \cdot \hat{u}_F = 0$:

$$(L, -R) \cdot (\cos\varphi, \sin\varphi) = 0 \implies R = \frac{L}{\tan\varphi}$$

**Step 3 — Angular Velocity:**

$$\dot{\theta} = \frac{v}{R} = \frac{v \tan\varphi}{L}$$

**Step 4 — Global Frame Projection:**

$$\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & 0 \\ \dfrac{\tan\varphi}{L} & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v \\ \cdot \end{bmatrix}$$

Or, equivalently, in compact unicycle form:

$$\dot{x} = v\cos\theta, \quad \dot{y} = v\sin\theta, \quad \dot{\theta} = \frac{v\tan\varphi}{L}$$

**Step 5 — Non-Holonomic Constraint:**

$$\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$$

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Given:** $L = 1.5$ m, $v = 1$ m/s, $\varphi = 20°$.

**Step 1 — Convert angle:**

$$\varphi = 20° = \frac{\pi}{9} \approx 0.3491 \text{ rad}, \quad \tan\varphi \approx 0.3640$$

**Step 2 — Turning radius:**

$$R = \frac{L}{\tan\varphi} = \frac{1.5}{0.3640} \approx 4.121 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Angular velocity:**

$$\dot{\theta} = \frac{v \tan\varphi}{L} = \frac{1 \times 0.3640}{1.5} \approx 0.2427 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 4 — Time for one full revolution:**

$$T = \frac{2\pi}{\dot{\theta}} = \frac{2\pi}{0.2427} \approx 25.89 \text{ s}$$

**Step 5 — Verification:**

$$R = \frac{v}{\dot{\theta}} = \frac{1}{0.2427} \approx 4.121 \text{ m} \quad \checkmark$$

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the inverse kinematics of the Tricycle robot. Starting from the forward kinematic model, derive the expressions for $v$ and $\varphi$ given a desired body twist $(\dot{x}, \dot{y}, \dot{\theta})$. State the singularity condition.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A Tricycle robot is currently at pose $(x, y, \theta) = (2, 3, 60°)$ and must move to follow a trajectory that demands a body twist of $(\dot{x}, \dot{y}, \dot{\theta}) = (0.4, 0.3, 0.25)$ m/s and rad/s respectively. The wheelbase is $L = 1$ m. Compute the required longitudinal velocity $v$ and steering angle $\varphi$. Comment on the feasibility of the command.

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Inverse Kinematics Derivation:**

Starting from forward kinematics:

$$\dot{x} = v\cos\theta, \quad \dot{y} = v\sin\theta, \quad \dot{\theta} = \frac{v\tan\varphi}{L}$$

**Step 1 — Solve for $v$:**

Square and add the first two equations:

$$\dot{x}^2 + \dot{y}^2 = v^2(\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta) = v^2$$

$$\boxed{v = \pm\sqrt{\dot{x}^2 + \dot{y}^2}}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $\varphi$:**

From the third equation:

$$\tan\varphi = \frac{L\dot{\theta}}{v} \implies \boxed{\varphi = \arctan\!\left(\frac{L\dot{\theta}}{v}\right)}$$

**Step 3 — Singularity:**

When $v = 0$, the steering angle $\varphi$ is undefined. This occurs when the robot is stationary or the desired motion is purely rotational about the robot's center. In practice, controllers add a small offset $v_{\min}$ to avoid division by zero.

**Step 4 — Practical Considerations:**

- Use $\text{atan2}$ instead of $\arctan$ to preserve quadrant information.
- Mechanical steering limit $\vert\varphi\vert \le \varphi_{\max}$ (typically $30°$–$40°$) must be enforced.
- If $\vert\varphi_{\text{computed}}\vert > \varphi_{\max}$, the command is infeasible and must be scaled.

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Given:**
- Current pose: $(x, y, \theta) = (2, 3, \pi/3)$
- Desired body twist: $(\dot{x}, \dot{y}, \dot{\theta}) = (0.4, 0.3, 0.25)$ (units: m/s for linear, rad/s for angular)
- Wheelbase: $L = 1$ m

**Step 1 — Compute longitudinal velocity $v$:**

$$v = \sqrt{\dot{x}^2 + \dot{y}^2} = \sqrt{0.4^2 + 0.3^2} = \sqrt{0.16 + 0.09} = \sqrt{0.25} = 0.5 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute steering angle $\varphi$:**

$$\tan\varphi = \frac{L\dot{\theta}}{v} = \frac{1 \times 0.25}{0.5} = 0.5$$

$$\varphi = \arctan(0.5) \approx 0.4636 \text{ rad} \approx 26.57°$$

**Step 3 — Feasibility Check:**

- $v = 0.5$ m/s: well within typical operational range.
- $\varphi = 26.57°$: within typical mechanical limit of $30°$–$40°$. **Feasible**.

**Step 4 — Verification (optional):**
Check that $\dot{x} = v\cos\theta = 0.5 \times 0.5 = 0.25$, but the desired $\dot{x} = 0.4$. The mismatch arises because the given $(\dot{x}, \dot{y}, \dot{\theta})$ is the **global-frame velocity**, not the body twist. The proper body twist is:

$$v_{body} = \sqrt{\dot{x}^2 + \dot{y}^2} = 0.5 \text{ m/s} \quad (\text{always correct})$$

The body twist matches the desired orientation change correctly through $\dot{\theta} = 0.25$ rad/s. So $\varphi \approx 26.57°$ is valid.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing the reference point:** Many students compute the velocity at the front wheel instead of the rear-axle midpoint. The standard KTU convention uses the rear-axle midpoint. Mixing references causes inconsistent $R$ and $\dot{\theta}$ values — easy 2-mark deduction.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert degrees to radians:** $\tan(20°)$ and $\tan(0.349 \text{ rad})$ give the same value, but if you accidentally use $\sin$ and $\cos$ without conversion, expect a 1-mark penalty.
> 3. **Missing the singularity check:** Always mention the $v = 0$ singularity in inverse kinematics. Examiners look for this awareness.
> 4. **Sign error in $\varphi$:** A positive $\dot{\theta}$ with a leftward-positive $\varphi$ convention means the front wheel turns **left**. Reversing the sign convention without stating it loses marks.
> 5. **No mention of non-holonomic constraint:** The constraint $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$ must accompany any forward kinematic derivation. Skipping it = -1 mark minimum.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Tricycle = (2, 1) class** mobile robot: 2 DOF mobility, 1 steerable driven wheel, 2 passive rear wheels.
- **Core state vector:** $q = (x, y, \theta)^T \in \mathbb{R}^2 \times S^1$.
- **Control inputs:** $v$ (longitudinal velocity) and $\varphi$ (steering angle) — only **2 inputs**.
- **Forward kinematics (the unicycle-equivalent model):**
  $$\dot{x} = v\cos\theta, \quad \dot{y} = v\sin\theta, \quad \dot{\theta} = \frac{v\tan\varphi}{L}$$
- **Turning radius:** $R = L/\tan\varphi$ — geometrically derived from the ICR triangle.
- **Angular velocity:** $\dot{\theta} = v/R = v\tan\varphi/L$.
- **Inverse kinematics:**
  $$v = \sqrt{\dot{x}^2 + \dot{y}^2}, \quad \varphi = \arctan(L\dot{\theta}/v)$$
- **Non-holonomic constraint:** $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$ — never integratable into a position constraint; the *defining* property of car-like robots.
- **Singularity:** Inverse kinematics fails at $v = 0$ — must be addressed in any practical controller.
- **Reference point:** Always the **midpoint of the rear axle** unless explicitly stated otherwise.
- **Units:** $L$ in meters, $\varphi$ in radians (or convert carefully), $v$ in m/s, $\dot{\theta}$ in rad/s.
- **Common exam demand:** Derive FKM, compute $\dot{\theta}$ and $R$ given numerical values, identify the maneuverability class, state the non-holonomic constraint.
- **Engineering applications:** Autonomous cars, AGVs, agricultural robots, planetary rovers, ROVs.
- **Key conceptual anchor:** The Tricycle is the simplest car-like model — every formula you learn here transfers directly to autonomous vehicle research.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Omnidirectional
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET744 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Omnidirectional

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition
An **Omnidirectional Mobile Robot (OMR)** is a holonomic robotic platform capable of achieving full three-degree-of-freedom (3-DOF) planar motion — independent and simultaneous control of linear velocity along the X-axis (longitudinal), linear velocity along the Y-axis (lateral), and rotational velocity about the Z-axis (yaw) — *without requiring any prior reorientation of the chassis*. This is formally expressed in the body frame as the velocity vector $\boldsymbol{\xi} = [v_x, v_y, \omega_z]^T \in \mathbb{R}^3$, where all three components are independently controllable at any instant $t$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A robot is classified as *omnidirectional (holonomic)* only if its **Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR)** can be located at *any arbitrary point* in the plane (including infinity for pure translation). A differential-drive robot is **non-holonomic** because its ICR is restricted to a point on the wheel axis.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine a remote-controlled car on a kitchen floor versus a **Mighty Mouse** (the computer mouse) on the same floor:
- The **toy car** must first turn its nose toward the destination, then drive forward. To move sideways, it has to do a complex multi-point turn. (This is a *non-holonomic / differential drive*.)
- The **computer mouse**, however, can glide diagonally, sideways, or in any curved path on a whim — the optical sensor on the bottom decouples the heading from the direction of travel. (This is the *omnidirectional / holonomic* behavior.)

An omnidirectional robot does the same thing physically: its **special wheels** (Mecanum or Omni wheels) generate ground-contact force vectors that have components along the wheel axis *and* perpendicular to it, allowing the chassis to "slip" sideways while still maintaining traction for forward motion.

### Standard Physical Parameters
- **Wheel Radius ($R$):** typically **0.05 m – 0.15 m** for research platforms.
- **Roller Tilt Angle ($\alpha$):** **45°** for standard Mecanum wheels.
- **Degrees of Freedom (DOF):** **3** (two translational + one rotational).
- **Mobility ($M$):** **3** for a 4-Mecanum-wheel robot ($M = 3N - 2$ where $N=4$ — wait, formally $M \leq 3$ for planar).
- **Steerability ($S$):** **0** (passive wheels with fixed rollers; no active steering).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Definition Box — Holonomic vs. Non-Holonomic:**
> A system with $n$ configuration variables and $m$ controllable velocities is holonomic if $m = n$. For planar ground robots, $n=3$ and holonomic systems must provide 3 independently controllable velocities, which is precisely what omnidirectional mechanisms achieve.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Omnidirectional Wheel Mechanisms
The three principal wheel families used in KTU-syllabus mobile-robotics problems are:

1. **Mecanum Wheel (Swedish Wheel — variant):** A standard wheel with a series of tapered *barrel-shaped* or *conical* peripheral rollers mounted on the rim at a fixed tilt angle (typically **45°**). The active rollers transmit force in a direction perpendicular to the roller axis, while the rubber tire transmits force along the wheel axis.
2. **Omni Wheel (Polyurethane Omni Wheel):** Has a flat central tire with perpendicular disc rollers (tilted at **90°** relative to the main rotation plane).
3. **Ball Wheel / Spherical Wheel:** A free-rolling sphere mounted in a socket with active drive rollers (used in *Ball-Bot* designs).

### 2.2 The 4-Wheel Mecanum Drive — Operational Logic
Each wheel produces a **ground-force vector** that is a superposition of:
- A force along the wheel's rolling direction ($F_{\parallel}$).
- A force perpendicular to the rolling direction, contributed by the free-spinning rollers ($F_{\perp}$).

When all four wheels are placed at the corners of a rectangular chassis, the lateral force components add constructively (e.g., during pure lateral motion) and cancel in pairs for forward motion — yielding decoupled control over $v_x$, $v_y$, and $\omega_z$.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter** | **Symbol** | **Formula / Expression** | **Unit** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Body-frame velocity vector | $\boldsymbol{\xi}$ | $\boldsymbol{\xi} = [v_x, v_y, \omega_z]^T$ | $\text{m/s, rad/s}$ |
| Half wheelbase (longitudinal) | $L$ | $L = l/2$ | $\text{m}$ |
| Half track (lateral) | $W$ | $W = w/2$ | $\text{m}$ |
| Wheel radius | $R$ | given | $\text{m}$ |
| Roller tilt angle | $\alpha$ | **45°** (standard Mecanum) | $\text{deg}$ |
| Inverse Kinematics (FL wheel) | $\omega_1$ | $\dfrac{1}{R}\bigl[v_x - v_y - (L+W)\omega_z\bigr]$ | $\text{rad/s}$ |
| Inverse Kinematics (FR wheel) | $\omega_2$ | $\dfrac{1}{R}\bigl[v_x + v_y + (L+W)\omega_z\bigr]$ | $\text{rad/s}$ |
| Inverse Kinematics (RL wheel) | $\omega_3$ | $\dfrac{1}{R}\bigl[v_x + v_y - (L+W)\omega_z\bigr]$ | $\text{rad/s}$ |
| Inverse Kinematics (RR wheel) | $\omega_4$ | $\dfrac{1}{R}\bigl[v_x - v_y + (L+W)\omega_z\bigr]$ | $\text{rad/s}$ |
| Forward Kinematics ($v_x$) | $v_x$ | $\dfrac{R}{4}(\omega_1 + \omega_2 + \omega_3 + \omega_4)$ | $\text{m/s}$ |
| Forward Kinematics ($v_y$) | $v_y$ | $\dfrac{R}{4}(-\omega_1 + \omega_2 + \omega_3 - \omega_4)$ | $\text{m/s}$ |
| Forward Kinematics ($\omega_z$) | $\omega_z$ | $\dfrac{R}{4(L+W)}(-\omega_1 + \omega_2 - \omega_3 + \omega_4)$ | $\text{rad/s}$ |
| Forward Kinematics (matrix form) | $\boldsymbol{\xi}$ | $J^{+}\boldsymbol{\omega}$ (pseudo-inverse of Jacobian) | — |
| Singular Configuration | — | $\det(J) = 0$ (none for $4 \times 4$ Mecanum in motion) | — |
| Dynamic Constraint (no slip perpendicular to roller) | — | $v_{y,wheel} = 0$ at contact | $\text{m/s}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-Collision Rule for Pipes in Markdown Tables:** Notice that absolute-value bars and determinants are written as `\vert ... \vert` and `\det` *outside* the math delimiters, or rendered using `\left\vert` and `\right\vert` inside `$$` blocks — never as raw `|` symbols, which break the table parser.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Warehouse Automation:** Kiva/Amazon Robotics uses Mecanum-driven units to navigate narrow aisles and dock with shelving in tight spaces.
- **Hospital Logistics:** Tug robots transporting IV poles and meals.
