In our design, the chassis CM is at its geometric center, so $\mathbf{r} = \mathbf{0}$ and no shift is needed. **Wheels and caster have their origins at their geometric centers too** (Cylinder and Sphere primitives are centered at the link origin by default).

## 3.6 Joint Origin Computation — Aligning Wheel Axis

The cylinder primitive has its symmetry axis along the local **Z**. To make the wheel spin about the robot's **Y-axis** (lateral), we rotate the wheel by $-\pi/2$ (or $+\pi/2$, depending on the wheel side) about the X-axis.

**Mathematical Interpretation:** The rpy in the joint origin is read as **roll, pitch, yaw**, applied in Z-Y-X order. Setting `rpy = "-1.5708 0 0"` produces:

$$R_{rpy} = R_z(0)\, R_y(0)\, R_x(-1.5708) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & \cos(-\tfrac{\pi}{2}) & -\sin(-\tfrac{\pi}{2}) \\ 0 & \sin(-\tfrac{\pi}{2}) & \cos(-\tfrac{\pi}{2}) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \\ 0 & -1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

This maps the wheel's local Z to the robot's Y, and the wheel's local Y to the robot's negative Z. Now the joint axis `xyz="0 0 1"` (wheel's local Z) correctly represents rotation about the robot's Y.

## 3.7 Final URDF — Complete XML for a Movable Differential-Drive Robot

```xml
<?xml version="1.0" ?>
<robot name="diff_drive_mobile_robot">

  <!-- ============================================== -->
  <!-- BASE LINK (chassis)                            -->
  <!-- ============================================== -->
  <link name="base_link">
    <visual>
      <origin xyz="0 0 0" rpy="0 0 0"/>
      <geometry>
        <box size="0.5 0.3 0.15"/>
      </geometry>
      <material name="chassis_blue">
        <color rgba="0.1 0.3 0.8 1.0"/>
      </material>
    </visual>
    <collision>
      <origin xyz="0 0 0" rpy="0 0 0"/>
      <geometry>
        <box size="0.5 0.3 0.15"/>
      </geometry>
    </collision>
    <inertial>
      <origin xyz="0 0 0" rpy="0 0 0"/>
      <mass value="5.0"/>
      <inertia ixx="0.046875"  ixy="0.0"  ixz="0.0"
                              iyy="0.113542" iyz="0.0"
                                                   izz="0.141667"/>
    </inertial>
  </link>

  <!-- ============================================== -->
  <!-- LEFT DRIVEN WHEEL                              -->
  <!-- ============================================== -->
  <link name="left_wheel">
    <visual>
      <origin xyz="0 0 0" rpy="0 0 0"/>
      <geometry>
        <cylinder radius="0.1" length="0.05"/>
      </geometry>
      <material name="wheel_black">
        <color rgba="0.05 0.05 0.05 1.0"/>
      </material>
    </visual>
    <collision>
      <origin xyz="0 0 0" rpy="0 0 0"/>
      <geometry>
        <cylinder radius="0.1" length="0.05"/>
      </geometry>
    </collision>
    <inertial>
      <origin xyz="0 0 0" rpy="0 0 0"/>
      <mass value="0.5"/>
      <inertia ixx="0.001354"  ixy="0.0"  ixz="0.0"
                              iyy="0.001354" iyz="0.0"
                                                   izz="0.0025"/>
    </inertial>
  </link>

  <joint name="left_wheel_joint" type="continuous">
    <parent link="base_link"/>
    <child  link="left_wheel"/>
    <origin xyz="0  0.175 0" rpy="-1.5708 0 0"/>
    <axis   xyz="0 0 1"/>
  </joint>

  <!-- ============================================== -->
  <!-- RIGHT DRIVEN WHEEL                             -->
  <!-- ============================================== -->
  <link name="right_wheel">
    <visual>
      <origin xyz="0 0 0" rpy="0 0 0"/>
      <geometry>
        <cylinder radius="0.1" length="0.05"/>
      </geometry>
      <material name="wheel_black">
        <color rgba="0.05 0.05 0.05 1.0"/>
      </material>
    </visual>
    <collision>
      <origin xyz="0 0 0" rpy="0 0 0"/>
      <geometry>
        <cylinder radius="0.1" length="0.05"/>
      </geometry>
    </collision>
    <inertial>
      <origin xyz="0 0 0" rpy="0 0 0"/>
      <mass value="0.5"/>
      <inertia ixx="0.001354"  ixy="0.0"  ixz="0.0"
                              iyy="0.001354" iyz="0.0"
                                                   izz="0.0025"/>
    </inertial>
  </link>

  <joint name="right_wheel_joint" type="continuous">
    <parent link="base_link"/>
    <child  link="right_wheel"/>
    <origin xyz="0 -0.175 0" rpy=" 1.5708 0 0"/>
    <axis   xyz="0 0 1"/>
  </joint>

  <!-- ============================================== -->
  <!-- CASTER WHEEL                                   -->
  <!-- ============================================== -->
  <link name="caster_wheel">
    <visual>
      <origin xyz="0 0 0" rpy="0 0 0"/>
      <geometry>
        <sphere radius="0.05"/>
      </geometry>
      <material name="caster_grey">
        <color rgba="0.5 0.5 0.5 1.0"/>
      </material>
    </visual>
    <collision>
      <origin xyz="0 0 0" rpy="0 0 0"/>
      <geometry>
        <sphere radius="0.05"/>
      </geometry>
    </collision>
    <inertial>
      <origin xyz="0 0 0" rpy="0 0 0"/>
      <mass value="0.2"/>
      <inertia ixx="0.0002" ixy="0.0" ixz="0.0"
                              iyy="0.0002" iyz="0.0"
                                                 izz="0.0002"/>
    </inertial>
  </link>

  <joint name="caster_joint" type="continuous">
    <parent link="base_link"/>
    <child  link="caster_wheel"/>
    <origin xyz="0.20 0 -0.075" rpy="0 0 0"/>
    <axis   xyz="0 0 1"/>
  </joint>

</robot>
```

## 3.8 Analytical Validation Matrix

| Property | Expected | Validated? |
|---|---|---|
| URDF is a tree (single root) | `base_link` is root, 3 children, no loops | ✓ |
| Robot is movable | Three `continuous` joints exist | ✓ |
| Inertia tensor is symmetric | `ixy = ixz = iyz = 0` for all links | ✓ |
| Mass distribution realistic | $m_{base} = 5\,\text{kg} \gg m_{wheels}+m_{caster}$ | ✓ |
| Wheel orientation correct | `rpy = ±π/2` aligns cylinder Z to robot Y | ✓ |
| Wheelbase correct | L = 0.175 + 0.175 = **0.35 m** ✓ | ✓ |
| Caster placed below chassis | z = −0.075 m (half of 0.15) | ✓ |
| Wheel spin axis | `<axis xyz="0 0 1"/>` in child frame | ✓ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 URDF XML Structure — Hierarchical Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["robot name=diff_drive_mobile_robot"] --> B["link: base_link"]
    A --> C["joint: left_wheel_joint type=continuous"]
    A --> D["joint: right_wheel_joint type=continuous"]
    A --> E["joint: caster_joint type=continuous"]

    B --> B1["visual: box 0.5x0.3x0.15"]
    B --> B2["collision: box 0.5x0.3x0.15"]
    B --> B3["inertial: mass=5.0, I tensor"]

    C --> F["link: left_wheel"]
    D --> G["link: right_wheel"]
    E --> H["link: caster_wheel"]

    F --> F1["visual: cylinder r=0.1 h=0.05"]
    F --> F2["collision: cylinder r=0.1 h=0.05"]
    F --> F3["inertial: mass=0.5, I tensor"]

    G --> G1["visual: cylinder r=0.1 h=0.05"]
    G --> G2["collision: cylinder r=0.1 h=0.05"]
    G --> G3["inertial: mass=0.5, I tensor"]

    H --> H1["visual: sphere r=0.05"]
    H --> H2["collision: sphere r=0.05"]
    H --> H3["inertial: mass=0.2, I tensor"]
```

## 4.2 TF (Transform) Tree — Robot's Coordinate Graph

The TF tree is the runtime representation of the URDF. Each node is a link's coordinate frame, each edge is a joint. RViz and `tf2` use this tree to answer "where is link X in link Y's frame?".

```mermaid
graph TD
    O["world"] --> R["base_link"]
    R -- "left_wheel_joint continuous" --> LW["left_wheel"]
    R -- "right_wheel_joint continuous" --> RW["right_wheel"]
    R -- "caster_joint continuous" --> CW["caster_wheel"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:**
> - The root `base_link` has **no parent transform**; it inherits its pose from the world's `map` → `odom` → `base_link` chain.
> - `left_wheel` spins continuously about its local Z (post-rotation, robot's Y-axis).
> - `caster_wheel` spins passively — it has no actuator but the joint type is `continuous` so it can rotate freely when the robot moves.

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Mobile Robot in ROS 2

```mermaid
graph LR
    URDF["URDF File XML"] --> PARSE["Robot State Publisher"]
    PARSE --> TF["tf2 Static and Dynamic Transforms"]
    TF --> RVIZ["RViz Visualization"]
    TF --> GAZ["Gazebo Simulator"]
    URDF --> GAZ
    URDF --> CTRL["ros2_control Hardware Interface"]
    CTRL --> MC["Motor Controller"]
    MC --> WHL["Wheel Joints"]
    WHL --> JOINT["Joint State Publisher"]
    JOINT --> TF
    GAZ --> SENS["Sensors LIDAR IMU Camera"]
    SENS --> NAV["Navigation 2 Stack"]
    NAV --> CTRL
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The URDF is the *single source of truth* parsed by all major ROS 2 components.
- `robot_state_publisher` reads joint states and broadcasts TF transforms.
- Gazebo reads the URDF (plus `<gazebo>` extensions) for physics simulation.
- `ros2_control` reads the `<transmission>` blocks to wire motors to joint command interfaces.
- Closed loop: sensor feedback informs the navigation stack, which commands wheel velocities, which update joint states.

## 4.4 Joint State Update Loop — Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Sim as Gazebo / Hardware
    participant JSB as Joint State Broadcaster
    participant RSP as Robot State Publisher
    participant TF2 as tf2 System
    participant APP as Navigation / RViz
    Sim->>JSB: position, velocity, effort of every joint
    JSB->>RSP: /joint_states message
    RSP->>TF2: compute T_base_to_child for each joint
    TF2->>APP: serve transform lookups and chain
    APP->>Sim: cmd_vel / joint trajectory commands
```

## 4.5 Wheel Frame Orientation — Visual Schematic

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["Parent frame: base_link, X forward, Y left, Z up"] -->|"xyz=0 0.175 0, rpy=-pi/2 0 0"| B["Child frame: left_wheel, Z now aligned with Y of base"]
    B -->|"axis xyz=0 0 1"| C["Wheel spins about its local Z, which is robot's Y, allowing forward rolling"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define URDF. List its key elements used to build a movable robot model. `[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PEAET744]`

**Model Answer (≈3 marks):**

**URDF (Unified Robot Description Format)** is an XML-based file format that describes a robot's physical structure, kinematics, and dynamics for use in simulation and control frameworks such as ROS 2 and Gazebo.

**Key elements of a movable URDF model:**
1. **`<robot>`** — root element naming the robot.
2. **`<link>`** — defines each rigid body, containing `<visual>`, `<collision>`, and `<inertial>` sub-blocks.
3. **`<joint>`** — connects two links and defines the type of relative motion. For a *movable* robot, the joint must be of type `continuous` (wheels), `revolute`, or `prismatic`.
4. **`<material>`** (optional) — reusable color/texture definitions.
5. **`<transmission>`** and **`<gazebo>`** (optional) — bind actuators and simulator-specific plugins.

---

### Q2. Differentiate between `revolute`, `continuous`, and `prismatic` joint types in URDF. Give one mobile-robot example for each. `[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PEAET744]`

**Model Answer (≈3 marks):**

| Joint Type | Motion | Limits | Mobile Robot Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| `revolute` | Rotation about a fixed axis | Has bounded range $[\theta_{min}, \theta_{max}]$ | Steering link of an Ackermann-steered car |
| `continuous` | Rotation about a fixed axis, **unbounded** | None (infinite rotation) | **Driven wheels** of a differential-drive robot, caster wheel |
| `prismatic` | Linear translation along an axis | Has bounded displacement range $[d_{min}, d_{max}]$ | Linear actuator of a 4-bar suspension, sliding sensor mount |

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Missing `<inertial>` block:** Gazebo will spawn the robot but the controller will refuse to apply torques. Examiners *will* deduct 2 marks.
> - **Wrong `rpy` on wheel joints:** If you omit the `±π/2` rotation, the wheel will appear *sideways* in RViz and the robot will behave as if glued to the ground. Always remember: cylinder geometry is **Z-aligned by default**.
> - **Cylinder vs. Capsule:** Use `<cylinder>` for thin wheels; `<capsule>` is for thick rounded ends. Mixing them loses 1 mark.
> - **Sign of `rpy`:** Left and right wheels need *opposite signs* in rpy (e.g., `-1.5708` and `+1.5708`) so that both wheels spin in the same sense for forward motion.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper, PEAET744]`

**(a)** Explain the structure of a URDF file with a neat block diagram. Describe the role of `<link>`, `<joint>`, `<visual>`, `<collision>`, and `<inertial>` elements in building a movable robot model. (7 marks)

**(b)** Design the complete URDF for a differential-drive mobile robot with the following specification: rectangular base of dimensions $0.4 \times 0.3 \times 0.1$ m, mass $4$ kg; two driven wheels (cylinders, radius $0.08$ m, thickness $0.04$ m, mass $0.4$ kg each); one front caster (sphere, radius $0.04$ m, mass $0.15$ kg). Wheelbase = $0.3$ m. Provide the full XML and compute the inertia tensor for the chassis. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**(a) URDF Structure Explanation (7 marks):**

1. **Root Element `<robot>`:** Every URDF begins with `<robot name="...">`. This is the parent of all kinematic and dynamic declarations. (1 mark)

2. **`<link>` — Rigid Bodies:** A link represents a single non-deformable part (chassis, wheel, sensor housing). Each link has its own local coordinate frame. (1 mark)

3. **`<joint>` — Kinematic Constraints:** Joints connect a parent and child link. The `type` attribute defines the motion: `fixed`, `revolute`, `continuous`, `prismatic`, `floating`, or `planar`. For a movable robot, the joints between the base and wheels are typically `continuous`. (1 mark)

4. **`<visual>` — Graphics:** Defines how the link appears in RViz/Gazebo. Contains `<geometry>` (box, cylinder, sphere, mesh) and `<material>` (color or texture). (1 mark)

5. **`<collision>` — Physics Proxy:** Defines a simplified geometry used for contact detection. May be coarser than the visual to reduce computational cost. (1 mark)

6. **`<inertial>` — Dynamics:** Contains `<mass>` and the inertia tensor in `<inertia>` (six attributes: `ixx, ixy, ixz, iyy, iyz, izz`). Required for any physics simulation. The tensor must be symmetric and positive-definite. (1 mark)

7. **Auxiliary Elements:** `<material>`, `<gazebo>`, `<transmission>`, `<sensor>` extend the description for color re-use, simulator plugins, actuator bindings, and sensor attachments. (1 mark)

**(b) Complete URDF Design and Inertia Calculation (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Compute the chassis inertia tensor (4 marks).**

Chassis: box of $a = 0.4$ m, $b = 0.3$ m, $c = 0.1$ m, $m = 4$ kg.

$$\begin{aligned}
I_{xx} &= \frac{1}{12}\, m\,(b^2 + c^2) = \frac{1}{12} \times 4 \times (0.09 + 0.01) = 0.0333\,\text{kg·m}^2 \\
I_{yy} &= \frac{1}{12}\, m\,(a^2 + c^2) = \frac{1}{12} \times 4 \times (0.16 + 0.01) = 0.0567\,\text{kg·m}^2 \\
I_{zz} &= \frac{1}{12}\, m\,(a^2 + b^2) = \frac{1}{12} \times 4 \times (0.16 + 0.09) = 0.0833\,\text{kg·m}^2
\end{aligned}$$

Off-diagonals: $I_{xy}=I_{xz}=I_{yz}=0$.

**Step 2: Write the full URDF XML (3 marks).**

```xml
<?xml version="1.0" ?>
<robot name="ktu_mobile_robot">
  <link name="base_link">
    <visual>
      <geometry><box size="0.4 0.3 0.1"/></geometry>
      <material name="blue"><color rgba="0.1 0.3 0.8 1.0"/></material>
    </visual>
    <collision>
      <geometry><box size="0.4 0.3 0.1"/></geometry>
    </collision>
    <inertial>
      <mass value="4.0"/>
      <inertia ixx="0.0333" ixy="0.0" ixz="0.0"
                              iyy="0.0567" iyz="0.0"
                                                  izz="0.0833"/>
    </inertial>
  </link>

  <link name="left_wheel">
    <visual>
      <geometry><cylinder radius="0.08" length="0.04"/></geometry>
    </visual>
    <collision>
      <geometry><cylinder radius="0.08" length="0.04"/></geometry>
    </collision>
    <inertial>
      <mass value="0.4"/>
      <inertia ixx="0.000893" ixy="0.0" ixz="0.0"
                              iyy="0.000893" iyz="0.0"
                                                    izz="0.00128"/>
    </inertial>
  </link>

  <joint name="left_wheel_joint" type="continuous">
    <parent link="base_link"/>
    <child  link="left_wheel"/>
    <origin xyz="0  0.15 0" rpy="-1.5708 0 0"/>
    <axis   xyz="0 0 1"/>
  </joint>

  <link name="right_wheel">
    <visual>
      <geometry><cylinder radius="0.08" length="0.04"/></geometry>
    </visual>
    <collision>
      <geometry><cylinder radius="0.08" length="0.04"/></geometry>
    </collision>
    <inertial>
      <mass value="0.4"/>
      <inertia ixx="0.000893" ixy="0.0" ixz="0.0"
                              iyy="0.000893" iyz="0.0"
                                                    izz="0.00128"/>
    </inertial>
  </link>

  <joint name="right_wheel_joint" type="continuous">
    <parent link="base_link"/>
    <child  link="right_wheel"/>
    <origin xyz="0 -0.15 0" rpy=" 1.5708 0 0"/>
    <axis   xyz="0 0 1"/>
  </joint>

  <link name="caster_wheel">
    <visual>
      <geometry><sphere radius="0.04"/></geometry>
    </visual>
    <collision>
      <geometry><sphere radius="0.04"/></geometry>
    </collision>
    <inertial>
      <mass value="0.15"/>
      <inertia ixx="9.6e-05" ixy="0.0" ixz="0.0"
                              iyy="9.6e-05" iyz="0.0"
                                                  izz="9.6e-05"/>
    </inertial>
  </link>

  <joint name="caster_joint" type="continuous">
    <parent link="base_link"/>
    <child  link="caster_wheel"/>
    <origin xyz="0.15 0 -0.05" rpy="0 0 0"/>
    <axis   xyz="0 0 1"/>
  </joint>
</robot>
```

**Validation of wheel inertia:** With $m = 0.4$ kg, $r = 0.08$ m, $h = 0.04$ m:

$$I_{xx} = I_{yy} = \tfrac{1}{12}\,(0.4)[3(0.0064)+0.0016] = \tfrac{0.4}{12}(0.0208) \approx 6.93 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{kg·m}^2 \;\;(\text{approx. } 0.000893)$$

$$I_{zz} = \tfrac{1}{2}(0.4)(0.0064) = 0.00128\,\text{kg·m}^2$$

**Validation of caster inertia:** With $m = 0.15$ kg, $r = 0.04$ m:

$$I = \tfrac{2}{5}(0.15)(0.0016) = 9.6 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{kg·m}^2$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, PEAET744]`

**(a)** Explain the different types of URDF joints with suitable mobile-robot examples. Show how the joint origin's `rpy` parameter is used to align a cylindrical wheel to roll along the X-axis. (7 marks)

**(b)** Derive the inertia tensor of a solid cylinder used as a wheel in a mobile robot. A wheel of mass $0.6$ kg, radius $0.075$ m, and thickness $0.05$ m is mounted via a `continuous` joint. Compute its principal moments of inertia and the off-diagonal terms, explaining their values. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**(a) Joint Types and Origin Alignment (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Six Joint Types (3 marks).**

1. `fixed` — No motion; attaches sensors or decorative sub-assemblies. *Example: LIDAR mount on chassis.*
2. `revolute` — Rotation about a fixed axis with bounded angle. *Example: steering knuckle of a car-like robot.*
3. `continuous` — Rotation about a fixed axis without bounds. *Example: driven wheels of a differential-drive robot, caster.*
4. `prismatic` — Linear translation along an axis with bounded displacement. *Example: linear slide of a 4-bar linkage suspension.*
5. `floating` — All 6 DOF free. *Example: free-floating satellite model.*
6. `planar` — Motion constrained to a 2-D plane (3 DOF). *Example: theoretical models of planar manipulators.*

**Step 2: Joint Origin and rpy (2 marks).**

The `<origin>` element specifies the static pose of the child link's frame relative to the parent at the joint's *zero state*. It uses two parts: `xyz` (translation in meters) and `rpy` (roll-pitch-yaw in radians, Z-Y-X convention).

**Step 3: Aligning a Cylinder Wheel to Roll along X (2 marks).**

URDF's default cylinder is aligned with its symmetry axis along the local **Z** of the link. To roll along the robot's **X**-axis, the wheel must spin about the robot's **Y**-axis. A rotation of $-\pi/2$ about X (in the joint origin) achieves this:

$$R_x\left(-\tfrac{\pi}{2}\right) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \\ 0 & -1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

After this rotation, the wheel's local Z is parallel to the parent's Y, so the joint `<axis xyz="0 0 1"/>` correctly represents spinning about Y. Translation `xyz="0 ±d 0"` places the wheel at the left or right side at distance $d$ from the centerline.

**(b) Inertia Tensor Derivation for a Cylinder (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Set up the geometry (1 mark).**

A solid cylinder of mass $m$, radius $r$, height $h$, centered at the origin, with symmetry axis along $z$.

**Step 2: Compute $I_{zz}$ (1.5 marks).**

$I_{zz}$ integrates $x^2 + y^2 = \rho^2$ over the volume. Using polar coordinates in the cross-section:

$$I_{zz} = \int_V \rho\,(x^2 + y^2)\,dV = \rho \int_0^h dz \int_0^{2\pi} d\theta \int_0^r \rho'^2 \cdot \rho'\,d\rho' = \rho \cdot h \cdot 2\pi \cdot \frac{r^4}{4}$$

With total mass $m = \rho \pi r^2 h$:

$$I_{zz} = \frac{1}{2}\, m\, r^2$$

**Step 3: Compute $I_{xx}$ (1.5 marks).**

$I_{xx} = \int_V \rho\,(y^2 + z^2)\,dV$. Using the additive decomposition and the fact that for a thin disc of mass $m$ and radius $r$ the polar moment is $\tfrac{1}{2}mr^2$ and the moment about a diameter is $\tfrac{1}{4}mr^2$:

$$I_{xx} = \frac{1}{12}\, m\,(3r^2 + h^2)$$

By symmetry, $I_{yy} = I_{xx}$.

**Step 4: Off-diagonals vanish (1 mark).**

The cylinder is symmetric about all three coordinate planes. For any pair of axes, the integrand (e.g., $xy$) is odd over the symmetric volume, so:

$$I_{xy} = I_{xz} = I_{yz} = 0$$

**Step 5: Numerical evaluation (2 marks).**

With $m = 0.6$ kg, $r = 0.075$ m, $h = 0.05$ m:

$$\begin{aligned}
I_{xx} = I_{yy} &= \frac{1}{12} \times 0.6 \times \left[3(0.075)^2 + (0.05)^2\right] \\
                &= \frac{0.6}{12} \times \left[0.016875 + 0.0025\right] \\
                &= \frac{0.6}{12} \times 0.019375 \\
                &= 9.69 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{kg·m}^2
\end{aligned}$$

$$I_{zz} = \frac{1}{2} \times 0.6 \times (0.075)^2 = 0.3 \times 0.005625 = 1.6875 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{kg·m}^2$$

**Step 6: Final inertia matrix in URDF notation (bonus, 0.5 mark):**

```
ixx = 9.69e-04, ixy = 0, ixz = 0,
iyy = 9.69e-04, iyz = 0,
izz = 1.6875e-03
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation for control:** The relevant moment for wheel angular acceleration is $I_{zz}$ (the spin axis). The motor torque $\tau$ required to produce angular acceleration $\alpha$ is $\tau = I_{zz} \cdot \alpha$. For our wheel, $I_{zz} = 1.69 \times 10^{-3}$ kg·m², which is small — meaning the motor can accelerate the wheel quickly with low torque.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep):**

- **URDF = XML** describing a robot as a tree of **links** connected by **joints**.
- **Three sub-blocks of a link:** `<visual>`, `<collision>`, `<inertial>` — *all three are mandatory for a fully simulating model.*
- **Movable robot ⟹ at least one non-`fixed` joint** (`continuous`, `revolute`, or `prismatic`).
- **Cylinder primitive is Z-aligned by default** — rotate by `±π/2` in `rpy` to make it roll.
- **Off-diagonal inertia terms are zero** for axis-aligned primitives (box, cylinder, sphere) when the frame is at the geometric center.
- **Wheel inertia formula (cylinder):** $I_{xx} = I_{yy} = \tfrac{1}{12}\, m\,(3r^2 + h^2)$, $I_{zz} = \tfrac{1}{2}\, m\, r^2$.
- **Box inertia formula:** $I_{xx} = \tfrac{1}{12}\, m\,(b^2 + c^2)$ and cyclic permutations.
- **Sphere inertia formula:** $I = \tfrac{2}{5}\, m\, r^2$ (isotropic — all three diagonal values equal).
- **rpy convention:** Z-Y-X extrinsic (yaw, then pitch, then roll), **always in radians**, not degrees.
- **Kinematic tree is a TREE** — one root, no cycles; otherwise use SDF.
- **Joint axis** is specified in the **child link's** local frame.
- **Negative signs in rpy for left vs. right wheel** ensure both wheels spin the same direction for forward motion.
- **URDF validates with `check_urdf`** CLI in ROS — always run it before submission/exam.
- **TF tree** is generated automatically from URDF by `robot_state_publisher`.
- **Differential-drive odometry:** $v = \tfrac{r}{2}(\omega_R + \omega_L)$, $\dot{\psi} = \tfrac{r}{L}(\omega_R - \omega_L)$.
- **Inertia tensor must be positive-definite** — diagonal entries > 0; otherwise the simulator will reject the model.
- **Use Xacro for modular URDFs** when the robot has many repeated components (e.g., 4-wheeled robot, multi-finger gripper).
- **`<transmission>` and `<gazebo>` tags** are essential when moving from pure visualization to actual hardware/simulator control.
- **Dimensional consistency** in URDF XML: lengths in **meters**, masses in **kilograms**, angles in **radians**, inertias in **kg·m²**.
- **Visual ≠ Collision geometry** in general — collision can be a simpler primitive for performance, while visual can be a high-resolution mesh.
- **For ESE:** if asked to "design a URDF," always (1) list dimensions and masses, (2) draw the kinematic tree, (3) compute inertia tensors, (4) write complete XML with `<inertial>` blocks, (5) verify the wheel rpy and axis.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sensors for Mobile Robots - Sensor Classification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET744 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Sensors for Mobile Robots - Sensor Classification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

A **sensor** in mobile robotics is a physical device that detects changes in the internal state of the robot (such as position, velocity, acceleration, joint angles) or changes in the external environment (such as distance to obstacles, light intensity, temperature, presence of landmarks), and converts this information into an electrical signal that can be processed by the robot's controller.

**Sensor Classification** is the systematic categorization of robotic sensors based on:
1. The **nature of the measured quantity** (functional/operational classification).
2. The **source of energy** used by the sensor (active vs passive).
3. The **type of signal output** (qualitative vs quantitative).
4. The **measurement principle** (electromagnetic, acoustic, mechanical, etc.).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine a human navigating a dark, unfamiliar room:**
> Your skin (touch), ears (sound), eyes (vision), and inner ear (balance) all act as sensors. Your eyes actively emit light signals (well, they process reflected light), your skin passively senses contact, and your inner ear provides proprioceptive feedback about your own body posture.

A mobile robot faces exactly the same challenge. It needs **eyes** (cameras, LIDAR), **ears** (microphones, ultrasonic sensors), **skin** (tactile/bump sensors), and an **inner ear** (IMUs, encoders) to navigate its world. The classification scheme helps engineers pick the right combination of "senses" for a given task — much like a doctor chooses medical tests based on what they need to diagnose.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 Scheme for PEAET744 places strong emphasis on understanding *why* a particular sensor is chosen for a specific task — not just memorizing categories. Students must connect classification to *robot perception challenges* like localization, mapping, and obstacle avoidance.

### 1.3 Key Physical Quantities and Constants

- **Speed of light in vacuum:** $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s
- **Speed of sound in air at 20 °C:** $v_s \approx 343$ m/s
- **Standard gravitational acceleration:** $g = 9.81$ m/s²
- **Sampling frequency unit:** Hz (samples per second)
- **Range / Field of View (FOV):** meters (m) or degrees (°)
- **Resolution:** smallest detectable change in the measured quantity

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Primary Classification Axes

#### A. Functional / Operational Classification
This is the **most important classification for KTU exams**, because it directly maps to robot perception tasks.

- **Proprioceptive Sensors:** Measure the **robot's internal state** — wheel rotations, joint angles, battery voltage, heading, linear/angular velocity.
  - *Examples:* Optical encoders, gyroscopes, accelerometers, current sensors.
- **Exteroceptive Sensors:** Measure the **robot's external environment** — distance to obstacles, presence of landmarks, ambient light, GPS signals.
  - *Examples:* Ultrasonic sensors, LIDAR, cameras, GPS receivers, infrared range finders.
- **Interoceptive (sometimes grouped):** Sensors that monitor internal parameters affecting operation, like battery level or motor temperature.

#### B. Active vs Passive (Energy-Source Classification)

- **Active Sensors:** Inject energy into the environment and measure the reflected/returned signal.
  - *Examples:* Ultrasonic sonar, LIDAR, radar, active infrared.
- **Passive Sensors:** Rely on natural energy emitted or reflected by the environment.
  - *Examples:* Cameras (CCD/CMOS), passive infrared (PIR), thermocouples, magnetometers.

#### C. Qualitative vs Quantitative

- **Qualitative (or "logical") sensors:** Output discrete binary or categorical states. E.g., a bump sensor returns `HIT / NO HIT`.
- **Quantitative (or "analog") sensors:** Output continuous numerical values proportional to the measured quantity. E.g., a potentiometer returning a voltage proportional to joint angle.

#### D. Measurement Range — Short vs Long

- **Contact / Short-range sensors:** Bumpers, whisker sensors, tactile skins.
- **Medium-range sensors:** Sonar (centimeters to ~10 m), IR range finders.
- **Long-range sensors:** 2D/3D LIDAR (up to 100 m), stereo cameras, radar.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following equations appear repeatedly in KTU University Exams for sensor performance characterization. Memorize the symbols and units.**

| # | Quantity / Performance Metric | Formula / Definition | Typical Units | Notes / Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Resolution** | $\Delta x = \dfrac{x_{\max} - x_{\min}}{2^n}$ | Same as measured quantity | $n$ = number of bits in ADC; quantizes analog-to-digital conversion |
| 2 | **Sensor Range** | $r_{\min} \le r \le r_{\max}$ | m | Useful range between minimum and maximum sensing distance |
| 3 | **Field of View (FOV)** | $\theta_{FOV} = 2 \cdot \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{d}{2f}\right)$ | rad or ° | $d$ = sensor dimension, $f$ = focal length (camera-like sensors) |
| 4 | **Time of Flight (ToF) range** | $d = \dfrac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$ | m | $c$ = signal speed, $\Delta t$ = round-trip time; applies to LIDAR, sonar, radar |
| 5 | **Angular resolution of rotating LIDAR** | $\Delta\theta = \dfrac{360°}{N}$ | °/sample | $N$ = number of samples per full revolution |
| 6 | **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)** | $\text{SNR} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{\text{signal}}}{P_{\text{noise}}}\right)$ | dB | Higher SNR → more reliable measurement |
| 7 | **Encoder counts to distance** | $d = \dfrac{\pi D \cdot N_{\text{counts}}}{n \cdot C}$ | m | $D$ = wheel diameter, $n$ = gear ratio, $C$ = counts per encoder revolution |
| 8 | **Gyroscope drift rate** | $\theta(t) = \theta_0 + \omega t + \tfrac{1}{2}\alpha t^2$ | rad | $\omega$ = constant angular rate, $\alpha$ = bias drift |
| 9 | **Accelerometer scale factor** | $V_{\text{out}} = V_0 + S \cdot a + \text{noise}$ | V | $S$ = sensitivity in V/(m/s²) |
| 10 | **Beam divergence of an active sensor** | $d_{\text{spot}}(R) = d_0 + 2 R \tan(\beta/2)$ | m | $R$ = range, $\beta$ = divergence half-angle |

### 2.3 Why These Classifications Matter in Engineering

- **Functional classification** drives the *localization and mapping algorithm* choice. E.g., wheel encoders (proprioceptive) give odometry for dead reckoning; LIDAR (exteroceptive) gives landmarks for SLAM.
- **Active vs passive** determines *operability in adverse conditions*. Passive cameras fail in the dark; active LIDAR works at night but may be blinded by another robot's emitter.
- **Range and FOV** dictate *sensor placement and density* on a robot. A wide-FOV LIDAR can sit on the roof; a narrow-beam sonar needs multiple units around the chassis.
- **Resolution** bounds the *smallest distinguishable feature* in mapping — too low a resolution, and the robot cannot recognize a doorway.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section deepens the mathematical reasoning behind the most-tested formulas in sensor classification problems.

### 3.1 Derivation: Time-of-Flight Range Equation

Active sensors like LIDAR, radar, and sonar determine distance by emitting a pulse and measuring how long it takes to return.

**Step 1 — Define the geometry.**
Let the sensor emit a pulse at time $t_0$. The pulse travels outward at speed $c$ (or $v_s$ for sound), hits an obstacle at distance $d$, and returns along the same path.

**Step 2 — Express the one-way travel time.**
The pulse covers a one-way distance of $d$ meters. Hence, the one-way time is:

$$
t_{\text{one-way}} = \dfrac{d}{c}
$$

**Step 3 — Account for round-trip travel.**
The measured round-trip time $\Delta t$ is twice the one-way time because the pulse travels to the obstacle *and* back:

$$
\Delta t = 2 \cdot t_{\text{one-way}} = 2 \cdot \dfrac{d}{c}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for distance.**

$$
d = \dfrac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}
$$

**Step 5 — Sanity check with numbers.** For a typical indoor sonar with $c = v_s = 343$ m/s, an obstacle 1 m away gives:

$$
\Delta t = \dfrac{2 \times 1}{343} \approx 5.83 \text{ ms}
$$

This is why sonar is fast enough for real-time obstacle avoidance but limited in range — the signal attenuates with distance.

### 3.2 Derivation: Encoder-Based Odometry

Wheel encoders count the number of pulses generated as the wheel rotates. Each pulse corresponds to a known angular increment.

**Step 1 — Single-count linear travel.**
If a wheel of diameter $D$ has $C$ counts per full revolution, the linear distance traveled per count is:

$$
\Delta d_{\text{per count}} = \dfrac{\pi D}{C}
$$

**Step 2 — Total distance from $N_{\text{counts}}$ counts.**

$$
d = N_{\text{counts}} \cdot \dfrac{\pi D}{C}
$$

**Step 3 — Include gear ratio.**
When the encoder is mounted on the motor shaft *before* a gear reduction $n$, the wheel rotates slower than the encoder. Therefore, the formula becomes:

$$
d = N_{\text{counts}} \cdot \dfrac{\pi D}{n \cdot C}
$$

**Step 4 — Worked example.**
Let $D = 0.2$ m, $C = 1000$ counts/rev, $n = 1$, $N_{\text{counts}} = 250$.
Then:

$$
d = 250 \cdot \dfrac{\pi \times 0.2}{1 \times 1000} = 250 \cdot \dfrac{0.6283}{1000} = 0.157 \text{ m}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation: Field of View for a Pinhole Camera-Like Sensor

**Step 1 — Define geometry.**
A pinhole camera with focal length $f$ images a scene through an aperture. A sensor of horizontal extent $d$ (e.g., CCD width) captures light over a horizontal angle $\theta_{\text{FOV}}$.

**Step 2 — Apply the thin-lens geometry.**
From similar triangles, the half-angle subtended by the sensor edge at the pinhole is:

$$
\tan\!\left(\dfrac{\theta_{\text{FOV}}}{2}\right) = \dfrac{d/2}{f}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the full field of view.**

$$
\theta_{\text{FOV}} = 2 \cdot \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{d}{2f}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Worked example.**
A camera with $d = 36$ mm (full-frame 35 mm sensor) and $f = 50$ mm yields:

$$
\theta_{\text{FOV}} = 2 \cdot \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{36}{2 \times 50}\right) = 2 \cdot \arctan(0.36) \approx 2 \times 19.8° = 39.6°
$$

This matches the classic diagonal-FOV of a 50 mm lens.

### 3.4 Derivation: Resolution from ADC Bit Depth

**Step 1 — Define dynamic range.**
A sensor measures a quantity $x$ over a known interval $[x_{\min}, x_{\max}]$. An Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC) with $n$ bits produces $2^n$ discrete levels.

**Step 2 — Compute resolution.**

$$
\Delta x = \dfrac{x_{\max} - x_{\min}}{2^n}
$$

**Step 3 — Worked example.**
An ultrasonic range sensor spans 0.05 m to 5 m, digitized by a 10-bit ADC.

$$
\Delta x = \dfrac{5 - 0.05}{2^{10}} = \dfrac{4.95}{1024} \approx 4.83 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 4.83 \text{ mm}
$$

This is the smallest distance change the robot can reliably distinguish.

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Major Sensor Classes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This matrix is high-yield for KTU Part A questions (compare any two sensors).**

| Property | Wheel Encoder | IMU (Acc + Gyro) | Ultrasonic Sonar | LIDAR (2D) | Mono Camera | GPS |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Type** | Proprioceptive | Proprioceptive | Exteroceptive (Active) | Exteroceptive (Active) | Exteroceptive (Passive) | Exteroceptive (Passive) |
| **Energy Source** | Passive | Passive | Active (acoustic) | Active (laser) | Passive (ambient light) | Passive (satellite) |
| **Range** | Unlimited (contact) | Unlimited | ~0.02 – 10 m | ~0.1 – 100 m | ~1 – 100 m (depends on optics) | Global, ±5 m typical |
| **Output** | Quantitative | Quantitative | Quantitative | Quantitative | Quantitative | Quantitative |
| **Frequency** | kHz | 100 – 1000 Hz | ~20 Hz typical | 5 – 40 Hz | 30 – 60 fps | 1 – 10 Hz |
| **Failure Mode** | Wheel slip | Bias drift | Specular reflection, cross-talk | Sun interference, rain, fog | Lighting changes | Indoor dead zones, multipath |
| **Best Use** | Odometry | Inertial nav short-term | Cheap obstacle detection | SLAM, navigation | Object recognition | Outdoor global localization |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Sensor Classification

The following Mermaid flowchart depicts how sensor signals propagate from the physical world through the sensor, the interface electronics, and finally into the robot's perception stack. This is also the path along which classification decisions are made.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Classify sensors used in mobile robots based on their functional classification. Give two examples for each category.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Based on function, mobile robot sensors are classified into two categories:

1. **Proprioceptive sensors** — measure the *internal state* of the robot (position, velocity, orientation, motor currents).
   - *Examples:* (i) Optical wheel encoder, (ii) MEMS gyroscope, (iii) Accelerometer.
2. **Exteroceptive sensors** — measure the *external environment* (obstacle distance, landmark positions, ambient light).
   - *Examples:* (i) Ultrasonic range finder, (ii) 2D LIDAR, (iii) CCD/CMOS camera, (iv) GPS receiver.

This classification is essential because proprioceptive sensors drive *dead reckoning*, while exteroceptive sensors enable *localization, mapping, and obstacle avoidance*.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between active and passive sensors. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Active Sensor | Passive Sensor |
|---|---|---|
| Energy injection | Emits its own signal into the environment | Does not emit; relies on ambient/external energy |
| Dependence on environment | Independent of external lighting/heat | Heavily dependent on ambient energy |
| Examples | Ultrasonic sonar, LIDAR, Radar | Camera, Passive Infrared (PIR), GPS, Magnetometer |

Active sensors (e.g., LIDAR) emit laser pulses and measure the reflected beam to compute distance via Time-of-Flight. Passive sensors (e.g., a vision camera) simply detect ambient light reflected from objects.

---

### 5.2 Part B Question (14 Marks) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Part B Module 2 carries 14 marks, typically split as 7 + 7 marks across two sub-parts. Students answer either Option A **or** Option B.

---

#### **Option A — Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q. (a) [7 Marks] Explain in detail the functional classification of sensors used in mobile robots. Discuss how the choice of sensor affects the choice of localization algorithm.**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Functional classification [3 marks]:**
Sensors in mobile robots are functionally classified as:
- **Proprioceptive** — measure internal robot state (e.g., wheel encoders, gyroscopes, accelerometers, joint-angle potentiometers). These directly contribute to *dead reckoning* and *inertial navigation*.
- **Exteroceptive** — measure the external environment (e.g., cameras, LIDAR, sonar, GPS, magnetometers). These enable *landmark-based localization*, *SLAM*, and *obstacle detection*.

**2. Sensor–algorithm coupling [2 marks]:**
- **Proprioceptive-only (encoder + IMU):** leads to *dead reckoning* algorithms. Errors accumulate unboundedly with distance — limited by drift.
- **Exteroceptive-only (camera + GPS):** enables *global localization* but suffers from dynamic environments and signal outages.
- **Hybrid (encoder + IMU + LIDAR):** enables *sensor fusion* techniques such as the Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) and Particle Filter SLAM, combining drift correction with global referencing.

**3. Trade-offs [2 marks]:**
- High-resolution LIDAR gives dense maps but is expensive and heavy.
- Cheap sonar arrays are affordable but suffer from specular reflection and cross-talk.
- Cameras are versatile but computationally expensive and lighting-sensitive.

**Conclusion:** The classification is not just a labeling exercise — it dictates the *type of perception algorithm* and the *overall system architecture* of the mobile robot.

---

**Q. (b) [7 Marks] An indoor mobile robot uses a 2D LIDAR with 720 samples per revolution mounted at 30 cm height. The LIDAR emits pulses of speed $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s. For an obstacle detected at 4 m, calculate: (i) the round-trip time, (ii) the angular resolution between consecutive samples, and (iii) the linear separation between two adjacent scan points on the obstacle.**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Round-trip time [2 marks]:**
Using the Time-of-Flight formula:

$$
\Delta t = \dfrac{2 d}{c} = \dfrac{2 \times 4}{3 \times 10^8} = \dfrac{8}{3 \times 10^8} \approx 2.67 \times 10^{-8} \text{ s} = 26.7 \text{ ns}
$$

**(ii) Angular resolution [2 marks]:**

$$
\Delta\theta = \dfrac{360°}{720} = 0.5°/\text{sample}
$$

**(iii) Linear separation on obstacle [3 marks]:**
For small angles, the arc length $s \approx R \cdot \Delta\theta$ (with $\Delta\theta$ in radians).

Convert $\Delta\theta$ to radians:

$$
0.5° \times \dfrac{\pi}{180°} = 8.727 \times 10^{-3} \text{ rad}
$$

Then the linear separation between two adjacent scan points on an obstacle 4 m away is:

$$
s = R \cdot \Delta\theta = 4 \times 8.727 \times 10^{-3} \approx 3.49 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m} \approx 3.49 \text{ cm}
$$

This is the spatial resolution limit — the robot cannot resolve features smaller than ~3.5 cm on an object 4 m away.

---

#### **Option B — Question B (14 Marks)**

**Q. (a) [7 Marks] With the help of a neat diagram, classify sensors based on energy source. Compare active and passive sensors across at least five parameters.**

**Model Answer:**

**Diagram [2 marks]:** *(Draw a tree showing Sensors → Active vs Passive → each with examples.)*

**Comparison Table [5 marks]:**

| Parameter | Active Sensor | Passive Sensor |
|---|---|---|
| Energy source | Self-emitted (laser, sonar pulse, RF) | Relies on natural / ambient energy |
| Power consumption | Higher (emitter + receiver) | Lower (receiver only) |
| Accuracy | Higher — controlled signal | Lower — affected by environment |
| Environmental dependence | Lower — works in dark | Higher — needs light / heat |
| Examples | LIDAR, sonar, radar, active IR | Camera, PIR, magnetometer, GPS |
| Failure modes | Interference from other active sensors | Sun glare, shadows, thermal noise |
| Cost | Usually higher | Usually lower |

**Conclusion [included for completeness]:** Active sensors dominate in *range and accuracy-critical* applications (autonomous cars, industrial robots), while passive sensors are preferred when *power efficiency* and *stealth* matter (surveillance drones, planetary rovers in power-budgeted mode).

---

**Q. (b) [7 Marks] A differential-drive mobile robot has wheel encoders with $C = 500$ counts/rev and wheel diameter $D = 15$ cm. The left wheel records 1200 counts and the right wheel records 1500 counts while traversing straight. Compute the distance traveled by each wheel and the total slip, if any.**

**Model Answer:**

**Distance per count:**
The distance covered by one count is:

$$
\Delta d = \dfrac{\pi D}{C} = \dfrac{\pi \times 0.15}{500} = \dfrac{0.4712}{500} = 9.425 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}
$$

**Left wheel distance:**

$$
d_L = 1200 \times 9.425 \times 10^{-4} \approx 1.131 \text{ m}
$$

**Right wheel distance:**

$$
d_R = 1500 \times 9.425 \times 10^{-4} \approx 1.414 \text{ m}
$$

**Analysis [3 marks]:**
- If both wheels *should* have traveled the same distance (straight-line motion), then the difference of:

$$
d_R - d_L = 1.414 - 1.131 = 0.283 \text{ m}
$$

indicates the robot is *not actually traveling straight* — it is curving to the **left** (right wheel covers more distance). This is a common source of odometric error. The apparent slip is actually wheel-speed mismatch; true wheel slip (no rotation but motion) would manifest as both wheels recording counts without robot movement.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Module 2 — Sensor Classification):**
> 1. **Mixing up proprioceptive and exteroceptive.** Wheel encoders are *proprioceptive*, not exteroceptive. GPS is *exteroceptive*, not proprioceptive. Many students swap these.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in Time-of-Flight.** LIDAR/sonar distance is $d = c\Delta t / 2$. Using $d = c\Delta t$ will *double* the answer and lose full marks.
> 3. **Units mismatch.** Angular resolution must be in **radians** when computing arc length $s = R\Delta\theta$. Mixing degrees with meters is a silent killer.
> 4. **No discussion of failure modes in Part B.** KTU examiners award marks for *limitations* and *trade-offs* — do not write only advantages.
> 5. **Skipping the diagram.** Even if the question doesn't ask for one explicitly, KTU evaluators award 1–2 marks for a *neat, labeled* classification diagram in any sensor-comparison answer.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Sensor Classification (Module 2):**
> - ✅ **Functional classification** is *Proprioceptive* vs *Exteroceptive* — the most important KTU classification.
> - ✅ **Energy classification** is *Active* (self-emits) vs *Passive* (ambient energy only).
> - ✅ **Output classification** is *Qualitative* (binary/categorical) vs *Quantitative* (continuous).
> - ✅ **Range classification** spans *contact* → *short* → *medium* → *long* range.
> - ✅ Time-of-Flight range: $d = c \Delta t / 2$ — applies to LIDAR, sonar, radar.
> - ✅ Encoder distance: $d = N_{\text{counts}} \cdot \pi D / C$ (with gear ratio $n$ if applicable).
> - ✅ Camera FOV: $\theta_{\text{FOV}} = 2 \arctan(d / 2f)$.
> - ✅ ADC resolution: $\Delta x = (x_{\max} - x_{\min}) / 2^n$.
> - ✅ Proprioceptive sensors → *dead reckoning*; Exteroceptive → *localization & mapping*.
> - ✅ Active sensors are accurate but power-hungry; Passive sensors are power-efficient but environment-dependent.
> - ✅ Always mention **failure modes** in KTU answers — examiners value *critical analysis*, not just enumeration.
> - ✅ Typical KTU exam expects a **neat labeled diagram** for any 7-mark classification question.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Characterizing Sensor Performance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET744 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Characterizing Sensor Performance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of mobile robotics, **Characterizing Sensor Performance** refers to the systematic quantitative and qualitative evaluation of a sensor's ability to convert physical phenomena (such as light, sound, distance, temperature, or magnetic field) into measurable electrical signals with acceptable fidelity. This characterization is governed by a set of standardized **performance metrics** that describe how accurately, reliably, and efficiently a sensor performs under specified operating conditions.

The principal performance descriptors used by KTU 2024 syllabus are:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Performance Descriptors**
> - **Accuracy** – Closeness of measurement to the true value.
> - **Precision / Repeatability** – Ability to produce identical outputs for identical inputs.
> - **Resolution** – Smallest detectable change in the measured quantity.
> - **Sensitivity** – Ratio of output change to input change (slope of transfer function).
> - **Range (Dynamic Range)** – Minimum and maximum measurable input values.
> - **Linearity** – Degree to which output is a linear function of input.
> - **Bandwidth / Response Time** – Speed of response to changing input.
> - **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)** – Useful signal level relative to background noise.
> - **Hysteresis** – Difference in output for increasing vs. decreasing inputs.
> - **Drift** – Slow change in output with time or environmental factors.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Weighing Scale at a Vegetable Shop**
>
> Imagine two weighing scales in a shop. The first is a cheap digital scale; the second is a calibrated jeweller's balance.
>
> - The **jeweller's balance** consistently shows 100.00 g every time you place the same gold piece — this is **high precision** and **high accuracy**.
> - The cheap digital scale may show 98 g, 102 g, 99 g for the same weight — this is **low precision** even if the average is near 100 g.
> - The cheapest scale cannot detect the difference between 100 g and 100.5 g — it has **poor resolution**.
> - If the cheap scale's reading slowly creeps from 100 g to 101 g over a day even with no load, it has **drift**.
> - If it shows different readings for the same weight when approached from above vs. below, it has **hysteresis**.
>
> In mobile robotics, our sensors (LiDAR, IMU, encoders, cameras) are these "scales" — and the **robot's intelligence depends entirely on how trustworthy these measurements are**.

### 1.3 Physical and Mathematical Constants

The characterization framework relies on the following universally accepted constants and reference metrics:

- **True Value (x_true)** – The actual physical quantity, denoted as $x_{true}$.
- **Measured Value (x_meas)** – The output reported by the sensor.
- **Absolute Error** – $\varepsilon_{abs} = \vert x_{meas} - x_{true} \vert$
- **Percent Error** – $\varepsilon_{\%} = \dfrac{\vert x_{meas} - x_{true} \vert}{x_{true}} \times 100\%$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Industry Standard** — The **Root Mean Square Error (RMSE)** is the de-facto industry standard for evaluating the **overall** performance of a robotic sensor over many trials.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown of Each Performance Metric

#### A. Accuracy vs. Precision

- **Accuracy** quantifies the **systematic bias** of a sensor — how close the *mean* of measurements is to the *true value*.
- **Precision** quantifies the **random scatter** of measurements about the mean — described statistically by standard deviation $\sigma$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in mobile robotics:** A poorly accurate wheel encoder causes cumulative odometry drift; a poorly precise IMU causes noisy SLAM maps. The two failures have entirely different remedies (calibration vs. filtering).

#### B. Resolution

The **resolution** $\Delta x_{min}$ is the smallest incremental change in the input that produces a detectable change in the output. For a digital sensor with $N$ bits over a full-scale range $R$:

$$\Delta x_{min} = \frac{R}{2^N - 1}$$

- A 12-bit encoder over a $360°$ range gives $\Delta\theta \approx 0.0879°$.
- A higher resolution does **not** guarantee higher accuracy — the bits may all be "wrong" together (quantization bias).

#### C. Sensitivity

Sensitivity $S$ is the slope of the sensor's transfer function at the operating point:

$$S = \frac{\partial y}{\partial x} \bigg\vert_{x = x_0}$$

where $y$ is the output and $x$ is the input. A highly sensitive sensor produces large output variations for small input changes — desirable for detecting subtle phenomena (e.g., gas leak detection).

#### D. Linearity and Non-Linearity Error

A sensor is **linear** if its transfer function $y = f(x)$ satisfies:

$$f(x) = S \cdot x + b$$

The **non-linearity error** is the maximum deviation of the actual transfer curve from the best-fit straight line, expressed as a percentage of the full-scale output (FSO):

$$\varepsilon_{NL} = \frac{\max \vert y_{actual} - y_{ideal} \vert}{y_{FSO}} \times 100\%$$

#### E. Hysteresis

Hysteresis error is the maximum difference between the upward-going (ascending) and downward-going (descending) calibration curves at the same input value:

$$\varepsilon_{hys} = \max \vert y_{up}(x) - y_{down}(x) \vert$$

Common in magnetic, mechanical, and capacitive sensors due to friction, magnetic remanence, or charge trapping.

#### F. Bandwidth and Response Time

- **Rise Time ($t_r$)** — Time for output to go from 10% to 90% of final value.
- **Settling Time ($t_s$)** — Time for output to remain within $\pm 2\%$ (or $\pm 5\%$) of the final value.
- **Bandwidth (BW)** — Frequency at which output amplitude drops to $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ (-3 dB) of its DC value.

For a first-order sensor: $BW = \frac{1}{2\pi \tau}$ where $\tau$ is the time constant.

#### G. Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)

$$SNR = \frac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}} = \left(\frac{A_{signal}}{A_{noise}}\right)^2$$

Expressed in decibels: $SNR_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}}\right)$

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Performance Metric | Mathematical Definition | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Absolute Error | $\varepsilon_{abs} = \vert x_{meas} - x_{true} \vert$ | Same as input |
| 2 | Percent Error | $\varepsilon_{\%} = \dfrac{\vert x_{meas} - x_{true} \vert}{x_{true}} \times 100\%$ | Dimensionless (%) |
| 3 | RMSE | $RMSE = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - x_{true})^2}$ | Same as input |
| 4 | Resolution (ADC) | $\Delta x_{min} = \dfrac{R}{2^N - 1}$ | $R$ = range, $N$ = bits |
| 5 | Sensitivity | $S = \dfrac{\partial y}{\partial x}$ | Output/Input |
| 6 | Non-linearity Error | $\varepsilon_{NL} = \dfrac{\max \vert y_{actual} - y_{ideal} \vert}{y_{FSO}} \times 100\%$ | % of FSO |
| 7 | Hysteresis | $\varepsilon_{hys} = \max \vert y_{up}(x) - y_{down}(x) \vert$ | Same as output |
| 8 | SNR | $SNR_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}}\right)$ | Decibels (dB) |
| 9 | Bandwidth (1st order) | $BW = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \tau}$ | Hz |
| 10 | Drift | $\dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta t}$ at constant $x$ | Output/Time |
| 11 | Dynamic Range | $DR = 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{x_{max}}{x_{min}}\right)$ | dB |
| 12 | Precision (std. dev.) | $\sigma = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N-1}\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}$ | Same as input |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** Autonomous vehicles (e.g., self-driving cars) require LiDAR sensors with sub-cm resolution, sub-degree accuracy, $\geq 10$ Hz update rate, and SNR $> 40$ dB to function safely in mixed traffic — every metric above directly influences a KTU student's future industrial sensor-selection decision.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Resolution of a Digital Rotary Encoder

**Problem Context:** A KTU student is designing a differential-drive robot's odometry system. They select a wheel encoder with $N = 12$ bits, mounted on a wheel of radius $r = 5$ cm. Derive the **angular resolution** and the resulting **linear distance resolution** per encoder tick.

**Step 1 — Express the full-scale range of the encoder.**

The encoder measures angular position over a full revolution of $360°$. Thus:

$$R = 360°$$

**Step 2 — Apply the resolution formula for an $N$-bit digital sensor.**

The number of discrete codes is $2^N$, spanning the range $R$ in $2^N - 1$ intervals:

$$\Delta \theta_{min} = \frac{R}{2^N - 1}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $N = 12$.**

$$\Delta \theta_{min} = \frac{360°}{2^{12} - 1} = \frac{360°}{4096 - 1} = \frac{360°}{4095}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the numerical value.**

$$\Delta \theta_{min} = 0.0879° \text{ per tick}$$

**Step 5 — Convert angular resolution to linear distance resolution.**

For a wheel of radius $r$, the arc length per radian is $r$, so the linear distance per tick is:

$$\Delta s = r \cdot \Delta \theta_{min} \quad \text{(with } \Delta\theta \text{ in radians)}$$

**Step 6 — Convert $0.0879°$ to radians.**

$$\Delta \theta_{min} \text{ (rad)} = 0.0879 \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 1.534 \times 10^{-3} \text{ rad}$$

**Step 7 — Substitute $r = 5$ cm $= 0.05$ m.**

$$\Delta s = 0.05 \times 1.534 \times 10^{-3} = 7.67 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}$$

**Step 8 — Final Result.**

$$\boxed{\Delta s \approx 76.7 \, \mu m \text{ per encoder tick}}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** After 1000 encoder ticks, the wheel has rolled $7.67$ cm. This quantifies the **odometric resolution** — a critical input to any Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) localization design.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Sensitivity and Non-linearity from a Calibration Curve

**Problem Context:** A thermistor used in a robot's motor-temperature monitoring system is calibrated against a reference thermometer. Five calibration points $(T_i, V_i)$ are recorded:

| $T$ (°C) | $V$ (V) |
|---|---|
| 25 | 0.50 |
| 40 | 0.82 |
| 55 | 1.18 |
| 70 | 1.50 |
| 85 | 1.85 |

Compute (a) the **best-fit linear sensitivity** using least squares, and (b) the **maximum non-linearity error** in % FSO.

**Step 1 — State the linear model.**

$$V = S \cdot T + b$$

**Step 2 — Recall the least-squares formulas.**

$$S = \frac{N \sum T_i V_i - \sum T_i \sum V_i}{N \sum T_i^2 - (\sum T_i)^2}, \quad b = \bar{V} - S \bar{T}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the sums from the table.**

$$N = 5$$

$$\sum T_i = 25 + 40 + 55 + 70 + 85 = 275$$

$$\sum V_i = 0.50 + 0.82 + 1.18 + 1.50 + 1.85 = 5.85$$

$$\sum T_i^2 = 625 + 1600 + 3025 + 4900 + 7225 = 17375$$

$$\sum T_i V_i = (25)(0.50) + (40)(0.82) + (55)(1.18) + (70)(1.50) + (85)(1.85)$$

$$= 12.5 + 32.8 + 64.9 + 105.0 + 157.25 = 372.45$$

**Step 4 — Compute the means.**

$$\bar{T} = \frac{275}{5} = 55, \quad \bar{V} = \frac{5.85}{5} = 1.17$$

**Step 5 — Compute the sensitivity.**

$$S = \frac{5 \times 372.45 - 275 \times 5.85}{5 \times 17375 - 275^2}$$

$$S = \frac{1862.25 - 1608.75}{86875 - 75625} = \frac{253.50}{11250}$$

$$\boxed{S = 0.02253 \text{ V/°C}}$$

**Step 6 — Compute the intercept.**

$$b = 1.17 - (0.02253)(55) = 1.17 - 1.2392 = -0.0692 \text{ V}$$

**Step 7 — Best-fit linear model.**

$$V_{ideal} = 0.02253 \, T - 0.0692$$

**Step 8 — Compute the residuals $\Delta V_i = V_i - V_{ideal}(T_i)$.**

| $T$ | $V_{actual}$ | $V_{ideal}$ | $\Delta V$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 25 | 0.50 | 0.4941 | +0.0059 |
| 40 | 0.82 | 0.8320 | -0.0120 |
| 55 | 1.18 | 1.1700 | +0.0100 |
| 70 | 1.50 | 1.5079 | -0.0079 |
| 85 | 1.85 | 1.8459 | +0.0041 |

**Step 9 — Identify the maximum deviation.**

$$\max \vert \Delta V \vert = 0.0120 \text{ V at } T = 40°C$$

**Step 10 — Determine the Full-Scale Output (FSO).**

$$y_{FSO} = V(85°C) - V(25°C) = 1.85 - 0.50 = 1.35 \text{ V}$$

**Step 11 — Compute the non-linearity error.**

$$\varepsilon_{NL} = \frac{0.0120}{1.35} \times 100\% = 0.89\%$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_{NL} \approx 0.89\% \text{ FSO}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Decision Rule (KTU 2024 Lab Standard):** A sensor with $\varepsilon_{NL} < 1\%$ FSO is considered **highly linear** and can be used without look-up-table correction in most mobile-robotics applications. Values between 1% and 5% require polynomial calibration; values $> 5\%$ mandate sensor replacement or advanced compensation (e.g., neural network calibration).

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Sensor Performance Classes

| Performance Attribute | Wheel Encoder | MEMS Gyroscope | LiDAR | Camera (Vision) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Typical Resolution | 12–16 bit | $\leq 0.01 °/s$ | $\pm 2$ cm @ 50 m | 1 pixel (VGA–HD) |
| Accuracy | High (mechanical) | Moderate (drift) | High (geometric) | Moderate (lighting) |
| Update Rate | $1$–$10$ kHz | $100$–$1$ kHz | $5$–$20$ Hz | $30$–$60$ Hz |
| Range | Unlimited (rotation) | $\pm 2000 °/s$ | $0.1$–$200$ m | $0.5$–$\infty$ m |
| Dominant Error | Quantization | Bias drift | Multipath reflection | Illumination variation |
| Cost | Low | Low–Medium | High | Medium |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Sensor Performance Characterization Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Characterization] --> B[Define Operating Conditions]
    B --> C[Static Tests DC Inputs]
    B --> D[Dynamic Tests Sinusoidal Step Inputs]
    C --> E[Calibration Curve y vs x]
    E --> F[Compute Sensitivity Slope]
    E --> G[Compute Non-linearity Error]
    E --> H[Compute Hysteresis Error]
    D --> I[Measure Rise Time Settling Time]
    D --> J[Compute Bandwidth at minus 3 dB]
    F --> K[Compute Resolution from ADC Bits]
    G --> K
    H --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Compute SNR from Signal and Noise Variance]
    L --> M[Generate Performance Report]
    M --> N[Decision Accept or Reject Sensor]
    N --> O[End Characterization]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Level Architecture Explanation:** The diagram above shows the canonical KTU-recommended test sequence. **Static tests** yield the calibration curve from which accuracy, precision, sensitivity, linearity, and hysteresis are extracted. **Dynamic tests** (step and frequency response) yield the bandwidth and response-time figures. Resolution and SNR are then computed as derived quantities that combine static and dynamic observations.

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Taxonomy of Sensor Performance Metrics

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Sensor Performance Characterization] --> Q[Static Characteristics]
    P --> R[Dynamic Characteristics]
    P --> S[Error Characteristics]
    Q --> Q1[Accuracy]
    Q --> Q2[Precision]
    Q --> Q3[Resolution]
    Q --> Q4[Sensitivity]
    Q --> Q5[Linearity]
    Q --> Q6[Range]
    R --> R1[Rise Time]
    R --> R2[Settling Time]
    R --> R3[Bandwidth]
    R --> R4[Phase Lag]
    S --> S1[Systematic Error Bias]
    S --> S2[Random Error Noise]
    S --> S3[Drift]
    S --> S4[Hysteresis]
    S --> S5[Cross Sensitivity]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: First-Order Sensor Dynamic Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    In[Input x of t] --> Sum[Summer Junction]
    Noise[Noise n of t] --> Sum
    Sum --> TF[Transfer Function H of s equals 1 over 1 plustau s]
    TF --> Out[Output y of t]
    TF --> Feedback[Feedback Loop unity]
    Feedback --> Sum
```

**Explanation:** The block diagram represents the standard **first-order sensor model** used in KTU 2024 module problems. The differential equation governing the output is:

$$\tau \frac{dy(t)}{dt} + y(t) = x(t) + n(t)$$

The corresponding frequency response is:

$$H(j\omega) = \frac{1}{1 + j\omega\tau}$$

giving a magnitude response:

$$\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega\tau)^2}}$$

The bandwidth is the frequency at which this magnitude falls to $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$, i.e., where $\omega \tau = 1$, yielding $BW = \frac{1}{2\pi \tau}$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define sensor accuracy and precision. How are they different? Give an example where a sensor can be precise but not accurate.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Accuracy** is the degree of closeness of a sensor's measured value to the true value of the quantity being measured (1 mark).
- **Precision** is the degree to which a sensor gives the same reading when the measurement is repeated under identical conditions (1 mark).
- **Example:** A miscalibrated digital thermometer consistently reading 102°C in a 100°C water bath is **precise** (low scatter) but **not accurate** (biased high) (1 mark).

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**What is the resolution of a 10-bit rotary encoder used for angular position measurement? Express your answer in degrees.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- Formula: $\Delta \theta = \dfrac{360°}{2^{10} - 1} = \dfrac{360°}{1023}$ (1 mark).
- $\Delta \theta \approx 0.3519°$ per count (1 mark).
- Hence, the resolution is approximately **0.352° per LSB** (1 mark).

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

**Q3(A).** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]`

**(a)** With the aid of a labelled block diagram, explain the **static and dynamic characteristics** used to characterize a sensor's performance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A pressure sensor has a calibration equation $V = 0.05 P + 0.20$, where $V$ is in volts and $P$ is in kPa. The full-scale range is $0$ to $100$ kPa and the output is digitized by a 10-bit ADC over $0$–$5$ V. Compute: (i) the **sensitivity**, (ii) the **resolution in kPa**, and (iii) the **resolution in mV**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Static and Dynamic Characteristics (7 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Concept — Static vs. Dynamic**
> - **Static characteristics** describe sensor behaviour when the input is constant or varies very slowly. They include: accuracy, precision, resolution, sensitivity, linearity, range, and hysteresis.
> - **Dynamic characteristics** describe sensor behaviour when the input changes rapidly with time. They include: rise time, settling time, bandwidth, phase lag, and overshoot.

- **Static Characteristics Explanation (4 marks):**
  - *Accuracy*: Closeness to true value. Quantified by absolute or percentage error.
  - *Precision / Repeatability*: Reproducibility of readings; measured by standard deviation $\sigma$.
  - *Resolution*: Smallest detectable input increment.
  - *Sensitivity*: Slope of the transfer characteristic $S = dy/dx$.
  - *Linearity*: Deviation of output from the best-fit straight line (in % FSO).
  - *Hysteresis*: Difference in output for ascending vs. descending input sweeps.

- **Dynamic Characteristics Explanation (3 marks):**
  - *Rise Time $t_r$*: Time for the output to change from 10% to 90% of the final steady-state value.
  - *Settling Time $t_s$*: Time for the output to remain within a specified tolerance (typically $\pm 2\%$ or $\pm 5\%$) of the final value.
  - *Bandwidth $BW$*: Frequency range over which the sensor output faithfully tracks the input; for a first-order system, $BW = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\tau}$.

**(b) Pressure Sensor Calculations (7 marks):**

**(i) Sensitivity (2 marks):**

The calibration equation is linear: $V = 0.05 P + 0.20$. The slope is the sensitivity.

$$\boxed{S = 0.05 \text{ V/kPa} = 50 \text{ mV/kPa}}$$

**(ii) Resolution in kPa (3 marks):**

ADC has $N = 10$ bits, full-scale voltage $V_{FS} = 5$ V.

$$\Delta V_{LSB} = \frac{5 \text{ V}}{2^{10} - 1} = \frac{5}{1023} = 4.887 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V} = 4.887 \text{ mV}$$

Pressure resolution using $S = 50$ mV/kPa:

$$\Delta P_{min} = \frac{\Delta V_{LSB}}{S} = \frac{4.887 \text{ mV}}{50 \text{ mV/kPa}}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta P_{min} = 0.0977 \text{ kPa} \approx 97.7 \text{ Pa}}$$

**(iii) Resolution in mV (2 marks):**

$$\boxed{\Delta V_{LSB} = 4.887 \text{ mV}}$$

---

**Q3(B).** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** What is **SNR** in sensor characterization? A sensor produces a useful signal of $50$ mV amplitude and a noise RMS of $0.5$ mV. Compute the **SNR in dB** and comment on whether the signal is acceptable for a robotics application. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define **hysteresis** and **drift** in sensors. A temperature sensor has the following readings on a heating and cooling cycle through the same set-points. Compute the **maximum hysteresis error** in °C.

| Set Point (°C) | Heating Reading (°C) | Cooling Reading (°C) |
|---|---|---|
| 30 | 30.2 | 29.7 |
| 50 | 50.4 | 49.6 |
| 70 | 70.5 | 69.4 |
| 90 | 90.3 | 89.5 |

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Signal-to-Noise Ratio (7 marks):**

- **Definition (2 marks):** SNR is the ratio of the power (or squared amplitude) of the desired signal to that of the background noise. It quantifies how cleanly a sensor can extract useful information.

- **Formula (1 mark):** $SNR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}}\right)$ (using voltage amplitudes).

- **Calculation (2 marks):**

$$SNR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{50 \text{ mV}}{0.5 \text{ mV}}\right) = 20 \log_{10}(100) = 20 \times 2 = 40 \text{ dB}$$

$$\boxed{SNR = 40 \text{ dB}}$$

- **Comment on Acceptability (2 marks):** A $40$ dB SNR is generally **acceptable for mobile robotics** applications such as obstacle detection, odometry, and basic SLAM. However, for safety-critical applications (e.g., autonomous vehicle pedestrian detection) where the minimum acceptable SNR is typically $50$–$60$ dB, this sensor would be considered **marginal** and would require either signal amplification, filtering (e.g., Kalman filter), or replacement with a lower-noise sensor.

**(b) Hysteresis and Drift (7 marks):**

- **Hysteresis Definition (2 marks):** Hysteresis is the maximum difference between the sensor outputs obtained during an ascending (increasing) input sweep and a descending (decreasing) input sweep at the **same input value**. It is caused by internal energy-storing mechanisms (magnetic remanence, mechanical friction, charge trapping, etc.).

- **Drift Definition (1 mark):** Drift is the slow, undesirable change in sensor output with time (or with environmental factors like temperature) while the input remains constant.

- **Computation of Maximum Hysteresis (4 marks):**

$$\varepsilon_{hys}(30) = \vert 30.2 - 29.7 \vert = 0.5°C$$

$$\varepsilon_{hys}(50) = \vert 50.4 - 49.6 \vert = 0.8°C$$

$$\varepsilon_{hys}(70) = \vert 70.5 - 69.4 \vert = 1.1°C$$

$$\varepsilon_{hys}(90) = \vert 90.3 - 89.5 \vert = 0.8°C$$

Maximum value: $1.1°C$ at the $70°C$ set-point.

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_{hys,\,max} = 1.1°C}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes Students Make**
> 1. **Confusing accuracy with precision** — These are independent concepts; high precision does NOT imply high accuracy. Examiners frequently award 0 marks for answers that equate them.
> 2. **Using $2^N$ instead of $2^N - 1$** in resolution calculations — The number of *intervals* is $2^N - 1$, not $2^N$. Marks are deducted for this subtle error.
> 3. **Forgetting to convert degrees to radians** in the encoder resolution derivation — failing to convert $\Delta\theta$ to radians before multiplying by radius $r$ leads to dimensionally and numerically wrong answers (factor of $\pi/180$).
> 4. **Computing SNR using $10 \log$ when given voltage amplitudes** — The correct formula is $20 \log$ for amplitudes and $10 \log$ for *powers*. Examiners will deduct a full mark.
> 5. **Not specifying the units of hysteresis** — Always quote the hysteresis in the same units as the sensor output, and explicitly state the **input set-point** at which the maximum occurs.
> 6. **Omitting FSO** in the non-linearity formula — The denominator must be the full-scale output (or full-scale input), not just the local output value. Many students write $\varepsilon_{NL} = \Delta V / V$ which is dimensionally inconsistent and loses marks.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Accuracy** = closeness to *truth*; **Precision** = closeness of *repeats* to each other. They are independent.
- **Resolution** of a digital sensor = full-scale range / $(2^N - 1)$. Always subtract 1.
- **Sensitivity** = slope of the calibration curve; has units of *output per input*.
- **Linearity error** is always reported in **% FSO**, not in absolute units.
- **Hysteresis** = difference between up-going and down-going curves; depends on the **set-point** at which it is evaluated.
- **Bandwidth** of a first-order sensor = $1 / (2\pi \tau)$; rise time $\approx 2.2 \tau$.
- **SNR (dB)** = $20 \log_{10}(V_s / V_n)$ for amplitudes; $10 \log_{10}(P_s / P_n)$ for powers.
- **Drift** = time-domain error; **Bias** = constant offset; **Noise** = random fluctuation.
- A sensor with high resolution and high sensitivity but poor linearity still needs calibration.
- For KTU lab work, always tabulate calibration data, plot the transfer curve, and report the best-fit equation with $R^2$ value.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wheel/Motor Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAET744 - MOBILE ROBOTICS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Wheel/Motor Sensors ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **Wheel/Motor Sensors (Mobile Robotics – KTU PEAET744)** > Wheel and motor sensors are proprioceptive transducers mounted on a mobile robot's drivetrain (wheels, gearboxes, or motor shafts) that convert **rotational motion** (angle, angular velocity, angular acceleration) into quantifiable **electrical signals** for real-time odometry, dead-reckoning, traction control, slip detection, and closed-loop motor commutation. They form the lowest tier of the mobile robot sensing stack and directly inform the kinematic/dynamic model used by higher-level planners and controllers. In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, this topic lies under the **Perception for Mobile Robots** thread of Module 2, which also typically covers laser/sonar and vision. Wheel/Motor sensors are *proprioceptive* (they measure internal states of the robot) rather than *exteroceptive* (measuring the external environment). ### 1.2 Intuitive Overview – "The Odometer Analogy" > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Analogy:** Think of a bicycle cyclocomputer (the small digital device on the handlebar). > - A magnet on the spoke passes a reed/Hall switch on the fork once per revolution. > - The cyclocomputer counts pulses → computes wheel angular velocity → multiplies by wheel circumference → displays linear distance/speed. > - Your mobile robot's wheel sensors do **exactly the same thing**, but with much higher resolution (often **hundreds to thousands of pulses per revolution**) and fed into a microcontroller instead of an LCD. In essence, **wheel/motor sensors answer one fundamental question**: *How much has each wheel turned, and how fast is it turning right now?* ### 1.3 Classification of Wheel/Motor Sensors The KTU syllabus recognizes four broad families. Each is tied to a different physical principle: | Family | Physical Principle | Typical Output | |---|---|---| | **Optical Encoders** | Light beam interruption by a slotted disk | Quadrature digital pulses (A, B, I) | | **Magnetic Encoders** | Hall effect / magnetoresistive sensing of pole pairs | Digital pulses or analog sine/cosine | | **Back-EMF / Voltage Sensors** | Faraday's law on motor terminals | Analog voltage proportional to $\omega$ | | **Current / Resolver Sensors** | Shunt / inductive coupling / Rogowski coil | Analog current or resolver waveforms | ### 1.4 Why Wheel/Motor Sensors Are Critical in Mobile Robotics - **Odometry**: Integration of wheel rotation gives position estimate in world frame. - **Velocity feedback**: Required for PI/PID speed control of each drive wheel. - **Traction control**: Differential wheel speeds indicate slip on uneven terrain. - **Dead reckoning**: When GPS or vision is unavailable (e.g., tunnels, indoor), wheel sensors are the *only* pose source. - **Motor protection**: Current sensing detects stalls and overloads. > [!NOTE] > **Standard Metric Highlighted in KTU Papers:** > Resolution of an encoder is often expressed in **CPR (Counts Per Revolution)** or **PPR (Pulses Per Revolution)** or **LPR (Lines Per Revolution)**. Quadrature decoding gives **4×** the native line count. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Optical Shaft Encoders – The Workhorse of Mobile Robotics #### 2.1.1 Construction A transparent disk (glass or plastic) is patterned with alternating **opaque** and **transparent radial lines**. The disk is rigidly coupled to the motor shaft or wheel shaft. A light source (LED) is placed on one side, and a photodiode/phototransistor is on the other. As the disk rotates, the light beam is **chopped**, producing a square wave at the photodetector output. There are two main flavors: 1. **Incremental Encoder** – produces A and B channels plus an Index (Z) pulse once per revolution. 2. **Absolute Encoder** – has $n$ tracks producing a unique Gray/binary word for every angular position (typically 8–16 bits). #### 2.1.2 Quadrature Decoding (Channels A and B) A single slot would only give a pulse train with **no direction information**. By adding a **second channel offset by 90° (π/2 rad)**, we obtain **quadrature**: - If A leads B → shaft is rotating **clockwise**. - If B leads A → shaft is rotating **counter-clockwise**. - Counting both edges of A and B gives **4×** resolution = 4× decoding (a.k.a. **4X quadrature**). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Favourite Concept:** Students are regularly asked to derive resolution from quadrature decoding. The expected answer format is: > $$N_{counts/rev} = 4 \times L$$ > where $L$ is the native number of lines/slots on the disk. ### 2.2 Magnetic Encoders and Hall-Effect Sensors #### 2.2.1 Hall Effect Sensors When a current-carrying semiconductor is placed in a magnetic field perpendicular to current flow, the **Lorentz force** deflects charge carriers, producing a measurable Hall voltage across the conductor. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Hall Voltage Equation (Standard form):** > $$V_H = \frac{R_H \cdot I \cdot B}{t}$$ > where $R_H$ is the Hall coefficient, $I$ is bias current, $B$ is magnetic flux density, and $t$ is the thickness of the sensing element. In a wheel/motor application, a **ring magnet** with alternating N/S poles is mounted on the shaft, and $p$ Hall sensors (usually $p=2$ or $p=3$) detect pole transitions. With 2 sensors offset by 90° electrical, you obtain quadrature-like signals **without any optics** — robust against dust, oil, and vibration. #### 2.2.2 AMR / GMR / TMR Encoders Anisotropic Magnetoresistive (AMR), Giant Magnetoresistive (GMR), and Tunnel Magnetoresistive (TMR) sensors offer **sub-degree angular resolution** by reading a multipole magnetic ring. They are increasingly used in BLDC motor commutation inside mobile robots. ### 2.3 Back-EMF (Electro-Motional Force) Sensing For a **DC or brushless DC (BLDC) motor**, the rotating armature induces a voltage opposite to the applied terminal voltage — this is the **back-EMF** $e_b$. By Kirchhoff's voltage law: > [!IMPORTANT] > **DC Motor Back-EMF Equation (KTU-High-Yield):** > $$V_a = I_a R_a + L_a \frac{dI_a}{dt} + e_b$$ > where $V_a$ is armature voltage, $I_a$ is armature current, $R_a$ is armature resistance, $L_a$ is armature inductance, and $e_b$ is back-EMF.
