> 5. **Ignoring the AOA singularity** — if the robot is collinear with the two beacons, AOA breaks down.
> 6. **Wrong sign convention** in TDOA: always $\Delta t = t_i - t_j$ consistently, otherwise the hyperbola branch is flipped.
> 7. **Forgetting to verify** the solution by substituting the computed $(x, y)$ back into one of the original range equations.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Ground-Based Beacons** are fixed reference transmitters at surveyed coordinates used for active localization.
- **Four measurement techniques:** TOA (range), TDOA (range difference), AOA (bearing), RSS (signal strength).
- **TOA** requires robot + beacon synchronization; **TDOA** requires only beacon-to-beacon sync; **AOA** requires no sync but needs directional sensors.
- **2D trilateration** requires a **minimum of 3** non-collinear beacons; **3D trilateration** requires **4** non-coplanar beacons.
- **2D AOA** requires only **2** beacons; **3D AOA** requires **3**.
- The **linearized trilateration** matrix form is $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{p} = \mathbf{b}$, with solution $\mathbf{p} = (\mathbf{A}^T \mathbf{A})^{-1} \mathbf{A}^T \mathbf{b}$ for over-determined systems.
- The **AOA singularity** occurs when the robot lies on the baseline between two beacons ($\theta_2 = \theta_1$).
- **Speed of sound** ($v_s = 343$ m/s) makes ultrasonic beacons tolerant of cheap clocks but limits their update rate; **speed of light** ($c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s) makes RF/laser systems fast but demands nanosecond-level clocking.
- **Multipath and NLOS** (non-line-of-sight) are the dominant sources of error in indoor beacon systems — never assume a clean line of sight in KTU design problems.
- **Always verify** the final $(x, y)$ by substitution into the original range equation.
- **Modular Answer Structure for 7-Mark Sub-Questions:** State setup → Show equations → Linearize → Solve matrix → Verify → State result. Examiners reward this structure.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Active Ranging
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET744 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Active Ranging

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Active Ranging** in mobile robotics is the process of *actively* projecting a known form of energy (acoustic, optical, or electromagnetic) into an environment and measuring the properties of the reflected/returned signal to determine the **distance, bearing, and geometric profile** of objects surrounding the robot.

Unlike *passive* ranging (e.g., vision cameras that merely capture ambient light), active ranging sensors **inject energy** into the scene. By precisely controlling the emission timing, wavelength, and modulation, the robot can extract highly accurate metric information (range $\rho$, azimuth $\theta$, elevation $\phi$) of obstacles, landmarks, and terrain.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> The four pillars of active ranging studied in Module 2 are:
> 1. **Ultrasonic (SONAR / Time-of-Flight acoustic)** ranging
> 2. **Laser Range Finder (LIDAR)** — based on optical Time-of-Flight
> 3. **Structured Light** triangulation
> 4. **Optical Triangulation (1-D and 2-D)**

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are standing in a dark cave and you shout "HELLO!". By listening carefully to the **echo that comes back**, you can estimate how far the cave wall is from you. The longer it takes for the echo to return, the farther the wall.

This is exactly what an **ultrasonic sensor** does, but with sound waves too high-pitched for human ears (typically **40 kHz** to **250 kHz**).

Now, instead of a shout, imagine you shine a **laser pointer** at a wall and a super-fast stopwatch measures the time it takes for the photon to bounce back. Since light travels at roughly **$3 \times 10^8$ m/s**, even small distances produce tiny delays — but measurable ones. This is **LIDAR** (Light Detection and Ranging).

Finally, imagine projecting a **straight line of light** (a laser stripe) onto a surface. The stripe bends inward when the surface is close and outward when far. By viewing this stripe from a *second, offset camera*, you can compute the 3-D shape of the object. This is **structured-light triangulation**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Active"?** Because the sensor is the *source* of the probing signal. The robot "paints" the environment with its own energy and listens for the answer.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Value |
|---|---|---|
| Speed of Light in vacuum | $c$ | $2.998 \times 10^8$ m/s |
| Speed of Sound in air (20°C) | $v_s$ | $\approx 343$ m/s |
| Acoustic wavelength of 50 kHz ultrasound | $\lambda$ | $\approx 6.86$ mm |
| Standard laser wavelength (near-IR) | $\lambda_L$ | $905$ nm or $1550$ nm |
| One-way light travel for 1 m | $\Delta t$ | $\approx 3.33$ ns |
| One-way sound travel for 1 m | $\Delta t$ | $\approx 2.91$ ms |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Physical Principles of Active Ranging

Active ranging sensors operate on one of three fundamental physical principles:

### A. Time-of-Flight (ToF)
- A pulse (or modulated wave) is emitted and the **round-trip travel time** $T$ is measured.
- Range is computed from $R = v \cdot T / 2$, where $v$ is the propagation velocity of the medium.
- **Why?** Because both light and sound travel at *known, fixed* speeds. Measuring time is therefore equivalent to measuring distance.
- **Best for:** Long ranges (meters to hundreds of meters), 3-D scanning.
- **Used in:** SONAR, Laser Rangefinder, Radar.

### B. Triangulation (Geometric)
- A beam is projected from a known **baseline** onto a target; the reflected spot is imaged by a sensor offset by a known **baseline distance $b$**.
- The geometry of the triangle formed by *projector–target–sensor* yields the range.
- **Why?** Because a closer object shifts the spot on the imaging sensor by a *larger* angle than a distant one. The baseline converts this angular shift into a measurable range.
- **Best for:** Short ranges (mm to few meters), high accuracy.
- **Used in:** Structured light, 1-D laser triangulation probes.

### C. Phase-Shift Detection
- A **continuously modulated** wave (usually amplitude-modulated light) is emitted; the **phase difference** $\Delta\phi$ between emitted and returned signal is measured.
- Range: $R = \frac{c \cdot \Delta\phi}{4 \pi f_m}$, where $f_m$ is the modulation frequency.
- **Why?** Phase can be measured with high precision even when the time delay is too small to clock directly.
- **Best for:** Mid-range, high accuracy (Kinect v1 used this principle).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below is the **exam-essentials** formula bank. All numericals in Section 3 derive from these.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Domain / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R = \dfrac{v \cdot T}{2}$ | Range from round-trip time-of-flight | SONAR, LIDAR; $v$ in m/s, $T$ in s, $R$ in m |
| 2 | $\Delta R = \dfrac{v}{2} \cdot \Delta T$ | Range resolution from time resolution $\Delta T$ | $v$ = medium velocity |
| 3 | $f_{Doppler} = \dfrac{2 v_r f_0}{c}$ | Doppler shift for moving target (speed $v_r$) | Used in FMCW radar |
| 4 | $R = \dfrac{\lambda}{4 \pi} \cdot \Delta\phi$ | Range from phase difference (modulation) | $\Delta\phi$ in radians |
| 5 | $R = \dfrac{b \cdot f}{x}$ | Triangulation range, baseline $b$, focal $f$, image displacement $x$ | Optical triangulation |
| 6 | $P_{rec} = \dfrac{P_{tx} \cdot G_t \cdot G_r \cdot \lambda^2 \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 R^4}$ | Radar / LIDAR range equation | $P$ in W, $\sigma$ = cross-section (m²) |
| 7 | $I_{rec} = \dfrac{I_0 \cos\theta_i \cdot A}{(R + d/\cos\theta_r)^2}$ | Structured light intensity received | Photogrammetry |
| 8 | $\theta_{div} = \dfrac{1.22 \lambda}{D}$ | Beam divergence (Airy disk) | $D$ = aperture diameter |
| 9 | $BW = \dfrac{v}{\Delta f_{chirp}}$ | Bandwidth of chirp for resolution | FMCW |
| 10 | $\text{Beam Radius}(z) = w_0 \sqrt{1 + \left(\dfrac{\lambda z}{\pi w_0^2}\right)^2}$ | Gaussian beam propagation | Laser optics |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Autonomous Vehicles (Waymo, Tesla, Mobileye):** Multi-beam LIDAR sweeps a 360° horizontal field-of-view at 10–20 Hz, producing dense 3-D point clouds used for obstacle detection and SLAM.
- **Warehouse AGVs (Amazon Kiva, KUKA):** 2-D safety LIDARs (e.g., SICK LMS) detect humans and obstacles within a 5–30 m safety zone.
- **Service Robots (iRobot, Softbank Pepper):** SONAR arrays provide low-cost proximity sensing on the robot's bumper skirt.
- **Industrial Metrology:** Laser triangulation probes inspect manufactured parts with **$\pm 5 \,\mu m$** repeatability.
- **Underwater ROVs/AUVs:** SONAR (often 200–700 kHz) is the only practical active ranging modality, since light attenuates rapidly in water.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering trade-off:** Active ranging sensors are *active*, meaning they consume power, can interfere with each other (cross-talk in dense robot swarms), and are detectable (a LIDAR pulse is visible to enemy IR seekers — relevant in defense robotics).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Range from Time-of-Flight (ToF)

### Statement
A robot emits a short pulse of light (or sound) that travels to a target at range $R$, reflects, and returns. Show that the measured range is $R = v T / 2$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

Let $T$ be the **round-trip travel time** of the pulse. The pulse covers a *total* path length of *outward + return*:

$$
L_{total} = R + R = 2R
$$

Since wavefronts travel at constant velocity $v$ in the medium (vacuum for laser, air for sound):

$$
v = \frac{L_{total}}{T} = \frac{2R}{T}
$$

Rearranging for $R$:

$$
R = \frac{v T}{2}
$$

This is the **fundamental ranging equation**.

### Worked Numerical — SONAR
An ultrasonic sensor fires a 40 kHz pulse and the echo returns in **$T = 6$ ms**. Compute the range. Take $v_s = 343$ m/s (air at 20°C).

$$
R = \frac{343 \times 6 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = \frac{2.058}{2} = 1.029 \text{ m}
$$

### Worked Numerical — LIDAR
A 905 nm laser pulse travels to a wall and returns in **$T = 33.4$ ns**. Compute the range. Take $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s.

$$
R = \frac{3 \times 10^8 \times 33.4 \times 10^{-9}}{2} = \frac{10.02}{2} = 5.01 \text{ m}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The order-of-magnitude difference ($10^{-3}$ vs $10^{-9}$ seconds) is precisely why LIDAR electronics are far more expensive than SONAR electronics — they must time nanosecond intervals with sub-nanosecond jitter.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Range Resolution from Time Resolution

### Statement
If the timing electronics can resolve a minimum time interval $\Delta T$, what is the corresponding range resolution $\Delta R$?

### Derivation

Starting from $R = vT / 2$, differentiate both sides with respect to $T$:

$$
\frac{dR}{dT} = \frac{v}{2}
$$

Hence the *minimum distinguishable change in range* is:

$$
\Delta R = \frac{v}{2} \cdot \Delta T
$$

### Application Examples
- **SONAR** with $\Delta T = 100 \, \mu s$ timing resolution: $\Delta R = (343/2)(100\times 10^{-6}) = 1.715$ cm.
- **LIDAR** with $\Delta T = 1$ ns: $\Delta R = (3\times 10^8 / 2)(1\times 10^{-9}) = 0.15$ m = **15 cm**.
- **High-end LIDAR** with $\Delta T = 50$ ps: $\Delta R = 7.5$ mm.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cross-Check:** LIDAR therefore needs picosecond-class TDCs (Time-to-Digital Converters) to achieve centimeter accuracy. This is why automotive LIDAR units cost thousands of dollars.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Laser Triangulation Range

### Statement
A laser beam is projected from point $P$ onto a target surface. An imaging sensor, displaced by a *baseline* $b$ from the projector, views the laser spot through a lens of focal length $f$. If the spot appears at image-plane displacement $x$ from the optical axis, show that the range to the target is $R = b f / x$.

### Derivation (Geometric — Similar Triangles)

Let the lens be at origin $O$, the projector be offset by baseline $b$ to the side, and the target be at distance $R$ in front of the lens. The reflected ray from the laser spot enters the lens at angle $\alpha$ and is focused onto the image plane at displacement $x$.

From the **geometry of the imaging lens** (thin-lens relation):

$$
\tan \alpha = \frac{x}{f}
$$

From the **triangle** formed by projector, target, and lens:

$$
\tan \alpha = \frac{b}{R}
$$

Equating the two expressions for $\tan \alpha$:

$$
\frac{x}{f} = \frac{b}{R}
$$

Solving for $R$:

$$
R = \frac{b f}{x}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is a beautiful example of *similar triangles* — the foundation of photogrammetry. Note the **inverse relationship**: closer targets produce *larger* $x$, farther targets produce *smaller* $x$.

### Worked Numerical
A triangulation sensor has baseline $b = 80$ mm, focal length $f = 20$ mm, and the spot is observed at $x = 0.5$ mm from the axis. Compute the range.

$$
R = \frac{(80 \times 10^{-3})(20 \times 10^{-3})}{0.5 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{1.6 \times 10^{-3}}{0.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 3.2 \text{ m}
$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Phase-Shift Ranging

### Statement
A light source is amplitude-modulated at frequency $f_m$. The reflected wave returns with a phase lag $\Delta\phi$ relative to the emitted wave. Derive the range equation.

### Derivation

The emitted wave is $E_{tx}(t) = A \cos(2\pi f_m t)$. After reflection from a target at range $R$, the wave arrives with delay $\tau = 2R/c$:

$$
E_{rx}(t) = A' \cos(2\pi f_m (t - \tau)) = A' \cos(2\pi f_m t - 2\pi f_m \tau)
$$

The phase lag is therefore:

$$
\Delta\phi = 2\pi f_m \tau = 2\pi f_m \cdot \frac{2R}{c} = \frac{4\pi f_m R}{c}
$$

Solving for $R$:

$$
R = \frac{c \cdot \Delta\phi}{4\pi f_m}
$$

### Worked Numerical
A modulated LIDAR has $f_m = 20$ MHz. The measured phase lag is $\Delta\phi = \pi/2$ rad. Compute the range.

$$
R = \frac{(3 \times 10^8)(\pi/2)}{4\pi (20 \times 10^6)} = \frac{3 \times 10^8 \times 0.5}{8 \times 10^7} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^8}{8 \times 10^7} = 1.875 \text{ m}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The maximum unambiguous range is achieved when $\Delta\phi = 2\pi$, giving $R_{max} = c/(2 f_m) = 7.5$ m for $f_m = 20$ MHz. Beyond this, the phase "wraps around" and produces aliasing.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Active Ranging Modalities

| Parameter | SONAR | 1-D Laser Triangulation | Pulsed LIDAR | Phase-Modulated LIDAR | Structured Light |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Range | 0.1–10 m | 0.05–1 m | 0.5–200 m | 0.1–50 m | 0.2–5 m |
| Accuracy | $\pm 1$ cm | $\pm 5 \,\mu m$ | $\pm 2$ cm | $\pm 1$ mm | $\pm 0.1$ mm |
| Beam divergence | $\approx 15°$–$30°$ | $< 1$ mrad | 0.5–3 mrad | 0.5–3 mrad | Projector FOV |
| Cost (USD) | $5 – $50 | $100 – $5,000 | $1,000 – $75,000 | $500 – $10,000 | $20 – $200 |
| Update rate | 10–50 Hz | 1–10 kHz | 5–100 kHz | 1–50 kHz | 30–60 Hz |
| Susceptibility to interference | High (cross-talk) | Low | Low | Low | Medium (ambient light) |
| Multi-path errors | Severe | Moderate | Low | Low | Moderate |
| Operating medium | Air, water | Air, opaque surfaces | Air | Air | Air |
| Speed of medium dependence | High (v_s ∝ T) | None | None | None | None |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For exam answers:** SONAR is *cheap but coarse*; LIDAR is *expensive but precise*; triangulation is *precise at short range*; structured-light is *excellent for 3-D scanning*. Pick the modality based on the required range, accuracy, and operating environment.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
