# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
**Q: Differentiate between active and passive ranging in mobile robotics. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Active Ranging | Passive Ranging |
|---|---|---|
| Energy source | Sensor emits its own energy | Sensor only receives ambient/scene energy |
| Operating in darkness | Yes (sensor provides illumination) | No (requires external illumination) |
| Computational load | Low (timing/geometry) | High (image processing, feature extraction) |
| Examples | SONAR, LIDAR, structured light | Stereo camera, monocular vision, depth-from-focus |

> **[Mark distribution]:** 1 mark for definition contrast, 1 mark for the operational table, 1 mark for examples.

---

### Question A2
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
**Q: An ultrasonic sensor in a mobile robot measures a round-trip time of 8 ms to a wall. Calculate the distance to the wall. Take speed of sound = 343 m/s.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Given: $T = 8$ ms $= 8 \times 10^{-3}$ s, $v_s = 343$ m/s

$$
R = \frac{v_s T}{2} = \frac{343 \times 8 \times 10^{-3}}{2}
$$

$$
R = \frac{2.744}{2} = 1.372 \text{ m}
$$

**∴ The wall is 1.372 m from the robot.** ✓ (1.5 marks for equation, 1 mark for substitution, 0.5 mark for unit)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Paper]`**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the working principle of a **laser triangulation sensor** used in mobile robotics. Derive the range equation and discuss the effect of baseline distance $b$ and focal length $f$ on the measurement range and accuracy.

**(b) [7 marks]** A triangulation sensor has baseline $b = 100$ mm, focal length $f = 25$ mm, and uses a 1/3-inch CMOS sensor with $7.4 \,\mu m$ pixel pitch. The image-processing unit can locate the centroid of the laser spot to **sub-pixel accuracy of $\pm 0.1$ pixel**. Compute:
- (i) the **range** when the spot is at $x = 1.0$ mm displacement from the optical axis,
- (ii) the **range resolution** at $R = 1$ m,
- (iii) the **maximum unambiguous range** before the spot leaves the sensor (sensor width = 6.4 mm).

---

### **Model Solution to Question A**

#### Part (a) — Principle & Derivation [7 marks]

A laser triangulation sensor consists of three functional units:

1. **A laser projector** that emits a focused beam onto the target.
2. **An imaging lens** offset from the projector by a fixed **baseline $b$**.
3. **A position-sensitive detector (PSD) or CMOS linear array** that records the position of the reflected spot.

When the target is at range $R$, the spot appears on the detector at displacement $x$ from the optical axis. By similar triangles (lens geometry + projector-target-lens geometry):

$$
\tan \alpha = \frac{x}{f} \quad \text{and} \quad \tan \alpha = \frac{b}{R}
$$

Equating and solving:

$$
R = \frac{b f}{x}
$$

**Effect of parameters:**

- **Larger baseline $b$** → larger $x$ for same $R$ → **better resolution** but introduces **shadowing/occlusion** at very close range.
- **Larger focal length $f$** → larger $x$ for same $R$ → **better resolution**, but **smaller depth of field** and larger sensor footprint.
- The sensor exhibits an **inverse relationship**: $R \propto 1/x$, so accuracy is *worst at long range* and *best at short range*.

> **[Mark distribution]:** 1 mark diagram, 2 marks derivation, 2 marks effect of $b$, 2 marks effect of $f$.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution [7 marks]

**Given:** $b = 100$ mm, $f = 25$ mm, pixel pitch $p = 7.4 \,\mu m$, sub-pixel accuracy $\delta = 0.1 \text{ pixel} = 0.74 \,\mu m$, sensor width $W = 6.4$ mm.

**(i) Range at $x = 1.0$ mm:**

$$
R = \frac{b f}{x} = \frac{(100)(25)}{1.0} = 2500 \text{ mm} = 2.5 \text{ m}
$$

**(ii) Range resolution at $R = 1$ m:**

First compute the spot displacement at $R = 1$ m:

$$
x = \frac{b f}{R} = \frac{(100)(25)}{1000} = 2.5 \text{ mm} = 2500 \,\mu m
$$

Range resolution from differentiating $R = b f / x$:

$$
\frac{dR}{dx} = -\frac{b f}{x^2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \vert \Delta R \vert = \frac{b f}{x^2} \cdot \Delta x
$$

With $\Delta x = \delta = 0.74 \,\mu m$:

$$
\Delta R = \frac{(100)(25)}{(2.5)^2} \times 0.74 \times 10^{-3} = 400 \times 0.74 \times 10^{-3} = 0.296 \text{ mm}
$$

**∴ Range resolution $\approx 0.3$ mm at 1 m range.** ✓ (3.5 marks)

**(iii) Maximum unambiguous range:**

The maximum displacement the sensor can record is half its width: $x_{max} = W/2 = 3.2$ mm.

$$
R_{max} = \frac{b f}{x_{max}} = \frac{(100)(25)}{3.2} \approx 781.25 \text{ mm} \approx 0.78 \text{ m}
$$

**∴ Maximum unambiguous range $\approx 0.78$ m.** ✓ (3.5 marks)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **pulsed LIDAR** system. Derive the range equation and discuss the role of the **constant-fraction discriminator (CFD)** and **time-to-digital converter (TDC)** in achieving sub-nanosecond timing accuracy.

**(b) [7 marks]** A 905 nm pulsed LIDAR system has the following specifications:
- Pulse energy $E_p = 1 \,\mu J$
- Beam divergence $\theta = 1$ mrad
- Target reflectivity $\rho = 0.2$ (Lambertian, 20% albedo)
- Receiver lens diameter $D = 25$ mm
- Detector responsivity $R_d = 0.6$ A/W
- Range to target $R = 50$ m

Compute:
- (i) the **spot size** on the target,
- (ii) the **received optical power** at the detector,
- (iii) the **number of photons** in the received pulse (Photon energy at 905 nm = $2.19 \times 10^{-19}$ J).

---

### **Model Solution to Question B**

#### Part (a) — Pulsed LIDAR Working & Derivation [7 marks]

A pulsed LIDAR (Light Detection And Ranging) operates in five stages:

1. **Trigger** — The MCU generates a start pulse.
2. **Emission** — A laser diode (typically 905 nm or 1550 nm) emits a short (~1–10 ns) optical pulse.
3. **Propagation & Reflection** — The pulse travels to the target and a fraction of it is scattered back.
4. **Detection** — An avalanche photodiode (APD) converts the returning photons into a current pulse.
5. **Timing & Computation** — A TDC measures the elapsed time $T$ between emission and detection; the range is computed as $R = cT/2$.

**Derivation** (already covered in §3.1):

$$
R = \frac{cT}{2}
$$

**Role of the CFD:** A simple leading-edge threshold trigger produces *walk error* — pulse-amplitude-dependent timing shifts. The **constant-fraction discriminator** triggers at a fixed *fraction* (e.g., 50%) of the pulse peak, eliminating amplitude-induced timing jitter and achieving **< 100 ps** precision.

**Role of the TDC:** A time-to-digital converter digitizes the time interval $T$ with picosecond-level resolution. Modern TDCs use tapped delay lines or Vernier oscillators, providing 1-bit outputs at every few picoseconds.

> **[Mark distribution]:** 2 marks block diagram, 2 marks derivation, 1.5 marks CFD, 1.5 marks TDC.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution [7 marks]

**Given:** $E_p = 1 \times 10^{-6}$ J, $\theta = 10^{-3}$ rad, $\rho = 0.2$, $D = 25 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $R_d = 0.6$ A/W, $R = 50$ m.

**(i) Spot size on target:**

$$
d_{spot} = 2 R \tan(\theta/2) \approx R \theta = 50 \times 10^{-3} = 0.05 \text{ m} = 5 \text{ cm}
$$

**(ii) Received optical power:**

The transmitted pulse has peak power $P_{tx} = E_p / \Delta t$. Assume a typical pulse width $\Delta t = 5$ ns, so $P_{tx} = (1 \times 10^{-6}) / (5 \times 10^{-9}) = 200$ W.

The beam illuminates an area $A_{spot} = \pi d_{spot}^2 / 4 = \pi (0.05)^2 / 4 \approx 1.963 \times 10^{-3}$ m². The reflected intensity is Lambertian; the fraction intercepted by the receiver aperture of area $A_{rx} = \pi D^2 / 4 = \pi (0.025)^2 / 4 \approx 4.909 \times 10^{-4}$ m² is:

$$
\text{Fraction} = \frac{A_{rx}}{A_{spot}} \cdot \rho = \frac{4.909 \times 10^{-4}}{1.963 \times 10^{-3}} \times 0.2 = 0.25 \times 0.2 = 0.05
$$

(Note: a full $1/R^2$ Lambertian derivation gives an extra $(1/2)$ factor; we use the engineering approximation here.)

$$
P_{rx} = P_{tx} \times 0.05 = 200 \times 0.05 = 10 \text{ W} \text{ (peak)}
$$

**∴ Received peak power $\approx 10$ W.** ✓ (3 marks)

**(iii) Number of photons in the received pulse:**

Energy in received pulse:

$$
E_{rx} = P_{rx} \cdot \Delta t = 10 \times 5 \times 10^{-9} = 5 \times 10^{-8} \text{ J}
$$

Number of photons:

$$
N = \frac{E_{rx}}{E_{photon}} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-8}}{2.19 \times 10^{-19}} \approx 2.28 \times 10^{11}
$$

**∴ Approximately $2.3 \times 10^{11}$ photons are detected per pulse.** ✓ (4 marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in the ToF equation: students frequently write $R = cT$ instead of $R = cT/2$. This *immediately* loses 2 marks.
> 2. **Unit mismatch:** writing $T$ in ms when $c$ is in m/s. Always convert to **SI base units** (s, m) before plugging in.
> 3. **Confusing beam divergence with spot radius** — divergence is in radians, spot size is in meters.
> 4. **In triangulation problems**, forgetting that $R \propto 1/x$ — the relationship is *non-linear*; do not write $R \propto x$.
> 5. **Phase-shift problems:** writing $R = c\Delta\phi/(2\pi f_m)$ instead of $R = c\Delta\phi/(4\pi f_m)$. The extra factor of 2 is because the wave goes *out and back*.
> 6. **Confusing "range resolution" with "range accuracy"** — resolution is the smallest *detectable* change, accuracy is the closeness to *true* value (includes calibration errors).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Active Ranging**

### 📌 Core Definitions
- **Active ranging** = sensor *emits* energy, measures the return → *range* $R$.
- Three physical principles: **Time-of-Flight**, **Triangulation**, **Phase-Shift Detection**.
- Key sensors: **SONAR** (cheap, slow, sound-based), **LIDAR** (fast, precise, light-based), **Structured Light** (3-D scanning, short range).

### 📌 Master Equations
- **ToF range:** $R = vT/2$
- **Resolution:** $\Delta R = v\Delta T/2$
- **Triangulation:** $R = bf/x$
- **Phase-shift:** $R = c\Delta\phi/(4\pi f_m)$
- **LIDAR range eq.:** $P_{rx} = P_{tx} G_t G_r \lambda^2 \sigma / [(4\pi)^2 R^4]$ (Radar/LIDAR)
- **Beam divergence:** $\theta = 1.22 \lambda / D$

### 📌 Critical Numbers to Memorize
- $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s (speed of light)
- $v_s = 343$ m/s at 20°C (speed of sound)
- 1 m → 3.33 ns light round-trip → 6.67 ns; 1 m → 5.83 ms sound round-trip
- 905 nm and 1550 nm are the two standard eye-safe LIDAR wavelengths (1550 nm is preferred for automotive because it does not penetrate the retina).

### 📌 Engineering Trade-offs
| Parameter | Best Choice |
|---|---|
| Long range, mm accuracy | Pulsed LIDAR |
| Sub-mm accuracy at < 1 m | Laser triangulation |
| Underwater operation | SONAR (high-frequency acoustic) |
| Low cost, indoor obstacle | SONAR array |
| 3-D dense reconstruction | Structured light |
| Mid-range with high accuracy | Phase-shift LIDAR |

### 📌 Pitfalls to Avoid
- Always divide round-trip time by 2.
- Convert all units to SI *before* substituting.
- Range in triangulation is **inverse** in image displacement.
- Phase-shift equation has a factor of **4π**, not 2π.
- Speed of sound **varies with temperature**: $v_s \approx 331 + 0.6 T_{^\circ C}$ m/s.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Motion/Speed Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET744 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Motion/Speed Sensors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Motion/Speed Sensors** are a class of proprioceptive sensors used in mobile robotic systems to measure the **kinematic state** of the robot — specifically its linear velocity, angular velocity, rotational displacement, and rate of change of position. They convert mechanical motion (rotation or translation) into measurable electrical signals (pulses, voltage, or frequency) that are processed by the robot's microcontroller or embedded controller to estimate the robot's odometric state in real-time.

In the context of **mobile robotics**, motion/speed sensors form the foundation of **dead reckoning** and **odometry** — the technique by which a robot estimates its current pose (position and orientation) by integrating velocity measurements over time, without requiring external references.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Speedometer of a Car

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** Think of the speedometer needle in your car. When the wheels rotate, a sensor inside the gearbox counts those rotations and converts them into a speed reading on your dashboard. The car has no idea *where* it is on the map — it only knows *how fast* and *how far* it has moved from where it started. Mobile robot motion/speed sensors work on exactly the same principle: they are the **"speedometer"** of the robot.

**Key Insight:** Just as you can calculate how far you've traveled by knowing your car's speed and the duration of travel, a mobile robot uses motion sensors to integrate velocity over time and determine **how far it has moved** (odometry).

## 1.3 Classification of Motion/Speed Sensors

Motion/speed sensors used in mobile robots are broadly classified as follows:

| S.No | Sensor Type | Measurand | Working Principle |
|------|-------------|-----------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Optical Encoder** (Incremental/Absolute) | Rotational position & speed | Light interruption by slotted disc |
| 2 | **Magnetic Encoder** | Rotational position & speed | Hall effect / magnetoresistive sensing |
| 3 | **Tachogenerator (DC/AC)** | Angular velocity | Electromagnetic induction (EMF) |
| 4 | **Resolvers** | Angular position | Electromagnetic coupling |
| 5 | **Doppler Radar/Lidar** | Linear velocity | Frequency shift of reflected EM/acoustic wave |
| 6 | **Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU)** | Angular & linear acceleration | Coriolis force / mass displacement |
| 7 | **Gear Tooth Sensor** | Rotational speed | Variable reluctance pickup |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** In KTU 2024 scheme examinations, the most frequently asked motion/speed sensors are **optical encoders (especially quadrature decoders)** and **tachogenerators**. Master their resolution formulas and signal processing principles.

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following physical constants and engineering parameters are central to motion/speed sensor analysis:

- **Speed of light:** $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s
- **Standard encoder supply voltage:** **5V DC** or **3.3V DC** (TTL/CMOS logic)
- **Typical encoder resolution (quadrature):** **100 to 10,000 CPR** (Counts Per Revolution)
- **Doppler shift at vehicular speeds:** In the order of **Hz to kHz** (for radar) and **MHz** (for acoustic)
- **Robot wheel diameter (typical differential drive):** **65 mm to 200 mm**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Optical Encoders — Operational Breakdown

Optical encoders are the **most widely used motion sensors in mobile robots**. They consist of three primary components:

1. **Light Source:** A Light Emitting Diode (LED) that emits a focused beam of infrared light.
2. **Slotted Code Disc:** A glass or plastic disc with precisely etched opaque (blocking) and transparent (passing) slots arranged radially.
3. **Photodetector Array:** A photodiode or phototransistor that converts the received light pulses into electrical signals.

### 2.1.1 Operational Steps (Logic Flow)

- **Step 1 — Light Emission:** The LED continuously emits an infrared beam aimed at the code disc.
- **Step 2 — Modulation by Disc:** As the disc rotates, the slots alternately block and pass the light. This produces a **pulsating light signal** whose frequency is proportional to the rotational speed.
- **Step 3 — Photodetection:** The photodiode converts the light pulses into an electrical square wave (TTL/CMOS level).
- **Step 4 — Signal Conditioning:** The output is conditioned (debounced, amplified) and fed to the robot's counter/timer unit.
- **Step 5 — Decoding:** The controller counts pulses per unit time to compute speed, and integrates over time to compute distance.

### 2.1.2 Incremental vs. Absolute Encoders

- **Incremental Encoder:** Produces a stream of pulses; position is *relative* to a reference (index) point. **Common in mobile robots** due to low cost and high resolution.
- **Absolute Encoder:** Outputs a unique binary (Gray) code for each angular position; position is *known immediately* after power-on. Used in industrial robotic arms.

### 2.1.3 Quadrature Encoding — The Heart of Direction Sensing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A single-channel incremental encoder cannot distinguish between clockwise and counter-clockwise rotation. To solve this, **quadrature encoders** use **two photodetectors (Channel A and Channel B)** offset by **90° electrical** (one-quarter of a slot pitch).

By observing which channel leads the other, the controller determines:
- **Direction of rotation** (forward/reverse)
- **Speed** (pulse frequency)
- **Position** (accumulated count)

This **4× decoding** technique means a disc with $N$ slots produces $4N$ counts per revolution (CPR), quadrupling the resolution.

## 2.2 Tachogenerator — The Analog Speed Sensor

A **tachogenerator** is a small DC or AC generator whose output voltage is directly proportional to the rotational speed of its shaft. When mechanically coupled to a motor or wheel, it produces:

$$E = K_e \cdot \omega$$

where $K_e$ is the **voltage constant** (V·s/rad) and $\omega$ is the angular velocity (rad/s).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it matters:** Tachogenerators provide an **analog voltage proportional to speed**, making them ideal for analog feedback control loops (e.g., PID speed control of DC motors) without requiring digital counters.

## 2.3 Doppler Effect-Based Speed Sensing

The **Doppler effect** is the change in observed frequency of a wave when there is relative motion between the source and the observer. A Doppler radar/lidar emits a wave of known frequency $f_0$ and measures the reflected frequency $f_r$ from a moving surface.

For a sensor transmitting frequency $f_0$ towards a target moving with velocity $v$ (approaching/receding), the **Doppler shift** is:

$$f_d = \frac{2 \cdot v \cdot f_0}{c}$$

for **electromagnetic waves** (radar/lidar) where the factor of 2 accounts for the round-trip path. For **acoustic waves** in a medium of sound speed $c_s$:

$$f_d = \frac{2 \cdot v \cdot f_0}{c_s}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Doppler radar speed guns** (used by traffic police) operate on this exact principle — they measure $f_d$ and compute vehicle speed $v$.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table contains the master formula set for KTU 2024 Motion/Speed Sensors. Memorize these.**

| S.No | Quantity / Concept | Formula | Units | Notes |
|------|-------------------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Linear velocity from angular velocity | $v = \omega \cdot r$ | m/s | $r$ = wheel radius |
| 2 | Angular velocity from RPM | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi \cdot N_{rpm}}{60}$ | rad/s | $N_{rpm}$ in rev/min |
| 3 | Encoder linear resolution (1× decoding) | $\Delta s = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D}{N}$ | m/count | $D$ = wheel dia, $N$ = slots |
| 4 | Encoder linear resolution (4× quadrature) | $\Delta s = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D}{4N}$ | m/count | Quadrature decoding |
| 5 | Encoder resolution with gearbox (ratio $i$) | $\Delta s = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D}{4N \cdot i}$ | m/count | $i$ = gear reduction ratio |
| 6 | Distance traveled | $d = n \cdot \Delta s$ | m | $n$ = number of counts |
| 7 | Tachogenerator EMF | $E = K_e \cdot \omega$ | V | $K_e$ in V·s/rad |
| 8 | Doppler shift (EM wave) | $f_d = \dfrac{2v f_0}{c}$ | Hz | $c = 3\times 10^8$ m/s |
| 9 | Doppler shift (acoustic) | $f_d = \dfrac{2v f_0}{c_s}$ | Hz | $c_s \approx 343$ m/s (air) |
| 10 | Robot speed from wheel RPM | $v = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N_{rpm}}{60}$ | m/s | Direct wheel-drive |
| 11 | RPM from pulse count | $N_{rpm} = \dfrac{60 \cdot f_{pulse}}{N_{slots}}$ | rev/min | $f_{pulse}$ in Hz |
| 12 | Pulse frequency from RPM | $f_{pulse} = \dfrac{N_{slots} \cdot N_{rpm}}{60}$ | Hz | 1× decoding |
| 13 | Pulse frequency (4× quadrature) | $f_{pulse} = \dfrac{4 N_{slots} \cdot N_{rpm}}{60}$ | Hz | Quadrature |
| 14 | Acceleration from encoder | $a = \dfrac{\Delta v}{\Delta t} = \dfrac{\Delta \omega}{\Delta t} \cdot r$ | m/s² | Numerical differentiation |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Critical Exam Tip:** In KTU problems, students often confuse **D** (diameter) and **r** (radius) in the resolution formula. **D is always the wheel diameter**, and the factor $\pi$ comes from the circumference $\pi D$. Always cross-check with the problem statement.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Motion/speed sensors are the workhorses of:

- **AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles)** in warehouses (encoder-based odometry)
- **Autonomous cars** (wheel speed sensors for ABS/ESP and odometry fusion)
- **Drone flight controllers** (optical flow sensors for ground-relative speed)
- **CNC machines and robotic arms** (resolvers and absolute encoders for joint feedback)
- **Traffic enforcement** (Doppler radar speed guns)
- **Space rovers** (encoders on wheel suspension for slip detection)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Linear Resolution of an Optical Encoder

**Problem Statement:** Derive the expression for the linear distance traveled per encoder count ($\Delta s$) for a mobile robot wheel of diameter $D$ fitted with an incremental encoder having $N$ slots on the disc, with quadrature decoding ($4\times$ mode) and a gear reduction ratio of $i$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Counts per wheel revolution (with quadrature decoding):**

Each slot on the encoder disc produces one pulse per channel. With **two channels (A and B)** in quadrature, **four edges** (rising A, rising B, falling A, falling B) occur per slot per revolution.

$$C_{rev} = 4 \times N$$

**Step 2 — Linear distance per wheel revolution:**

The wheel rolls along the ground without slipping. The linear distance covered in one full revolution equals the circumference of the wheel:

$$C_{wheel} = \pi \cdot D$$

**Step 3 — Linear distance per encoder count:**

Divide the total circumference by the total counts per revolution:

$$\Delta s = \frac{C_{wheel}}{C_{rev}} = \frac{\pi \cdot D}{4N}$$

**Step 4 — Incorporating the gearbox ratio $i$:**

If the encoder is mounted on the **motor shaft** (before the gearbox), and the gear reduction ratio from motor to wheel is $i:1$ (meaning the motor turns $i$ times for every single wheel revolution), then the encoder disc turns $i$ times per wheel revolution. This further subdivides the resolution:

$$\Delta s = \frac{\pi \cdot D}{4N \cdot i}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** This is the **most important formula in motion/speed sensor design** for KTU exams. The smaller $\Delta s$, the higher the resolution (more precision per count).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Wheel Speed from Encoder Pulse Frequency

**Problem Statement:** A differential-drive mobile robot has wheels of diameter $D = 0.15$ m, each fitted with an incremental encoder of $N = 500$ slots operated in $4\times$ quadrature mode. The encoder outputs a pulse train of frequency $f_{pulse} = 8000$ Hz. Determine (a) the rotational speed of each wheel in RPM, and (b) the linear speed of the robot in m/s. (Assume direct drive, $i = 1$.)

### Part (a) — Wheel Rotational Speed

**Step 1 — Counts per wheel revolution (4× quadrature):**

$$C_{rev} = 4 \times N = 4 \times 500 = 2000 \text{ counts/rev}$$

**Step 2 — Relate pulse frequency to revolutions per second:**

Since there are $C_{rev}$ counts per revolution, the number of revolutions per second is:

$$n_{rev/s} = \frac{f_{pulse}}{C_{rev}} = \frac{8000}{2000} = 4 \text{ rev/s}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to RPM:**

$$N_{rpm} = n_{rev/s} \times 60 = 4 \times 60 = 240 \text{ RPM}$$

### Part (b) — Linear Robot Speed

**Step 1 — Compute wheel angular velocity:**

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi \cdot N_{rpm}}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 240}{60} = 8\pi \approx 25.133 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute linear speed using $v = \omega \cdot r$:**

With wheel radius $r = D/2 = 0.075$ m:

$$v = \omega \cdot r = 25.133 \times 0.075 = 1.885 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Alternative verification using circumference method:**

$$v = n_{rev/s} \times \pi D = 4 \times \pi \times 0.15 = 0.6\pi \approx 1.885 \text{ m/s} \quad \checkmark$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** The robot moves at approximately **1.885 m/s**, and the wheel rotates at **240 RPM**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Vehicle Speed from Doppler Radar

**Problem Statement:** A police Doppler radar operates at a transmit frequency of $f_0 = 10.525$ GHz (X-band). The radar detects a Doppler shift of $f_d = 1580$ Hz from an approaching vehicle. Determine the speed of the vehicle in m/s and km/h.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — State the Doppler equation for EM waves:**

$$f_d = \frac{2 \cdot v \cdot f_0}{c}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for velocity $v$:**

$$v = \frac{f_d \cdot c}{2 \cdot f_0}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$v = \frac{1580 \times 3 \times 10^8}{2 \times 10.525 \times 10^9}$$

**Step 4 — Simplify:**

$$v = \frac{1580 \times 3 \times 10^8}{2.105 \times 10^{10}} = \frac{4.74 \times 10^{11}}{2.105 \times 10^{10}}$$

$$v \approx 22.52 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to km/h:**

$$v = 22.52 \times 3.6 \approx 81.07 \text{ km/h}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** The vehicle is traveling at approximately **22.52 m/s** or **81.07 km/h**, which is a typical highway speed — well within the X-band radar's measurement range.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Encoder vs. Tachogenerator vs. Doppler

| Parameter | Optical Encoder | Tachogenerator | Doppler Radar/Lidar |
|-----------|----------------|----------------|---------------------|
| **Output type** | Digital pulses | Analog voltage | Frequency shift |
| **Measurand** | Position + Speed (derived) | Speed only | Speed (direct) |
| **Direction sensing** | Yes (with quadrature) | No (sign of voltage indicates direction) | Yes (sign of $f_d$) |
| **Resolution** | High (limited by $N$ and decoding) | Continuous (analog) | Continuous |
| **Lower speed limit** | Excellent (counts) | Poor (offset drift) | Moderate (depends on $f_0$) |
| **Cost** | Low–Medium | Medium | High |
| **Susceptibility to noise** | Low (digital) | High (analog) | Moderate |
| **Robotic application** | Wheel odometry (universal) | Motor speed feedback | Ground-relative velocity (drones) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Optical Encoder Working Principle

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the functional architecture of a quadrature optical encoder system as used in mobile robots.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[LED Light Source] --> B[Slotted Code Disc]
    B --> C[Channel A Photodetector]
    B --> D[Channel B Photodetector]
    C --> E[Comparator and Signal Conditioner A]
    D --> F[Comparator and Signal Conditioner B]
    E --> G[Quadrature Decoder Module]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Direction Logic Unit]
    G --> I[Counter and Timer Unit]
    H --> J[Microcontroller or FPGA]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Robot Pose Estimator Odometry]
```

**Explanation of the flow:**

- The **LED** emits light that passes through (or is blocked by) the rotating code disc.
- The two **photodetectors** (Channel A and Channel B) convert the light pulses into electrical signals, offset by 90°.
- The **signal conditioners** shape the signals into clean digital square waves.
- The **quadrature decoder** identifies the four edge transitions per slot.
- The **direction logic** determines rotation direction by checking the phase relationship between A and B.
- The **counter** accumulates pulses to compute position; the **timer** measures pulse frequency to compute speed.
- The **microcontroller** fuses this data into the robot's pose estimate (odometry).

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Tachogenerator Feedback Control Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[DC Motor Shaft] --> B[Tachogenerator Coupling]
    B --> C[Tachogenerator Generates EMF E]
    C --> D[Signal Conditioning and Filtering]
    D --> E[Comparator with Reference Voltage]
    E --> F[Error Signal Computation]
    F --> G[PID Controller]
    G --> H[PWM Signal Generation]
    H --> I[Power Amplifier / H-Bridge]
    I --> A
```

**Explanation:**

- The tachogenerator produces a voltage proportional to motor speed.
- This voltage is compared to a reference (desired speed) voltage to produce an **error signal**.
- The error drives a **PID controller**, which outputs a **PWM command** to the motor driver.
- The driver regulates the motor's armature voltage, closing the speed control loop.

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Doppler Speed Measurement System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Oscillator Frequency f0] --> B[Transmitter Antenna]
    B --> C[Moving Target Surface]
    C --> D[Reflected Wave Frequency f0 plus fd]
    D --> E[Receiver Antenna]
    E --> F[Mixer and Frequency Comparator]
    A --> F
    F --> G[Doppler Shift Extraction fd]
    G --> H[Speed Calculator v equals 2 fd c over 2 f0]
    H --> I[Digital Display of Speed]
```

**Explanation:**

- The oscillator generates a stable transmit frequency $f_0$.
- The transmitted wave reflects off the moving target with a frequency shift $f_d$.
- The mixer compares the received and transmitted frequencies, extracting $f_d$.
- The speed calculator applies the Doppler formula to compute the target's velocity.

---

## 4.4 Quadrature Signal Waveform — Conceptual Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SQ[Quadrature Signal Relationships]
        A[Channel A: Square Wave]
        B[Channel B: Square Wave 90 degrees phase shifted]
        A --> C[Forward Rotation: A leads B]
        B --> C
        A --> D[Reverse Rotation: B leads A]
        B --> D
    end
```

**Explanation:**

- When the disc rotates **forward** (clockwise), Channel A's rising edge occurs **before** Channel B's rising edge — A **leads** B.
- When the disc rotates **reverse** (counter-clockwise), Channel B's rising edge occurs **before** Channel A's — B **leads** A.
- The decoder uses this 90° phase relationship to determine direction unambiguously.

---

## 4.5 Mobile Robot Odometry System — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Left Wheel Encoder] --> C[Microcontroller Timer and Counter]
    B[Right Wheel Encoder] --> C
    C --> D[Velocity Computation Module]
    D --> E[Pose Update x, y, theta]
    E --> F[Robot Pose Estimator]
    F --> G[Navigation and Path Planner]
```

**Explanation:**

- Both wheel encoders feed their pulse trains to the microcontroller.
- The **velocity computation module** converts pulse counts into linear and angular velocities.
- The **pose update module** integrates velocities over time to update the robot's position $(x, y)$ and orientation $(\theta)$.
- This pose estimate is used by the **navigation and path planner** to make motion decisions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Q: Differentiate between incremental and absolute encoders. State one application of each in mobile robotics.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Incremental Encoder | Absolute Encoder |
|-----------|--------------------|--------------------|
| **Output** | Pulses (relative to reference) | Unique binary/Gray code for each position |
| **Position after power-off** | Lost (requires homing) | Retained |
| **Cost & complexity** | Low & simple | Higher & more complex |
| **Resolution** | Determined by slot count + decoding | Determined by bit-depth of code disc |
| **Application in mobile robots** | Wheel odometry in differential drive robots | Joint position sensing in robotic arms (industrial) |

**(3 marks — 1 for definition of each, 1 for application)**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Q: What is the Doppler effect? Write the Doppler shift equation for a radar speed sensor and explain each term.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Doppler effect** is the apparent change in frequency of a wave when there is relative motion between the source and the observer (or reflecting surface).

$$f_d = \frac{2 \cdot v \cdot f_0}{c}$$

where:
- $f_d$ = Doppler shift frequency (Hz)
- $v$ = velocity of the target (m/s)
- $f_0$ = transmitted frequency (Hz)
- $c$ = speed of light ($3 \times 10^8$ m/s)
- The factor of **2** accounts for the round-trip path (transmit + reflect).

**Application:** Used in police radar guns, automotive adaptive cruise control, and drone ground-relative speed sensing.

**(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for equation with terms, 1 for application)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Every Part B question provides an **internal choice** — the student must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**A differential-drive mobile robot is fitted with two identical incremental optical encoders, one on each drive wheel. Each wheel has diameter $D = 120$ mm and is driven through a gearbox with reduction ratio $i = 20:1$ (motor turns 20 times per wheel revolution). Each encoder disc has $N = 250$ slots and operates in $4\times$ quadrature mode. The left encoder produces 4800 pulses in 0.5 seconds during straight-line motion, and the right encoder produces 4800 pulses in the same interval. The wheelbase (distance between wheels) is $L = 300$ mm.**

**Solve the following:**

**(a)** Calculate the linear resolution (mm/count) of the encoder system and the linear speed of the robot. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If the robot now turns right and the right encoder produces 4500 pulses in 0.5 s while the left produces 4800 pulses in 0.5 s, determine the new linear velocities of both wheels, the angular velocity of the robot, and the radius of curvature of its turning path. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A:

### Part (a) — Resolution and Linear Speed

**Step 1 — Counts per wheel revolution (4× quadrature):**

$$C_{rev} = 4 \times N = 4 \times 250 = 1000 \text{ counts/rev}$$

**Step 2 — Linear resolution with gearbox:**

$$\Delta s = \frac{\pi \cdot D}{4N \cdot i} = \frac{\pi \times 0.120}{1000 \times 20} = \frac{0.3770}{20000}$$

$$\Delta s = 1.885 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m/count} = 0.01885 \text{ mm/count}$$

**Step 3 — Pulse frequency:**

$$f_{pulse} = \frac{4800}{0.5} = 9600 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 4 — Linear speed:**

$$v = f_{pulse} \times \Delta s = 9600 \times 1.885 \times 10^{-5} = 0.1810 \text{ m/s} \approx 18.1 \text{ cm/s}$$

**Alternative method — using RPM:**

$$N_{rpm} = \frac{9600}{1000} \times 60 = 576 \text{ RPM (wheel)}$$

$$v = \frac{\pi D \cdot N_{rpm}}{60} = \frac{\pi \times 0.120 \times 576}{60} = 3.622 \text{ m/s}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** The above two methods give different results because we must be careful about whether the 4800 counts were on the *encoder disc* (motor side) or already on the *wheel side*. Since the problem says "encoders on drive wheels" and includes the gearbox $i = 20:1$, the 4800 counts are on the encoder disc (motor side). The linear resolution formula $\Delta s = \pi D / (4N \cdot i)$ already accounts for the gear reduction. **Always include $i$ in the resolution calculation when the encoder is on the motor shaft.**

**Corrected Step 4 (using $\Delta s$ method):**

$$v = f_{pulse} \times \Delta s = 9600 \times 1.885 \times 10^{-5} = 0.1810 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\boxed{v_L = v_R = 0.181 \text{ m/s}, \quad \Delta s = 0.01885 \text{ mm/count}}$$

### Part (b) — Differential Drive Kinematics During a Right Turn

**Step 1 — Compute pulse frequencies:**

$$f_L = \frac{4800}{0.5} = 9600 \text{ Hz}, \quad f_R = \frac{4500}{0.5} = 9000 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 2 — Compute individual wheel linear speeds:**

$$v_L = 9600 \times 1.885 \times 10^{-5} = 0.1810 \text{ m/s}$$

$$v_R = 9000 \times 1.885 \times 10^{-5} = 0.1697 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Compute angular velocity of the robot (differential drive equation):**

$$\omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L} = \frac{0.1697 - 0.1810}{0.300} = \frac{-0.0113}{0.300}$$

$$\omega = -0.0377 \text{ rad/s}$$

The **negative sign** indicates a **right turn** (clockwise rotation), consistent with $v_L > v_R$.

**Step 4 — Compute the Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR) and radius of curvature:**

The **radius of curvature** $R$ of the robot's path is the distance from the ICR to the midpoint between the wheels:

$$v = \frac{v_L + v_R}{2} = \frac{0.1810 + 0.1697}{2} = 0.1754 \text{ m/s}$$

$$R = \frac{v}{\vert \omega \vert} = \frac{0.1754}{0.0377} = 4.653 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{v_L = 0.181 \text{ m/s}, \quad v_R = 0.170 \text{ m/s}, \quad \omega = -0.0377 \text{ rad/s}, \quad R = 4.65 \text{ m (right turn)}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Alternative]

**(a)** With the aid of a block diagram, explain the working principle of a **quadrature optical encoder**. Show how direction of rotation is determined from the A and B channel signals. Derive the expression for linear resolution of an encoder fitted to a wheel of diameter $D$ with $N$ slots, operating in $4\times$ quadrature mode through a gearbox of ratio $i$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Doppler radar speed sensor operates at $24$ GHz (K-band). A car is approaching the sensor and produces a Doppler shift of $1778$ Hz. Compute the speed of the car in m/s and km/h. If the same car were measured by an acoustic Doppler sensor operating at $40$ kHz in air ($c_s = 343$ m/s), what would be the acoustic Doppler shift? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B:

### Part (a) — Quadrature Optical Encoder Explanation

**Working Principle (Block Diagram Description):**

A quadrature optical encoder consists of:
1. An **LED** that emits infrared light.
2. A **slotted code disc** (with $N$ transparent and $N$ opaque slots) attached to the rotating shaft.
3. **Two photodetectors** (Channel A and Channel B) placed such that their angular separation corresponds to **one-quarter slot pitch**, producing a 90° electrical phase shift between channels.
4. **Signal conditioning circuitry** that converts the photodetector outputs into clean TTL/CMOS square waves.
5. A **quadrature decoder** in the microcontroller that identifies the four edge transitions (rising A, rising B, falling A, falling B) per slot.

**Direction Detection Logic:**

- During **forward rotation**: Channel A's rising edge occurs **before** Channel B's rising edge (A leads B by 90°).
- During **reverse rotation**: Channel B's rising edge occurs **before** Channel A's (B leads A by 90°).
- The decoder checks the state of Channel B at the instant of Channel A's rising edge: if B is LOW → forward; if B is HIGH → reverse.

**Derivation of Linear Resolution:**

As derived in Section 3.1, the linear distance per encoder count is:

$$\Delta s = \frac{\pi \cdot D}{4N \cdot i}$$

This represents the smallest linear displacement of the wheel that the encoder can resolve.

**(7 marks — 2 for diagram, 2 for direction logic, 3 for derivation)**

### Part (b) — Doppler Radar and Acoustic Calculations

**Step 1 — Apply the Doppler equation for EM waves:**

$$f_d = \frac{2 \cdot v \cdot f_0}{c}$$

$$v = \frac{f_d \cdot c}{2 \cdot f_0} = \frac{1778 \times 3 \times 10^8}{2 \times 24 \times 10^9}$$

$$v = \frac{5.334 \times 10^{11}}{4.8 \times 10^{10}} = 11.11 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to km/h:**

$$v = 11.11 \times 3.6 = 40.0 \text{ km/h}$$

**Step 3 — Acoustic Doppler shift:**

Using the same speed $v = 11.11$ m/s with the acoustic equation:

$$f_{d,acoustic} = \frac{2 \cdot v \cdot f_0}{c_s} = \frac{2 \times 11.11 \times 40{,}000}{343}$$

$$f_{d,acoustic} = \frac{888{,}800}{343} = 2591 \text{ Hz}$$

$$\boxed{v = 11.11 \text{ m/s} = 40.0 \text{ km/h}, \quad f_{d,acoustic} = 2591 \text{ Hz}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mark Losers:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in the Doppler equation. The factor of 2 is **mandatory** for radar/lidar because the wave travels to the target AND back. Students who write $f_d = v f_0 / c$ lose 2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing $D$ and $r$** in the encoder resolution formula. The problem always gives $D$ (diameter); the formula uses $\pi D$, NOT $2\pi r$ (though they are mathematically equal, students must be consistent with the given variable).
> 3. **Sign errors in differential drive kinematics.** The right turn means $v_L > v_R$, making $\omega$ negative. Students who write $\omega = +0.0377$ rad/s (just magnitude) lose 1 mark for missing the direction.
> 4. **Not converting units** — RPM to rad/s requires the $2\pi/60$ factor. Forgetting this is a frequent error.
> 5. **Misidentifying which shaft the encoder is on** (motor vs. wheel) — this changes whether the gear ratio $i$ appears in the denominator or not. **Read the problem statement carefully.**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Motion/Speed Sensors (Module 2)**

### Core Concepts
- ✅ **Motion/speed sensors** measure the kinematic state of a mobile robot and are the basis of **odometry**.
- ✅ **Optical encoders** use a slotted disc + LED + photodetector to generate pulses proportional to rotation.
- ✅ **Incremental encoders** output relative pulses; **absolute encoders** output unique codes for each position.
- ✅ **Quadrature encoders** use two channels (A and B) offset by 90° to determine **direction** and quadruple resolution.
- ✅ **Tachogenerators** are analog devices producing a voltage $E = K_e \omega$ proportional to angular speed.
- ✅ **Doppler sensors** (radar/lidar/acoustic) measure velocity via the frequency shift $f_d = 2v f_0 / c$ of reflected waves.

### Master Formulas (Must Memorize)
- ✅ Linear speed: $v = \omega r = \pi D N_{rpm} / 60$
- ✅ Encoder resolution (4× quadrature, with gearbox): $\Delta s = \pi D / (4 N i)$
- ✅ Distance traveled: $d = n \cdot \Delta s$
- ✅ Tachogenerator EMF: $E = K_e \omega$
- ✅ Doppler shift (EM): $f_d = 2 v f_0 / c$
- ✅ Doppler shift (acoustic): $f_d = 2 v f_0 / c_s$
- ✅ Differential drive kinematics: $v = (v_L + v_R) / 2$, $\omega = (v_R - v_L) / L$

### Engineering Judgment Points
- ✅ **Resolution** improves with more encoder slots and higher decoding mode (1×, 2×, 4×).
- ✅ **Gearbox** in the drive train further increases effective resolution by ratio $i$.
- ✅ **Tachogenerators** are obsolete for precision robotics but still found in legacy industrial systems.
- ✅ **Doppler sensors** are non-contact and ideal for measuring speed of objects a robot cannot physically touch (e.g., drones measuring ground speed, traffic radar).
- ✅ **Encoder errors** accumulate over time due to wheel slip, wheel diameter uncertainty, and encoder quantization — this is a fundamental limitation of dead reckoning.
- ✅ The **radius of curvature** $R = v / |\omega|$ describes the path of a turning differential-drive robot.

### Typical KTU Question Patterns
- **3-mark questions:** Define sensor, write formula, state application.
- **7-mark sub-questions:** Derive resolution formula, compute speed from given RPM/count data.
- **14-mark full questions:** Complete differential-drive kinematics problem with encoder data + Doppler-based speed measurement comparison.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vision-Based Sensors. Representing Uncertainty in Sensor Measurements - Statistical Representation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET744 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Vision-Based Sensors. Representing Uncertainty in Sensor Measurements - Statistical Representation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Vision-Based Sensors — Formal Definition

A **Vision-Based Sensor** in mobile robotics is an electro-optical perception device that captures, digitises, and interprets electromagnetic radiation (primarily in the **380 nm – 750 nm** visible band, though near-infrared extensions up to **1100 nm** are common) to extract geometric, semantic, and motion information about the robot's operating environment. The two dominant semiconductor technologies are **Charge-Coupled Device (CCD)** and **Complementary Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor (CMOS)** active-pixel sensors.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *"A vision sensor is a non-contact, passive/active transducer that converts incident light intensity from a 3-D scene into a 2-D discrete intensity matrix (digital image) I(u,v,t), where (u,v) are pixel coordinates and t denotes the discrete sampling instant."*

### 1.2 Representing Uncertainty — Formal Definition

Uncertainty in a sensor measurement $z$ is the quantified degree to which the *true* physical quantity differs from the *reported* value. Statistically, it is represented as a **random variable** $Z$ described by a **Probability Density Function (PDF)** $p(z)$, which assigns a likelihood to every possible value the measurement could assume.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Idea:** *A single number from a sensor is not enough — we must also express how much we **trust** that number. Probability theory gives us the mathematical machinery to do exactly this.*

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Foggy Window"

Imagine you are driving through thick fog and your GPS says *"Turn right in 50 m."* You do not blindly trust it. You mentally assign a confidence:
- A satellite-derived reading? High trust (low uncertainty).
- A reading from a single Wi-Fi triangulation? Lower trust (higher uncertainty).

In robotics, the GPS measurement is the **mean** $\mu$, and the "thickness of the fog" is the **standard deviation** $\sigma$. A wider fog ⇒ larger $\sigma$ ⇒ less trustworthy measurement. Statistical representation simply makes this intuition **mathematical**.

| Sensor Class | Typical Uncertainty Symbol | Physical Source |
|---|---|---|
| Range Finder (LiDAR) | $\sigma_r \approx \pm 0.02$ m | Time-of-flight quantisation |
| Wheel Odometry | $\sigma_\theta \approx \pm 0.05$ rad | Wheel slip, encoder granularity |
| Camera Bearing | $\sigma_\phi \approx \pm 0.5°$ | Pixel quantisation, lens distortion |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Vision Sensor — Operational Pipeline

The operation of a vision sensor is a deterministic-to-stochastic transformation broken into **five cascading stages**:

1. **Photon Collection Stage** — Light from the 3-D scene passes through the lens aperture (diameter $D$, focal length $f$). The **f-number** $N = f/D$ controls irradiance.
2. **Opto-Electronic Conversion** — Photons strike the silicon die. CCD shifts charge pixel-by-pixel to a single readout amplifier; CMOS performs per-pixel amplification (lower power, higher noise).
3. **Sampling & Quantisation** — Continuous irradiance $E(x,y)$ is sampled on a discrete grid of pitch $\Delta u \times \Delta v$ and quantised to $2^n$ grey levels (typically $n = 8$ ⇒ **256 levels**, or $n = 10$–$12$ for scientific CMOS).
4. **Noise Injection (Stochastic Layer)** — Three dominant noise sources corrupt the ideal signal:
   - **Photon shot noise:** Poisson-distributed, variance $\sigma^2_{shot} = \lambda$ (mean photon count).
   - **Dark current noise:** $\sigma^2_{dark} = I_{dark} \cdot t_{int}$ (integration time dependent).
   - **Readout noise:** Additive Gaussian, $\sigma^2_{read}$.
5. **Digital Output** — Final pixel intensity $I(u,v) = I_{true}(u,v) + \eta(u,v)$, where $\eta$ is the composite noise.

### 2.2 Why Do We Need Statistical Representation?

A deterministic model $z = h(x)$ assumes the sensor is *perfect*. In reality:
- **Systematic errors** (bias, miscalibration) shift the mean.
- **Random errors** (noise, quantisation) spread the distribution.

Statistical representation answers three engineering questions:

- **Where** is the true value most likely? → *Mean* $\mu$
- **How spread out** are plausible values? → *Variance* $\sigma^2$
- **How coupled** are errors across sensor channels? → *Covariance* $\Sigma$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Uncertainty Quantification

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Gaussian (1-D) PDF | $p(z) = \dfrac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}}\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(z-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$ | $\sigma > 0$ |
| 2 | Mean (Expectation) | $\mu = \mathbb{E}[Z] = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} z\,p(z)\,dz$ | SI units of $z$ |
| 3 | Variance | $\sigma^2 = \mathbb{E}[(Z-\mu)^2]$ | Squared units |
| 4 | Std. Deviation | $\sigma = \sqrt{\sigma^2}$ | Same as $z$ |
| 5 | Covariance (2-D) | $\sigma_{ij} = \mathbb{E}[(Z_i-\mu_i)(Z_j-\mu_j)]$ | Cross-channel error coupling |
| 6 | Covariance Matrix | $\Sigma = \begin{bmatrix}\sigma_{xx} & \sigma_{xy}\\ \sigma_{yx} & \sigma_{yy}\end{bmatrix}$ | Symmetric, positive-definite |
| 7 | Multivariate Gaussian | $p(\mathbf{z}) = \dfrac{1}{(2\pi)^{n/2}\vert\Sigma\vert^{1/2}}\exp\!\left(-\tfrac{1}{2}(\mathbf{z}-\boldsymbol{\mu})^\top\Sigma^{-1}(\mathbf{z}-\boldsymbol{\mu})\right)$ | $\mathbf{z}\in\mathbb{R}^n$ |
| 8 | Mahalanobis Distance | $d_M = \sqrt{(\mathbf{z}-\boldsymbol{\mu})^\top\Sigma^{-1}(\mathbf{z}-\boldsymbol{\mu})}$ | Dimensionless; in $\sigma$-units |
| 9 | Propagation (linear) | $\Sigma_y = J\,\Sigma_z\,J^\top$ | $J$ = Jacobian of $\mathbf{y}=g(\mathbf{z})$ |
| 10 | Pinhole Projection | $u = f_x\dfrac{X}{Z},\quad v = f_y\dfrac{Y}{Z}$ | Camera intrinsics |
| 11 | Image Noise Variance | $\sigma^2_{total} = \sigma^2_{shot} + \sigma^2_{dark} + \sigma^2_{read}$ | Additive noise model |
| 12 | SNR (digital) | $\text{SNR}_{dB} = 20\log_{10}\!\dfrac{\mu_{signal}}{\sigma_{noise}}$ | Decibels |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crucial Rule for KTU Problems:** When propagating uncertainty through any nonlinear function $g(\mathbf{z})$, always compute the **Jacobian** $J = \partial g/\partial \mathbf{z}$ and apply $\Sigma_y = J\Sigma_z J^\top$. Neglecting the second-order term is valid only if $g$ is approximately linear in the neighbourhood of $\boldsymbol{\mu}$.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Autonomous Vehicles:** Fusing LiDAR, camera and radar uncertainties via Kalman filters requires every sensor to be represented as a Gaussian with explicit $\Sigma$.
- **Medical Robotics (Da Vinci, ROSA):** Surgical tool tracking uses vision with sub-millimetre covariance matrices for haptic feedback.
- **Aerial Swarms:** UWB ranging noise is modelled as Gaussian; the covariance is broadcast over the network for distributed SLAM.
- **Industrial Bin Picking:** Stereo-camera depth uncertainty grows quadratically with range — must be propagated into grasp-planning cost functions.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the 1-D Gaussian PDF from First Principles

**Step 1 — Define the desirable properties of an uncertainty model.**
We want a PDF $p(z)$ that:
(a) is non-negative everywhere,
(b) integrates to 1 (normalisation),
(c) is symmetric about its mean $\mu$,
(d) decays smoothly to zero as $\vert z-\mu\vert \to \infty$,
(e) has a single tunable spread parameter $\sigma$.

The simplest function satisfying all five is the exponential of a negative quadratic. Hence we postulate:

$$
p(z) = A \exp\!\left(-B\,(z-\mu)^2\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the normalisation condition.**

$$
\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} p(z)\,dz = 1
$$

Substitute the postulated form:

$$
A\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\exp\!\left(-B\,(z-\mu)^2\right)dz = 1
$$

**Step 3 — Use the standard Gaussian integral.**

$$
\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\exp\!\left(-B\,x^2\right)dx = \sqrt{\dfrac{\pi}{B}}
$$

So:

$$
A\sqrt{\dfrac{\pi}{B}} = 1 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad A = \sqrt{\dfrac{B}{\pi}}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the variance and identify B.**

By definition, the variance is:

$$
\sigma^2 = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty}(z-\mu)^2\,p(z)\,dz
$$

Substitute $p(z)$ and let $u = z-\mu$:

$$
\sigma^2 = A\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}u^2 e^{-B u^2}du
$$

Using the moment identity $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}u^2 e^{-B u^2}du = \dfrac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2B^{3/2}}$:

$$
\sigma^2 = A\cdot\dfrac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2B^{3/2}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{B}{\pi}}\cdot\dfrac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2B^{3/2}} = \dfrac{1}{2B}
$$

Therefore:

$$
B = \dfrac{1}{2\sigma^2}, \qquad A = \dfrac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}}
$$

**Step 5 — Assemble the final expression.**

$$
p(z) = \dfrac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}}\,\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(z-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)
$$

This is the canonical **Normal distribution** $\mathcal{N}(\mu,\sigma^2)$.

---

### 3.2 Worked Analytical Example — Vision Sensor Uncertainty Propagation

> **Problem (KTU Style):** A stereo camera estimates the depth $Z$ of a feature using triangulation. The disparity $d$ is measured with mean $\mu_d = 32$ pixels and standard deviation $\sigma_d = 0.5$ pixels. The baseline is $B = 0.12$ m and focal length $f = 800$ pixels. Compute the depth $Z$, the depth variance $\sigma_Z^2$, and the 95% confidence interval. Assume $d$ is Gaussian.

**Step 1 — Set up the depth model.**

For a rectified stereo pair, depth is:

$$
Z = \dfrac{f\,B}{d}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the mean depth via first-order linearisation.**

For a nonlinear function, $\mathbb{E}[g(Z)] \neq g(\mathbb{E}[Z])$ exactly, but the linearised approximation gives:

$$
\mu_Z \approx g(\mu_d) = \dfrac{f\,B}{\mu_d} = \dfrac{800 \times 0.12}{32}
$$

$$
\mu_Z = \dfrac{96}{32} = 3.0\ \text{m}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Jacobian (sensitivity of $Z$ w.r.t. $d$).**

$$
J = \dfrac{\partial Z}{\partial d} = -\dfrac{f\,B}{d^2} = -\dfrac{96}{32^2} = -\dfrac{96}{1024}
$$

$$
J = -0.09375\ \text{m/pixel}
$$

**Step 4 — Propagate the variance.**

$$
\sigma_Z^2 = J^2\,\sigma_d^2 = (-0.09375)^2 \times (0.5)^2
$$

$$
\sigma_Z^2 = 0.008789 \times 0.25 = 0.002197\ \text{m}^2
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the standard deviation.**

$$
\sigma_Z = \sqrt{0.002197} \approx 0.0469\ \text{m} \approx 4.69\ \text{cm}
$$

**Step 6 — Construct the 95% Confidence Interval.**

For a Gaussian, 95% of the probability mass lies within $\pm 1.96\,\sigma$ of the mean:

$$
\text{CI}_{95\%} = \mu_Z \pm 1.96\,\sigma_Z = 3.0 \pm 1.96 \times 0.0469
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{CI}_{95\%} = [2.908\ \text{m},\ 3.092\ \text{m}]}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation for KTU:** Notice that $\sigma_Z \propto 1/d^2$. As the feature moves further away, the disparity shrinks, and the depth uncertainty explodes — a *classic* result in stereo vision that examiners love to test.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common PDF Choices for Sensors

| Sensor Type | Recommended PDF | Parameters | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| LiDAR range | Gaussian $\mathcal{N}(\mu_r,\sigma_r^2)$ | $\mu_r, \sigma_r$ | Central-limit dominated |
| Wheel encoder ticks | Uniform $\mathcal{U}(a,b)$ | $a = k-0.5,\ b = k+0.5$ | Quantisation is bounded |
| Photon-counting camera | Poisson $\mathcal{P}(\lambda)$ | $\lambda = \eta P t_{int}$ | Discrete photon arrivals |
| Sonar range | Mixture: Gaussian + outliers | $\alpha\,\mathcal{N} + (1-\alpha)\,\mathcal{U}$ | Specular multipath |
| IMU bias | Random walk (integral of WN) | $\sigma_{RW} = \sigma_b\sqrt{\Delta t}$ | Brownian motion model |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Vision Sensor Acquisition & Uncertainty Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    scene3d[Scene3D Real World] --> lens[Optical Lens f-number N]
    lens --> sensor[CCD or CMOS Sensor Array]
    sensor --> sampling[Spatial Sampling Delta u x Delta v]
    sampling --> quant[Quantisation 2 to n levels]
    quant --> ideal[Ideal Image I true u v]
    ideal --> noise[Noise Injection Layer]
    noise --> shot[Shot Noise Poisson lambda]
    noise --> dark[Dark Current Noise]
    noise --> readout[Readout Noise Gaussian]
    shot --> final[Digital Image I u v]
    dark --> final
    readout --> final
    final --> feature[Feature Extraction]
    feature --> stat[Statistical Model]
    stat --> mean[Mean mu]
    stat --> var[Variance sigma squared]
    stat --> cov[Covariance Matrix Sigma]
    mean --> fusion[Sensor Fusion Engine EKF Particle Filter]
    var --> fusion
    cov --> fusion
    fusion --> pose[Robot Pose Estimate with Uncertainty Ellipse]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Uncertainty Representation Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    level0[Level 0 Scalar Reading] --> level1[Level 1 Mean plus Std Dev]
    level1 --> level2[Level 2 Full PDF p of z]
    level2 --> level3[Level 3 Multivariate Gaussian mu Sigma]
    level3 --> level4[Level 4 Non Gaussian Mixture or Particle Set]
    level1 --> example1[Use Case Single Encoder]
    level2 --> example2[Use Case LiDAR Range]
    level3 --> example3[Use Case Stereo x y z]
    level4 --> example4[Use Case Global Localization with Ambiguity]
```

### 4.3 Vision Sensor Architecture Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph OPTICS [OPTICAL SUBSYSTEM]
        obj[Scene Object 3D]
        lens1[Compound Lens]
        iris[Iris Aperture]
    end
    subgraph OPTO [OPTO ELECTRONIC SUBSYSTEM]
        ccd[CCD Array M by N Pixels]
        amp[Charge Amplifier]
        adc[12 bit ADC]
    end
    subgraph PROC [DIGITAL PROCESSING SUBSYSTEM]
        demosaic[Demosaicing Bayer]
        denoise[Noise Filtering]
        calib[Intrinsic Extrinsic Calibration]
        stat1[Uncertainty Estimator]
    end
    obj --> lens1 --> iris --> ccd --> amp --> adc --> demosaic --> denoise --> calib --> stat1
    stat1 --> output[Output z hat and Sigma]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU Exams:** When asked to *"draw the block diagram of a vision-based sensor with uncertainty modelling"*, the examiner expects **three explicit blocks**: Optics → Sampling/Quantisation → Statistical Estimator. Skipping the last block is the #1 reason students lose marks.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define vision-based sensors. List any two applications in mobile robotics.*

**Model Answer:**
A vision-based sensor is a non-contact electro-optical transducer that converts incident light from a 3-D scene into a 2-D digital image $I(u,v,t)$ for interpretation. It uses CCD or CMOS arrays with a lens of focal length $f$.

**Applications (any two):**
1. **Obstacle detection and avoidance** in autonomous ground vehicles.
2. **Visual SLAM (Simultaneous Localisation and Mapping)** using monocular or stereo cameras.
3. **Object recognition** for pick-and-place manipulators.
4. **Lane and traffic-sign detection** in self-driving cars.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*What is meant by representing uncertainty statistically? Why is a Gaussian distribution preferred for sensor noise modelling?*

**Model Answer:**
Representing uncertainty statistically means expressing the sensor reading not as a single number but as a **random variable** with a **probability density function** $p(z)$ characterised by parameters such as the mean $\mu$ and variance $\sigma^2$.

**Why Gaussian preference (any three points, 1 mark each):**
1. **Central Limit Theorem:** Sum of many independent error sources tends toward Gaussian, regardless of individual distributions.
2. **Mathematical tractability:** Closed-form expressions for marginals, conditionals and products exist.
3. **Maximum entropy:** Among all distributions with a given mean and variance, Gaussian has the highest entropy ⇒ least-biased choice.
4. **Linear propagation:** Uncertainty transforms cleanly via Jacobian: $\Sigma_y = J\Sigma_z J^\top$.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**
> *(a)* Explain the working of a CCD vision sensor with a neat block diagram. Differentiate between CCD and CMOS sensors. **(7 Marks)**
>
> *(b)* A mobile robot's sonar returns a range measurement $r = 2.40$ m. The sensor is modelled as $\mathcal{N}(\mu_r,\sigma_r^2)$ with $\sigma_r = 0.15$ m. Compute the probability that the true range lies in $[2.20, 2.60]$ m. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**1. Working of CCD Sensor (5 marks):**
- Photons strike the silicon die through a lens.
- Each pixel well accumulates charge proportional to incident irradiance and integration time $t_{int}$.
- Charge is shifted pixel-by-pixel to a serial readout register (analogue shift register).
- A single output amplifier converts charge to voltage.
- ADC quantises voltage to digital intensity.

**2. Block Diagram (1 mark):**

```
Lens → Photodiode Array → Charge Transfer Stage → Output Amplifier → ADC → Digital Image
```

**3. CCD vs CMOS (1 mark):**

| Feature | CCD | CMOS |
|---|---|---|
| Readout | Sequential bucket-brigade | Per-pixel amplifier |
| Noise | Lower | Higher |
| Power | Higher | Lower |
| Speed | Slower | Faster |
| Cost | Higher | Lower |

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Standardise the bounds.**

$$
z_1 = \dfrac{2.20 - 2.40}{0.15} = -1.333
$$

$$
z_2 = \dfrac{2.60 - 2.40}{0.15} = +1.333
$$

**Step 2 — Look up the standard normal CDF.**

$$
P(Z \le 1.333) = \Phi(1.333) \approx 0.9088
$$

$$
P(Z \le -1.333) = 1 - \Phi(1.333) = 0.0912
$$

**Step 3 — Subtract to get the interval probability.**

$$
P(2.20 \le R \le 2.60) = 0.9088 - 0.0912 = 0.8176
$$

$$
\boxed{P \approx 81.76\%}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative Choice)*

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**
> *(a)* With the help of a neat diagram, explain the pinhole camera model. Derive the perspective projection equations. **(7 Marks)**
>
> *(b)* A mobile robot uses a laser range finder that measures distance $r$ with variance $\sigma_r^2 = (0.05 + 0.02\,r)^2$. For a measurement $r = 5.0$ m, compute (i) the standard deviation, (ii) the 95% confidence interval, and (iii) the probability that the true range exceeds 5.20 m. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**1. Pinhole Diagram (3 marks):**
A point $P(X,Y,Z)$ in the camera frame passes through the optical centre $O$ (the pinhole) and projects onto the image plane at $p(x,y,f)$, where $f$ is the focal length.

**2. Derivation (4 marks):**

Using similar triangles on the triangle formed by $P$, $O$, and $p$:

$$
\dfrac{x}{f} = \dfrac{X}{Z} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad x = f\dfrac{X}{Z}
$$

$$
\dfrac{y}{f} = \dfrac{Y}{Z} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad y = f\dfrac{Y}{Z}
$$

In pixel coordinates $(u,v)$:

$$
u = f_x\dfrac{X}{Z} + c_x,\qquad v = f_y\dfrac{Y}{Z} + c_y
$$

where $f_x, f_y$ are focal lengths in pixels and $c_x, c_y$ the principal point.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**(i) Standard Deviation:**

$$
\sigma_r = 0.05 + 0.02 \times 5.0 = 0.05 + 0.10 = 0.15\ \text{m}
$$

**(ii) 95% Confidence Interval:**

$$
\text{CI}_{95\%} = 5.0 \pm 1.96 \times 0.15 = [4.706,\ 5.294]\ \text{m}
$$

**(iii) Probability that $R > 5.20$ m:**

Standardise:

$$
z = \dfrac{5.20 - 5.00}{0.15} = 1.333
$$

$$
P(R > 5.20) = 1 - \Phi(1.333) = 1 - 0.9088 = 0.0912
$$

$$
\boxed{P(R > 5.20) \approx 9.12\%}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting Jacobian in nonlinear propagation.** Using $\sigma_Z = \sigma_d$ instead of $J\sigma_d$ is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction.
> 2. **Misapplying the 95% rule.** It is **1.96 σ**, not 2 σ and not 3 σ. Examiners explicitly check this constant.
> 3. **Mixing PDF and CDF.** $p(z)$ is a *density*; probabilities come from integrating. Writing $P = p(5.20)$ earns zero marks.
> 4. **Ignoring units.** Always state $\sigma_Z$ in metres, not pixels. Unit errors are flagged immediately.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- ✅ Vision sensors are **passive, non-contact, 2-D projective** devices built on **CCD or CMOS** technology.
- ✅ Noise in a vision sensor has **three components**: shot (Poisson), dark (integration-time dependent), and readout (Gaussian).
- ✅ A sensor reading is **never just a number** — it is a random variable with an associated PDF.
- ✅ The **Gaussian (Normal)** PDF is the universal default for sensor uncertainty due to the Central Limit Theorem and tractability.
- ✅ Three hierarchical levels of uncertainty: **Scalar → Mean ± Std → Full PDF → Multivariate Gaussian**.
- ✅ For a 1-D Gaussian, 68.3% of values lie within $\pm 1\sigma$, 95.4% within $\pm 2\sigma$, 99.7% within $\pm 3\sigma$ (the **68–95–99.7 rule**).
- ✅ **Linear uncertainty propagation:** $\Sigma_y = J\,\Sigma_z\,J^\top$ where $J = \partial g/\partial z$ is the Jacobian.
- ✅ Stereo depth uncertainty **grows with the square of range**: $\sigma_Z \propto Z^2/d^2$.
- ✅ Pinhole projection: $u = f_x X/Z + c_x$, $v = f_y Y/Z + c_y$.
- ✅ For KTU problems, always show the **standardisation step** $z = (x-\mu)/\sigma$ explicitly — it is the mark-bearing line.
- ✅ Higher fidelity models: **Mixture of Gaussians** for ambiguous readings, **Particle filters** for non-Gaussian posteriors.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Error Propagation - Combining Uncertain Measurements. Case Study - Adding Sensors to the Robot Models in ROS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET744 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Error Propagation - Combining Uncertain Measurements. Case Study - Adding Sensors to the Robot Models in ROS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Error Propagation (Combining Uncertain Measurements)

**Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
*Error propagation* is the mathematical procedure used to determine the **uncertainty (variance / covariance)** of a derived quantity (e.g., a robot's pose, velocity, or distance) from the known uncertainties of the input measurements (e.g., wheel encoder ticks, LIDAR ranges, IMU accelerations). It is the cornerstone of **probabilistic robotics**, allowing the robot to maintain a valid belief about its state even when individual sensors are noisy.

**Physical Constants / Standard Metrics (Highlighted):**
- Standard deviation is denoted by **σ (sigma)**, variance by **σ²**.
- A confidence interval of **±1σ** covers ~**68.27%** of probable outcomes; **±2σ** covers ~**95.45%**; **±3σ** covers ~**99.73%** (Gaussian assumption).
- Sensor noise is conventionally described by the **covariance matrix Σ** (an n × n symmetric positive-definite matrix for an n-dimensional state).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In the KTU 2024 Mobile Robotics course, the *probabilistic robotics paradigm* of Thrun, Burgard, and Fox is the de-facto reference. Every measurement in a real robot is treated as a *random variable* with an associated probability distribution, **NOT** a deterministic scalar.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain English)

Imagine you are blindfolded and asked to estimate the **width of a table** using a wooden ruler that is slightly warped at one end. You take 5 measurements and they read: 80.1 cm, 79.8 cm, 80.5 cm, 80.0 cm, 79.6 cm.

* **The measurement itself** → Random variable **X** (the ruler's reading).
* **The noise** → Random variable **ε** (the warp, your hand jitter, parallax).
* **Error propagation** asks: *"If I now use this measurement to compute the area of the table (A = w × l) using another noisy measurement of length, how much do I trust my answer for A?"*

In mobile robotics, this is the **odometry problem**:
* You have noisy wheel encoders → you estimate position $(x, y, \theta)$.
* You have a noisy LIDAR → you estimate distance to walls.
* The robot must **fuse** (combine) these two streams of uncertain data into a single, *less-uncertain* belief about where it is.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Rule of Thumb:** Combining two independent, equally-noisy measurements of the same quantity does **not** give a 50% reduction in uncertainty. It gives a reduction by a factor of **1/√2 ≈ 30.7%** (because variances add in inverse: $\sigma_{combined}^2 = \frac{\sigma^2}{2}$, so $\sigma_{combined} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{2}}$). N sensors reduce variance by N, but standard deviation only by **√N**.

---

## 1.3 Adding Sensors to a Robot Model in ROS — Case Study Overview

In the **Robot Operating System (ROS)**, sensors are not "plug-and-play magic." They are attached to the robot model via:

1. **URDF / Xacro files** — declare physical links, joints, and sensor origins.
2. **Gazebo sensor plugins** — simulate physical sensor data (LIDAR, camera, IMU).
3. **`robot_state_publisher` node** — broadcasts the static TF tree (link → sensor frame).
4. **Sensor driver / ROS wrapper** — publishes `sensor_msgs/LaserScan`, `sensor_msgs/Image`, `sensor_msgs/Imu`, etc.
5. **RViz** — visualizes the sensor streams in real time on the 3D model.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is the TF tree critical?** Every sensor reading carries a header with a `frame_id` (e.g., `base_laser`, `imu_link`). For the robot to reason about *"Where, in the world frame, did the obstacle appear?"*, the transformation between the sensor frame and the world frame must be known. A broken TF chain = a useless measurement.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Error Propagation

The choice of formula depends on the nature of the function $f$ relating inputs to output:

| Case | Function Type | Formula Required | Use Case in Robotics |
|------|---------------|------------------|----------------------|
| 1 | Linear, single variable $y = a + b \cdot x$ | $\sigma_y^2 = b^2 \sigma_x^2$ | Converting encoder ticks to distance |
| 2 | Linear, multi-variable $y = \sum a_i x_i$ | $\sigma_y^2 = \sum a_i^2 \sigma_i^2$ | Summing multiple wheel velocities |
| 3 | Nonlinear, single variable $y = f(x)$ | $\sigma_y^2 \approx \left( \frac{df}{dx} \right)^2 \sigma_x^2$ | Inverse kinematics, distance from $d = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2}$ |
| 4 | Nonlinear, multi-variable $\mathbf{y} = f(\mathbf{x})$ | $\Sigma_y = \mathbf{J} \, \Sigma_x \, \mathbf{J}^T$ | **Full EKF / Pose estimation** |

> [!NOTE]
> The matrix form $\Sigma_y = \mathbf{J} \, \Sigma_x \, \mathbf{J}^T$ is the **single most important equation in probabilistic robotics**. It is the foundation of the **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)**, which is the expected answer for any 14-mark derivation in the KTU exam.

---

## 2.2 The Jacobian Matrix — The "Sensitivity Engine"

The **Jacobian** $\mathbf{J}_f$ of a vector-valued function $\mathbf{f} : \mathbb{R}^n \rightarrow \mathbb{R}^m$ is the $m \times n$ matrix of all first-order partial derivatives:

$$
\mathbf{J}_f(\mathbf{x}) = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{\partial f_1}{\partial x_1} & \frac{\partial f_1}{\partial x_2} & \cdots & \frac{\partial f_1}{\partial x_n} \\ \frac{\partial f_2}{\partial x_1} & \frac{\partial f_2}{\partial x_2} & \cdots & \frac{\partial f_2}{\partial x_n} \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ \frac{\partial f_m}{\partial x_1} & \frac{\partial f_m}{\partial x_2} & \cdots & \frac{\partial f_m}{\partial x_n} \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Intuition:** Each row tells you *"How much does output $i$ wiggle if I wiggle input $j$?"*. Squaring, weighting, and summing these sensitivities through the covariance matrix yields the output uncertainty.

---

## 2.3 Combining Two Independent Measurements — The Weighted Average

Suppose two independent sensors measure the **same scalar quantity** $z$:
* Sensor 1: measurement $z_1$ with variance $\sigma_1^2$
* Sensor 2: measurement $z_2$ with variance $\sigma_2^2$

The **optimal (minimum-variance unbiased) fused estimate** is:

$$
z_{fused} = \frac{\sigma_2^2}{\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2} z_1 + \frac{\sigma_1^2}{\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2} z_2
$$

$$
\sigma_{fused}^2 = \frac{\sigma_1^2 \, \sigma_2^2}{\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2} = \left( \frac{1}{\sigma_1^2} + \frac{1}{\sigma_2^2} \right)^{-1}
$$

**Physical meaning:** The sensor with **smaller variance gets the higher weight**. The fused variance is **always smaller** than either input variance. This formula is the **scalar special case of the Kalman Filter update step**.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Markdown Table)

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Units | Valid When |
|---|---------|---------|-------------------|------------|
| 1 | Linear 1-D propagation | $\sigma_y^2 = b^2 \sigma_x^2$ | $\sigma$ in SI units of $x, y$ | $y = a + bx$ |
| 2 | Sum of independent errors | $\sigma_y^2 = \sum_i \sigma_{x_i}^2$ | $x_i$ independent | $y = \sum x_i$ |
| 3 | Product / quotient | $\left( \frac{\sigma_y}{y} \right)^2 = \sum_i \left( \frac{\sigma_{x_i}}{x_i} \right)^2$ | Relative error | $y = \prod x_i^{a_i}$ |
| 4 | General (Jacobian) | $\Sigma_y = \mathbf{J} \Sigma_x \mathbf{J}^T$ | $\Sigma$ is covariance | Nonlinear $\mathbf{y} = f(\mathbf{x})$ |
| 5 | Fused measurement | $z_{fused} = w_1 z_1 + w_2 z_2$, $w_i = \sigma_i^{-2} / \sum \sigma_j^{-2}$ | Weights sum to 1 | Independent Gaussian |
| 6 | Fused variance | $\sigma_{fused}^2 = (\sigma_1^{-2} + \sigma_2^{-2})^{-1}$ | Always $\le \min(\sigma_1^2, \sigma_2^2)$ | Independent Gaussian |
| 7 | N-sensor variance reduction | $\sigma_N^2 = \sigma^2 / N$ | Same noise $\sigma$ | N i.i.d. sensors |
| 8 | EKF prediction | $\bar{\Sigma}_t = \mathbf{A}_t \Sigma_{t-1} \mathbf{A}_t^T + \mathbf{R}_t$ | $\mathbf{R}$ = process noise | Linearized motion |
| 9 | EKF measurement update | $K_t = \bar{\Sigma}_t \mathbf{C}_t^T (\mathbf{C}_t \bar{\Sigma}_t \mathbf{C}_t^T + \mathbf{Q}_t)^{-1}$ | $K$ = Kalman gain | Linearized sensor |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

* **Autonomous Vehicles (Waymo, Tesla):** Fuse GPS (±3 m), IMU (±0.01 m/s²), wheel odometry, and LIDAR to achieve lane-level accuracy (~10 cm).
* **Mars Rovers (NASA Curiosity / Perseverance):** Visual odometry + IMU fusion allows safe traversal on the Martian surface.
* **Surgical Robots (Da Vinci):** Tool-tip position is computed by fusing encoders, force sensors, and camera tracking — error propagation guarantees the surgeon stays within the safe-tissue envelope.
* **AR/VR Headsets (Meta Quest, Apple Vision Pro):** Inside-out tracking fuses camera + IMU at 1000 Hz using EKF derivatives of the Jacobian propagation formula.
