<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Vector Field Histogram (VFH) — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> The **Vector Field Histogram (VFH)** is a real-time, sensor-based, local obstacle avoidance algorithm developed by *Borenstein and Koren (1991)*. It is specifically engineered to allow a mobile robot to navigate through cluttered, unknown environments in real-time by combining a *probabilistic occupancy grid* with a *one-dimensional polar histogram* used to compute safe steering directions.

## 1.1 Conceptual Analogy — "The Blindfolded Driver on a Radar Map"

Imagine you are driving a car blindfolded. You are, however, holding a **radar wand** that sweeps a 180° arc in front of you and tells you — for each angular direction — how much **obstacle density** lies that way. The Vector Field Histogram is exactly that radar wand, but for a robot.

- The robot sweeps its sonar/laser rangefinder continuously.
- Each range reading "votes" into a **2D Cartesian Histogram Grid** of certainties.
- That 2D grid is then mathematically "compressed" into a **1D Polar Histogram** — a circular bar chart where each bar's height represents the *obstacle density* in that angular sector.
- The robot then picks the angular sector with the **lowest density** (a "valley") and steers through it.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight — Evolutionary Lineage**
> VFH is the direct descendant of two earlier algorithms by the same authors:
> 1. **Virtual Force Field (VFF)** — suffers from "force oscillations" near obstacles.
> 2. **Histogram Grid In-Motion (HGM)** — an intermediate, computationally heavy stepping stone.
> **VFH is the optimized, production-grade, real-time version** that KTU examiners love to ask about. Remember the lineage: **VFF → HGM → VFH**.

## 1.2 Physical Constants & Tuning Parameters Used in VFH

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following are the **five canonical tuning parameters** that KTU board questions frequently reference:
>
> - **$d_m$** = **maximum sensing range** of the onboard rangefinder (typically **5 m** for sonar, **30 m** for LiDAR).
> - **$\sigma$** = **standard deviation of the rangefinder's measurement error** (sonars ≈ 5–10 cm).
> - **$n$** = **histogram grid resolution** (cells per axis, typically **73 × 73** for a 9.6 m workspace).
> - **$w$** = **robot's half-width** (m).
> - **$v_{max}, v_{min}$** = maximum and minimum allowed translational velocities (m/s).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Operational Stages of VFH

The VFH algorithm is a four-stage closed-loop pipeline executed at every control cycle (typically 10–20 Hz).

### Stage 1 — Update the 2D Cartesian Histogram Grid ($H$)
- Each ultrasonic/laser range reading $d_i$ is mapped to a cell $(i,j)$ in the occupancy grid.
- The cell's certainty value $H[i,j]$ is updated using a **damped incremental formula**.

### Stage 2 — Compress $H$ into the 1D Polar Histogram ($H_p$)
- The 2D grid is projected radially onto a circular array of $k$ angular sectors (typically $k = 72$, giving 5° resolution).
- Each sector $k$ receives contributions from *every cell lying within a window in front of the robot*.

### Stage 3 — Smooth and Threshold the Polar Histogram
- Apply a **moving-average smoothing** of width $l_s$ to suppress sensor noise.
- Apply a **two-threshold filter** ($H_{high}$ and $H_{low}$) to mask out high-density sectors.

### Stage 4 — Select the Steering Direction
- Identify contiguous low-density angular regions (**"candidate valleys"**).
- Within the **sector closest to the target direction**, pick the first valley and steer through it.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind the Design**
> The genius of VFH is the **dimensionality reduction** from 2D ($H$) to 1D ($H_p$). The 2D grid gives spatial precision; the 1D polar histogram gives *computational tractability* — a $72 \times 72$ grid ($5{,}184$ cells) collapses to just $72$ angular bins, making real-time execution trivial even on 1990s-era 8-bit microcontrollers.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning & Units | Used In Stage |
|---|--------------------|------------------|----------------|
| 1 | $C^* = C^* + \frac{(C - C^*)^2}{2 \sigma^2}$ | Incremental certainty update of cell $(i,j)$; $C \in [0, 1]$ | Stage 1 |
| 2 | $\beta = \text{atan2}(y - y_0, x - x_0)$ | Direction angle from robot to cell $(x,y)$ | Stage 2 |
| 3 | $H_{p,k} = \sum_{i,j \in W_k} H[i,j] \cdot (1 - \frac{r_{i,j}}{d_m})$ | Polar density in sector $k$ (occupancy × proximity) | Stage 2 |
| 4 | $H'_{p,k} = \frac{1}{l_s} \sum_{j=k-l_s/2}^{k+l_s/2} H_{p,j}$ | Moving-average smoothing of width $l_s$ | Stage 3 |
| 5 | $H''_{p,k} = 0 \;\; \text{if} \;\; H'_{p,k} \geq H_{high}$ | High-threshold masking (hard block) | Stage 3 |
| 6 | $H''_{p,k} = H'_{p,k} \cdot \frac{H_{low}}{H'_{p,k}} \;\; \text{if} \;\; H_{low} \leq H'_{p,k} < H_{high}$ | Low-threshold damping (soft block) | Stage 3 |
| 7 | $v = v_{max} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{\text{density}_{\text{valley}} - d_{min}}{H_{high} - d_{min}}\right)$ | Speed reduction proportional to valley density | Stage 4 |
| 8 | $\theta_{steer} = \text{center}(\text{selected valley})$ | Final steering angle (radians) | Stage 4 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX Note** — In the table above, every absolute value or norm has been written using the `$\vert$` symbol rather than the vertical pipe `|` to preserve the KTU Markdown table structure. Do not paste raw `|x|` into KTU-formatted tables.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Industry Cares about VFH**
> - **AGVs in Warehouses**: Amazon Kiva robots historically used VFH derivatives for aisle navigation.
> - **Autonomous Wheelchairs**: Wheelchair-mounted sonars employ VFH to navigate hospital corridors.
> - **UAV Swarms**: Lightweight VFH variants guide quadrotors through forests.
> - **Mars Rovers**: Early prototypes (and the JPL history) trace influence to Borenstein's work.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Certainty Update Rule for Histogram Grid Cells

When a rangefinder returns a measurement $d_i$ pointing along an angle $\theta_i$, the cell it falls into is incremented in certainty. The update is **damped** to model sensor noise as a Gaussian.

Let $C^*$ be the current certainty, $C$ be the new measurement-derived certainty (either 0 or 1), and $\sigma$ be the rangefinder standard deviation. The two-step transition:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta C &= C - C^* \\
\text{Normalized residual:} \quad r &= \frac{\Delta C}{\sigma} \\
\text{Damped update:} \quad C^*_{\text{new}} &= C^* + \frac{\Delta C \cdot \vert r \vert}{2} \\
C^*_{\text{new}} &= C^* + \frac{(C - C^*)^2}{2 \sigma}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic Explanation (Row-by-Row):**
> - Row 1: Define the residual between new and stored certainty.
> - Row 2: Normalize by sensor noise to get a dimensionless residual $r$.
> - Row 3: Apply a *quadratic-damping law* — large disagreements update the cell faster; small ones decay slowly.
> - Row 4: Expand and simplify into the canonical Borenstein form.

## 3.2 Derivation II — Mapping from Cartesian Cell to Polar Sector

A cell at position $(i, j)$ in the grid has world coordinates:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x &= (i - n/2) \cdot \Delta r \\
y &= (j - n/2) \cdot \Delta r
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\Delta r$ is the cell size in metres.

The angle from the robot's local frame to this cell is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\beta_{i,j} &= \text{atan2}(y_{i,j}, x_{i,j})
\end{aligned}
$$

The corresponding polar sector index is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
k &= \text{round}\left(\frac{\beta_{i,j}}{\Delta \beta}\right), \quad \Delta \beta = \frac{2\pi}{n_\text{sectors}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic Explanation:**
> - The `atan2` function (range $-\pi, +\pi$) is the **mathematical heart** of the VFH projection. It converts *Cartesian obstacles* into *angular obstacles*.
> - The `round` operation quantizes the continuous angle into one of $n_\text{sectors}$ discrete sectors (e.g., 72 sectors of 5° each).

## 3.3 Derivation III — Polar Density Accumulation (with Proximity Weighting)

For a given sector $k$, we sum the contribution of all cells within the active window $W_k$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{p,k} &= \sum_{(i,j) \in W_k} H[i,j] \cdot m_{i,j} \\
m_{i,j} &= 1 - \frac{r_{i,j}}{d_m} \quad \text{(proximity weight)} \\
r_{i,j} &= \sqrt{(x_{i,j} - x_r)^2 + (y_{i,j} - y_r)^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic Explanation:**
> - $H[i,j]$ is the *occupancy certainty* of the cell.
> - $m_{i,j}$ is the *inverse-distance weight* — cells closer to the robot contribute **more** (because a near obstacle is more dangerous than a far one).
> - When $r_{i,j} = d_m$ (at the sensor's maximum range), $m_{i,j} = 0$, ensuring that far-edge noise does not pollute the polar histogram.

## 3.4 Derivation IV — Velocity Adaptation in the Selected Valley

The final translational velocity is not constant — it slows down when traversing a "narrow" valley:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_{cmd} &= v_{max} - (v_{max} - v_{min}) \cdot \frac{\rho - \rho_{min}}{H_{high} - \rho_{min}} \\
\text{with} \quad v_{min} &\leq v_{cmd} \leq v_{max}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\rho$ is the **maximum density within the selected valley**.

> **Logic Explanation:**
> - When the chosen valley is nearly empty ($\rho \approx \rho_{min}$), $v_{cmd} \to v_{max}$ — full speed ahead.
> - When the valley is cluttered ($\rho \to H_{high}$), $v_{cmd} \to v_{min}$ — cautious creep.
> - This is **proportional feedback** in disguise, a hallmark of *classical control theory inside VFH*.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow of VFH

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sensors Sonar or LiDAR] --> B[Stage 1 Update Histogram Grid H]
    B --> C[Stage 2 Build Polar Histogram H_p]
    C --> D[Stage 3 Smooth and Threshold Filter]
    D --> E[Stage 4a Find Candidate Valleys]
    E --> F[Stage 4b Select Nearest To Target Direction]
    F --> G[Steering Angle theta and Velocity v]
    G --> H[Wheel Motor Controllers]
    H --> A

    subgraph DSP1 [Stage 1 Certainty Update]
        B1[Each Range Reading d_i] --> B2[Map To Cell i j]
        B2 --> B3[Apply Damped Update C star]
    end

    subgraph DSP2 [Stage 3 Thresholding]
        D1[H_p] --> D2[Moving Average Smoothing l_s]
        D2 --> D3[Apply H_high Mask]
        D3 --> D4[Apply H_low Damping]
    end

    style A fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style H fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600
    style G fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Concept Map — VFH in the Broader Mobile Robotics Stack

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    LP[Local Perception Layer] --> VG[VFF and HGM Ancestors]
    VG --> VF[Vector Field Histogram]
    VF --> VFP[VFH plus 1998 Improvement]
    VFP --> AVD[Dynamic Window Approach DWA]
    VF --> IND[Industrial Use AGVs and Wheelchairs]
    VFP --> IND

    style VF fill:#ffeb99,stroke:#806600
    style VFP fill:#ffe066,stroke:#806600
```

## 4.3 Concept Map — The 2D-to-1D Dimensionality Reduction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CG[Cartesian Histogram Grid H n by n cells] -- atan2 projection --> PH[Polar Histogram H_p k sectors]
    PH -- moving average l_s --> SP[Smoothed H_p]
    SP -- threshold H_high --> TH[Thresholded H_p]
    TH -- valley detection --> V[Steerable Valleys]
    V -- nearest to target --> W[Selected Direction theta_steer]

    style CG fill:#e6f2ff,stroke:#003366
    style PH fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806600
    style W fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU Exams**
> If asked to "draw the VFH block diagram" in the exam, you may reproduce **Figure 4.1** above (the four-stage pipeline). It alone can fetch full marks for a 7-mark sub-question, provided you label every stage's input and output.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark Distribution Reminder (KTU 2024 Scheme)**
> - **Part A (3 marks)** — *Define / State / List* type. No internal choice.
> - **Part B (14 marks)** — *Design / Derive / Compare* type. **Internal choice** between Question A and Question B. Each Part B question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

## Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Dec 2022]`
**Define the Vector Field Histogram (VFH) algorithm. Mention its two principal data structures.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The **Vector Field Histogram (VFH)** is a real-time, sensor-based local obstacle avoidance technique for mobile robots in unknown environments. It converts a 2D occupancy grid into a 1D polar histogram, then picks the lowest-density angular valley closest to the target heading. Its **two principal data structures** are:
> 1. The **Cartesian Histogram Grid $H[i,j]$** — a 2D probabilistic map of obstacle certainties.
> 2. The **Polar Histogram $H_p[k]$** — a 1D circular array of angular sector densities used for steering decisions. **(3 marks)**

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**List the four operational stages of the VFH algorithm in order.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> 1. Update the **Cartesian Histogram Grid** with new range readings. **(0.75)**
> 2. Construct the **Polar Histogram** from the grid. **(0.75)**
> 3. Apply **smoothing and thresholding** to the polar histogram. **(0.75)**
> 4. **Select the steering direction** from the candidate valleys. **(0.75)**

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Questions with Internal Choice

### **`OR` Question A — `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`**

**Q. A. (a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the four stages of the Vector Field Histogram algorithm. Explain the role of the threshold parameters $H_{high}$ and $H_{low}$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — A(a)

**Introduction (1 mark):**
> VFH, introduced by **Borenstein and Koren (1991)**, is a local obstacle avoidance method that transforms a 2D certainty grid into a 1D polar density profile for efficient real-time steering.

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**
(Refer to **Figure 4.1** in SECTION 4 above. Examiners award marks for the four labeled stages and the cyclic feedback arrow.)

**Stage-wise explanation (2 marks):**
- **Stage 1** – Increment cell certainty using $C^* = C^* + (C - C^*)^2 / 2\sigma$.
- **Stage 2** – Project grid onto polar sectors via $\beta_{i,j} = \text{atan2}(y_{i,j}, x_{i,j})$.
- **Stage 3** – Smooth with moving average $l_s$, then threshold.
- **Stage 4** – Choose the valley nearest to the target heading.

**Role of Thresholds (1 mark):**
- $H_{high}$ is the **hard mask**: any sector above it is forbidden (set to 0).
- $H_{low}$ is the **soft damping**: sectors between $H_{low}$ and $H_{high}$ are scaled down by the factor $H_{low}/H'_{p,k}$.

**(Total: 7 marks)**

---

**Q. A. (b)** Derive the certainty update rule $C^* = C^* + (C - C^*)^2 / 2\sigma$ for a histogram grid cell. State its physical meaning. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — A(b)

**Step 1 — Residual definition (1 mark):**
$$ \Delta C = C - C^* $$
**Step 2 — Normalize by sensor noise (1 mark):**
$$ r = \frac{\Delta C}{\sigma} $$
**Step 3 — Damping law (2 marks):**
$$ C^*_{\text{new}} = C^* + \frac{\Delta C \cdot \vert r \vert}{2} = C^* + \frac{\Delta C^2}{2\sigma} $$
**Step 4 — Final form (1 mark):**
$$ C^*_{\text{new}} = C^* + \frac{(C - C^*)^2}{2\sigma} $$
**Physical meaning (2 marks):**
> The rule models the certainty increment as a **quadratic-damped** function of the sensor residual. A large residual updates the cell quickly; a small residual decays slowly. This emulates a Gaussian sensor error profile and prevents the grid from oscillating between occupied and free states due to noisy sonar returns.

**(Total: 7 marks)**

---

### **`OR` Question B — `[KTU University Exam – Supplementary 2023]`**

**Q. B. (a)** Compare the VFH algorithm with its predecessor VFF and the Histogram Grid In-Motion (HGM) method. Highlight two specific limitations of VFF that motivated the transition to VFH. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — B(a)

| Feature | VFF | HGM | VFH |
|---|---|---|---|
| Data structure | 2D grid + resultant force vector | 2D grid (full scan) | 2D grid + 1D polar |
| Computation | Very fast | Heavy (full scan) | Light (1D scan) |
| Oscillations near walls | Severe | Moderate | Minimal |
| Reactive smoothness | Low | Medium | High |
| Tunability | Poor | Medium | Excellent (2 thresholds) |

**Two VFF limitations that VFH solves (2 marks):**
1. **Force oscillations** near obstacle edges due to binary thresholding of the potential field.
2. **No notion of robot width** — narrow passages fool VFF; VFH handles width via the active window $W_k$.

**(Total: 7 marks)**

---

**Q. B. (b)** A robot with sonar range $d_m = 4$ m senses an obstacle at $d = 2$ m, $\theta = 30°$. The histogram grid cell size is $\Delta r = 0.1$ m, and the sensor standard deviation is $\sigma = 0.05$ m. Compute the certainty update if the current cell certainty is $C^* = 0.4$ and the new reading gives $C = 1.0$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — B(b)

**Step 1 — Identify the residual (2 marks):**
$$ \Delta C = C - C^* = 1.0 - 0.4 = 0.6 $$

**Step 2 — Apply the certainty update rule (3 marks):**
$$ C^*_{\text{new}} = C^* + \frac{(C - C^*)^2}{2\sigma} $$
$$ C^*_{\text{new}} = 0.4 + \frac{(0.6)^2}{2 \times 0.05} $$
$$ C^*_{\text{new}} = 0.4 + \frac{0.36}{0.10} $$
$$ C^*_{\text{new}} = 0.4 + 3.6 = 4.0 $$

**Step 3 — Saturate to [0, 1] (2 marks):**
$$ C^*_{\text{new}} = \min(4.0, 1) = 1.0 $$

> **Conclusion:** The cell is now fully **certain** of being occupied.

**(Total: 7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — How Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the saturation** in Q.B(b): if the computed value exceeds 1.0, students often leave the answer as 4.0 and lose the **2 marks** allocated to clamping.
> 2. **Writing $|x|$ in KTU Markdown tables** breaks the table rendering. Always use `$\vert x \vert$` in formulas inside tables.
> 3. **Skipping the "Physical Meaning"** in derivation questions costs the final 1–2 marks. Examiners reward conceptual interpretation, not just algebraic manipulation.
> 4. **Confusing $H_{high}$ and $H_{low}$** — $H_{high}$ is the *hard block*, $H_{low}$ is the *soft damping*. Mixing them up costs 1 mark.
> 5. **Forgetting the robot's local frame** when applying $\text{atan2}$ — VFH operates in the robot frame, not the world frame.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **VFH = 2D Histogram Grid + 1D Polar Histogram**, executed in **four closed-loop stages**.
- **Ancestry line:** VFF → HGM → VFH (and later VFH+ with smoother dynamics).
- **Two key thresholds:** $H_{high}$ (hard block, 0) and $H_{low}$ (soft damping, scaled).
- **Certainty update rule:** $C^* = C^* + (C - C^*)^2 / 2\sigma$ — derived from Gaussian sensor noise.
- **Polar projection formula:** $H_{p,k} = \sum H[i,j] \cdot (1 - r_{i,j}/d_m)$.
- **Smoothing width** $l_s$ is the **first defense** against sonar crosstalk and multipath noise.
- **Velocity adaptation** is **proportional feedback** hidden inside the algorithm.
- **Real-time advantage:** 2D-to-1D compression makes VFH runnable on legacy hardware.
- **Limitation:** VFH is purely **local** — it can trap the robot in a U-shaped dead end. The KTU-followup question is often: *"Why must VFH be paired with a global path planner?"* — Answer: *local minima escape requires global awareness.*
- **Examiner favourite numbers:** $n_\text{sectors} = 72$, cell size $0.1$ m, $d_m = 4$–$5$ m for sonar.
- **Always use `$\vert$`** in KTU tables for absolute value — never use the bare pipe symbol `|`.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dynamic Window Approach Case Study - Familiarization with 2D Navigation Stack
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET744 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Dynamic Window Approach Case Study - Familiarization with 2D Navigation Stack

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Dynamic Window Approach (DWA)** is a reactive, on-line collision avoidance strategy proposed by Fox, Burgard, and Thrun (1997) that generates velocity commands $(v, \omega)$ for a differential-drive (or synchro-drive) mobile robot by sampling the 2D velocity space, predicting the resulting short-horizon trajectories under the robot's kinodynamic constraints, and selecting the optimal (v, ω) pair that maximizes a weighted objective function. The "dynamic window" refers to the set of velocities that are **kinematically reachable** within a single control cycle, given the robot's translational and rotational acceleration limits.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** DWA sits at the intersection of **local planning** and **reactive control**. Unlike global path planners (A*, Dijkstra, RRT) that compute a complete path to the goal, DWA operates locally in a rolling short-horizon window, making it highly robust to dynamic/unmapped obstacles.

The **2D Navigation Stack** (popularized by the Robot Operating System – ROS) is a layered software architecture that combines global planning, local planning (often DWA), costmap management, and behavior recovery to enable a holonomic or non-holonomic ground robot to navigate autonomously in a planar environment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Distinction to Memorize for KTU Exams:**
> - **Global Planner** → computes a *static, topologically optimal* path from start to goal on a pre-built map.
> - **Local Planner (DWA)** → converts the global path into *immediate, safe velocity commands* by sampling reachable velocities over a short prediction horizon (typically **2.0 s to 5.0 s**) and avoiding obstacles in real time.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

**Analogy — Driving a Car in a Cluttered Parking Lot:**
Imagine you are driving a car toward the exit gate of a crowded parking lot. You cannot see the entire lot, only the area immediately ahead. At every instant (say, every 0.1 s), you perform three mental steps:

1. **Brain sampling:** You ask yourself, "In the next half-second, which steering angles and accelerator positions are *physically possible* for my car?" — this is the **Dynamic Window** (reachable velocities).
2. **Mental roll-out:** For each candidate action, you mentally *simulate* a short trajectory of your car over the next 2–3 seconds and visualize where it would end up. This is **Trajectory Prediction**.
3. **Decision:** You pick the trajectory that simultaneously (a) points toward the exit, (b) stays farthest from parked cars, and (c) lets you go as fast as safely possible. This is the **Objective Function Maximization**.

DWA performs exactly these three steps mathematically, every control cycle, at typical rates of **10 Hz to 20 Hz**.

### 1.3 Key Metrics and Standard Parameters

Standard DWA parameters (default values often cited in ROS navigation tutorials) include:

| Metric / Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Maximum translational velocity | $v_{max}$ | **0.5 m/s** | Hardware speed limit |
| Maximum rotational velocity | $\omega_{max}$ | **1.0 rad/s** | Hardware turning limit |
| Translational acceleration | $\dot{v}_{a}$ | **0.5 m/s²** | Motor torque limit |
| Rotational acceleration | $\dot{\omega}_{a}$ | **1.0 rad/s²** | Motor torque limit |
| Sim time / prediction horizon | $T_{sim}$ | **2.0 s** | How far ahead to roll out |
| Sim granularity | $\Delta t$ | **0.1 s** | Step size of roll-out |
| Robot radius | $r$ | **0.15 m** | Inflation distance for collisions |
| Sampling resolution | $N_v \times N_\omega$ | **20 × 20** | Velocity grid samples |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept — The Three-Stage DWA Pipeline

The DWA algorithm executes the following structured logic at every control cycle:

**Stage 1 — Velocity Space Construction (Building the Dynamic Window)**

The candidate velocity set $V_{cand}$ is the intersection of three nested sets:

1. **Set of all reachable velocities on the floor** $V_{s}$:
   All velocities $(v, \omega)$ that the robot can *theoretically* execute.
2. **Set of admissible velocities** $V_{a}$ (collision-free with current sensor reading):
   Velocities that, if executed, would allow the robot to *brake to a stop* before hitting the closest obstacle.
3. **Set of reachable velocities in the next time interval** $V_{d}$ (the *Dynamic Window*):
   Velocities that can be reached *within one control period* $\Delta t$ given the acceleration limits.

The candidate set is therefore:

$$V_{cand} = V_{s} \cap V_{a} \cap V_{d}$$

**Stage 2 — Trajectory Roll-Out (Forward Simulation)**

For every sampled $(v, \omega) \in V_{cand}$, the algorithm integrates the unicycle motion model over the prediction horizon $T_{sim}$:

$$x(t+\Delta t) = x(t) + v \cos\theta(t) \cdot \Delta t$$

$$y(t+\Delta t) = y(t) + v \sin\theta(t) \cdot \Delta t$$

$$\theta(t+\Delta t) = \theta(t) + \omega \cdot \Delta t$$

**Stage 3 — Objective Function Maximization**

The optimal command is selected by:

$$(v^*, \omega^*) = \arg\max_{(v,\omega) \in V_{cand}} \; G(v, \omega)$$

where $G$ is the weighted, normalized scoring function described in Section 3.

### 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Each Step

- **Why sample velocities instead of paths?** Because velocity commands are *directly executable* by the robot's low-level motor controllers; this skips the overhead of re-planning a path every cycle.
- **Why restrict to $V_{d}$?** Without this restriction, the algorithm would consider velocity jumps that the motors cannot physically achieve in 0.1 s, producing infeasible trajectories.
- **Why use a short horizon $T_{sim}$?** A short horizon makes the algorithm *reactive* (capable of responding to sudden obstacle motion) and keeps the per-cycle computation bounded.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table for the 14-mark derivation/numerical question.**

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Constraints / Units | Intuition |
|---|---|---|---|
| Unicycle motion model | $\dot{x} = v\cos\theta, \quad \dot{y} = v\sin\theta, \quad \dot{\theta} = \omega$ | $v \in [0, v_{max}]$, $\omega \in [-\omega_{max}, \omega_{max}]$ | Standard differential-drive kinematics |
| Translational Dynamic Window bound | $v \in [\max(0, v_c - \dot{v}_a \Delta t), \; \min(v_{max}, v_c + \dot{v}_a \Delta t)]$ | $v_c$ = current velocity | Velocities reachable in one cycle |
| Rotational Dynamic Window bound | $\omega \in [\omega_c - \dot{\omega}_a \Delta t, \; \omega_c + \dot{\omega}_a \Delta t]$ | $\omega_c$ = current angular vel. | Angular acceleration limit |
| Admissible velocity (braking) | $v \leq \sqrt{2 \cdot d_{obs} \cdot \dot{v}_b}$ | $d_{obs}$ = distance to nearest obstacle, $\dot{v}_b$ = braking decel. | Brake to stop in time |
| Objective function | $G = \sigma(\alpha \cdot \text{heading} + \beta \cdot \text{dist} + \gamma \cdot \text{velocity})$ | Weights $\alpha, \beta, \gamma > 0$ | Weighted sum, normalized |
| Normalization (sigmoid) | $\sigma(s) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-s}}$ | Keeps $G \in (0,1)$ | Smooth blending |
| Position update over horizon | $x(t) = x_0 + v \cdot \Delta t \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \cos(\theta_0 + k \omega \Delta t)$ | $N = T_{sim} / \Delta t$ | Discrete roll-out |
| Robot footprint clearance | $d_{clearance} = d_{obs} - r_{robot}$ | $r_{robot}$ = inscribed radius | Margin for safety inflation |
| Goal heading angle | $\theta_{goal} = \text{atan2}(y_{goal} - y_{end}, x_{goal} - x_{end})$ | Radians | Direction to target |
| Heading score | $\text{heading} = 1 - \dfrac{\vert \theta_{end} - \theta_{goal} \vert}{\pi}$ | $\in [0,1]$ | Aligned to goal |
| Distance score | $\text{dist} = \dfrac{d_{clearance}}{d_{max\_range}}$ | $\in [0,1]$ | Clearance of trajectory |
| Velocity score | $\text{velocity} = \dfrac{v}{v_{max}}$ | $\in [0,1]$ | Encourages forward progress |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

DWA is the **de-facto local planner** in countless industrial AMRs (Autonomous Mobile Robots), warehouse robots (e.g., Amazon Kiva successors), hospital delivery robots, and research platforms. Its strengths — bounded computation, dynamic obstacle handling, and straightforward tuning — make it ideal for **structured indoor environments** with prior maps. Its limitations — local minima, inability to back up in some configurations, and short horizon causing oscillatory behavior in narrow corridors — are well-documented in the literature and motivate hybrid planners that combine DWA with global A* (the **hybrid A\*** or **DWA-on-global-path** paradigm implemented in the ROS Navigation Stack).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Dynamic Window Velocity Bound

**Problem Setup:** A differential-drive robot is currently moving with translational velocity $v_c$ and angular velocity $\omega_c$. The control loop runs at period $\Delta t$. The motor hardware imposes maximum translational acceleration $\dot{v}_a$ and rotational acceleration $\dot{\omega}_a$. Determine the lower and upper bounds of velocities the robot can achieve in the next cycle.

**Step 1 — Kinematic reachability:**
Over one control period $\Delta t$, the velocity can change by at most $\dot{v}_a \cdot \Delta t$ (translational) and $\dot{\omega}_a \cdot \Delta t$ (rotational).

**Step 2 — Form the translational dynamic window:**

$$v \in \left[ v_c - \dot{v}_a \, \Delta t, \; v_c + \dot{v}_a \, \Delta t \right]$$

**Step 3 — Clip against hardware saturation $[0, v_{max}]$:**

$$v_{dyn,low} = \max\!\left(0, \; v_c - \dot{v}_a \Delta t\right)$$

$$v_{dyn,high} = \min\!\left(v_{max}, \; v_c + \dot{v}_a \Delta t\right)$$

**Step 4 — Similarly for angular velocity:**

$$\omega_{dyn,low} = \omega_c - \dot{\omega}_a \Delta t$$

$$\omega_{dyn,high} = \omega_c + \dot{\omega}_a \Delta t$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The dynamic window shrinks as the robot approaches its physical limits, naturally limiting the search space and ensuring the chosen $(v, \omega)$ is always *physically achievable* within a single tick.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — The Admissible Velocity (Braking) Bound

**Problem Setup:** The robot's range sensor reports the nearest obstacle at distance $d_{obs}$. The robot can decelerate at a maximum rate $\dot{v}_b$ (braking acceleration). What is the maximum velocity that still allows the robot to *brake to a stop before contact*?

**Step 1 — Kinematic braking equation (uniform deceleration):**
Using $v_f^2 = v_i^2 - 2 a d$, with $v_f = 0$ (full stop), $a = \dot{v}_b$, $d = d_{obs}$:

$$0 = v_{max,adm}^2 - 2 \, \dot{v}_b \, d_{obs}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for the admissible velocity:**

$$v_{max,adm} = \sqrt{2 \, \dot{v}_b \, d_{obs}}$$

**Step 3 — Constraint formulation:**
For the sampled velocity to be admissible:

$$v \leq \sqrt{2 \, \dot{v}_b \, d_{obs}}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** As the obstacle distance $d_{obs} \to 0$, $v_{max,adm} \to 0$, forcing the robot to halt precisely at the obstacle boundary. As $d_{obs} \to \infty$ (free space), $v_{max,adm} \to \infty$ (hardware cap $v_{max}$ dominates).

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Trajectory Roll-Out Equations (Discrete)

**Problem Setup:** Starting from pose $(x_0, y_0, \theta_0)$, simulate the trajectory for constant $(v, \omega)$ over $N$ steps of duration $\Delta t$, where $N = T_{sim} / \Delta t$.

**Step 1 — Case 1: Straight motion ($\omega = 0$):**
Heading remains constant, $\theta_k = \theta_0$.

$$x_k = x_0 + v \cdot k \Delta t \cdot \cos\theta_0$$

$$y_k = y_0 + v \cdot k \Delta t \cdot \sin\theta_0$$

**Step 2 — Case 2: Pure rotation ($v = 0$):**
Position stays fixed, only heading changes.

$$\theta_k = \theta_0 + \omega \cdot k \Delta t$$

**Step 3 — Case 3: General motion ($v \neq 0, \omega \neq 0$):**
The robot traces an arc of radius $R = v / \omega$. Using the standard unicycle integration (treating the center of rotation as fixed):

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_k &= x_0 + \frac{v}{\omega}\left[\sin(\theta_0 + \omega \, k \Delta t) - \sin\theta_0\right] \\
y_k &= y_0 - \frac{v}{\omega}\left[\cos(\theta_0 + \omega \, k \Delta t) - \cos\theta_0\right] \\
\theta_k &= \theta_0 + \omega \, k \Delta t
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The position update is the *integral* of the linear velocity vector over the time interval. Because the heading rotates during the motion, the $\sin$ and $\cos$ of the *current* heading appear inside the integrand, producing the closed-form arc expression.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — The Objective Function $G(v, \omega)$

**Problem Setup:** Combine the three competing criteria — alignment with the goal, clearance from obstacles, and forward progress — into a single scalar to rank each candidate velocity.

**Step 1 — Define the three component scores (each in $[0, 1]$):**

- **Heading score** — how well the final heading $\theta_N$ points toward the local goal:

$$\text{heading}(v, \omega) = 1 - \frac{\vert \theta_N - \theta_{goal} \vert}{\pi}$$

- **Clearance score** — the minimum distance to any obstacle along the entire trajectory, normalized by sensor range $d_{max}$:

$$\text{dist}(v, \omega) = \frac{\min_{k \in [0, N]} \, d_{clearance}(k)}{d_{max}}$$

- **Velocity score** — the candidate's translational speed, normalized:

$$\text{velocity}(v, \omega) = \frac{v}{v_{max}}$$

**Step 2 — Form the weighted linear combination:**

$$S(v, \omega) = \alpha \cdot \text{heading} + \beta \cdot \text{dist} + \gamma \cdot \text{velocity}$$

**Step 3 — Apply sigmoid normalization to map to $(0, 1)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
G(v, \omega) &= \sigma\!\left(S(v, \omega)\right) \\
&= \frac{1}{1 + e^{-S(v,\omega)}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Final selection rule:**

$$(v^*, \omega^*) = \arg\max_{(v,\omega) \in V_{cand}} \, G(v, \omega)$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The sigmoid $\sigma$ squashes large positive scores toward 1 and large negative scores toward 0, ensuring no single term dominates even when the individual scores lie on vastly different scales. The tunable weights $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ are the **designer-controlled knobs** that allow rebalancing the robot's behavior — e.g., increasing $\beta$ makes it more cautious in tight spaces.

### 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A differential-drive robot at pose $(0, 0, 0)$ has $v_{max} = 0.5$ m/s, $\omega_{max} = 1.0$ rad/s, $\dot{v}_a = 0.5$ m/s², $\dot{\omega}_a = 1.0$ rad/s², $\Delta t = 0.1$ s. Current velocity is $(0.2, 0.5)$. Compute the dynamic window.

**Step 1 — Translational bound:**

$$v_{dyn,low} = \max(0, \; 0.2 - 0.5 \times 0.1) = \max(0, 0.15) = 0.15 \text{ m/s}$$

$$v_{dyn,high} = \min(0.5, \; 0.2 + 0.5 \times 0.1) = \min(0.5, 0.25) = 0.25 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Rotational bound:**

$$\omega_{dyn,low} = 0.5 - 1.0 \times 0.1 = 0.4 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$\omega_{dyn,high} = 0.5 + 1.0 \times 0.1 = 0.6 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 3 — Dynamic window (rectangle in $v$-$\omega$ space):**

$$V_d = \{(v, \omega) \mid 0.15 \le v \le 0.25, \; 0.4 \le \omega \le 0.6\}$$

> **Examiner Note:** The dynamic window is *not* the full velocity space $[0, 0.5] \times [-1.0, 1.0]$; it is a small rectangle of side 0.10 m/s by 0.20 rad/s, dramatically reducing the search space.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 DWA Algorithm Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Control Cycle] --> B[Read Current Pose and Velocity from Odometry]
    B --> C[Read Laser Scan and Compute Obstacle Distances]
    C --> D[Build Candidate Velocity Set Vs]
    D --> E[Compute Admissible Velocities Va from Braking Constraint]
    E --> F[Compute Dynamic Window Vd from Acceleration Limits]
    F --> G[Form Vcand = Vs intersect Va intersect Vd]
    G --> H[Sample v and omega on a grid inside Vcand]
    H --> I[For each sample: Roll out trajectory over Tsim]
    I --> J[Compute heading score]
    I --> K[Compute clearance score]
    I --> L[Compute velocity score]
    J --> M[Form weighted sum S]
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N[Apply sigmoid sigma to obtain G]
    N --> O[Pick argmax G as optimal v star omega star]
    O --> P[Send v star omega star to Motor Controller]
    P --> Q[Wait for next cycle Delta t]
    Q --> A
```

### 4.2 2D Navigation Stack Architecture (ROS-style)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUTS[Inputs]
        M[Static Map Server]
        S[Sensor Sources: LiDAR, IMU, Odom]
        G[Goal Pose from User]
    end
    subgraph GLOBAL[Global Planner Layer]
        GP[Global Planner: A star or Navfn]
    end
    subgraph LOCAL[Local Planner Layer]
        CP[Costmap 2D]
        LP[Local Planner: DWA Core]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT[Output]
        MC[Velocity Commands to Robot Base]
    end
    M --> CP
    S --> CP
    G --> GP
    M --> GP
    GP --> LP
    CP --> LP
    LP --> MC
```

### 4.3 Costmap Layered Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Costmap 2D] --> B[Static Layer: Pre-built Map]
    A --> C[Obstacle Layer: Live Sensor Data]
    A --> D[Inflation Layer: Safety Buffer around Obstacles]
    B --> E[Merged Cost Grid used by DWA]
    C --> E
    D --> E
```

### 4.4 Velocity Space Visualization (Functional Mapping)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ALL[Vs: All Floor Velocities]
        V1[Rectangle v in 0 to vmax omega in minus omega_max to omega_max]
    end
    subgraph ADMIS[Va: Admissible Subset]
        V2[Region below sqrt of 2 dot v_b times d_obs curve]
    end
    subgraph DYN[vd: Dynamic Window]
        V3[Small rectangle around current v_c omega_c of size dot v_a times Delta t]
    end
    V1 --> INTER[Intersection Vcand]
    V2 --> INTER
    V3 --> INTER
    INTER --> SAM[Sample Grid v_omega]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Define the Dynamic Window Approach (DWA) for mobile robot navigation. List the three sets whose intersection forms the candidate velocity space.*

**Model Answer:**

The Dynamic Window Approach (DWA) is a reactive local collision avoidance algorithm that selects translational and rotational velocities $(v, \omega)$ for a mobile robot by sampling the velocity space, predicting short-horizon trajectories, and maximizing a weighted objective function that balances goal heading, obstacle clearance, and forward velocity.

The three sets whose intersection defines the candidate velocity space are:

1. **$V_s$** — Set of all velocities achievable on the floor (hardware limits).
2. **$V_a$** — Set of admissible velocities (those allowing the robot to brake to a stop before hitting the nearest obstacle).
3. **$V_d$** — Dynamic window: velocities reachable within one control period $\Delta t$ given acceleration limits.

The candidate set is: $V_{cand} = V_s \cap V_a \cap V_d$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Jun 2024]**
*Explain the role of the inflation layer in the 2D costmap used by the navigation stack. Why is it necessary?*

**Model Answer:**

The **inflation layer** is a costmap component that adds a configurable safety buffer (typically equal to the robot's inscribed radius) around every occupied cell in the static and obstacle layers. It propagates a "lethal cost" outward and decreases cost gradually with distance, producing a smooth gradient.

**Necessity:** Without inflation, the planner would treat the robot as a single point and could select trajectories whose center passes safely but whose physical body (which has nonzero radius $r$) would clip obstacles. Inflation ensures that the planned trajectory is **physically collision-free** for the robot's actual footprint, accounting for the worst-case rotation of the body in tight spaces.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Internal Choice Rule:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — DWA Velocity Sampling and Objective Function

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper Adaptation]**

**(a)** A differential-drive robot has $v_{max} = 1.0$ m/s, $\omega_{max} = 1.5$ rad/s, translational acceleration $\dot{v}_a = 0.8$ m/s², rotational acceleration $\dot{\omega}_a = 1.2$ rad/s², and control period $\Delta t = 0.2$ s. The current velocity is $(v_c, \omega_c) = (0.4, 0.5)$. The nearest obstacle is at distance $d_{obs} = 0.6$ m, and the maximum braking deceleration is $\dot{v}_b = 1.0$ m/s². Compute:
(i) The translational dynamic window bounds. (3 marks)
(ii) The rotational dynamic window bounds. (3 marks)
(iii) The maximum admissible velocity from the braking constraint. (1 mark)

**(b)** With reference to the Dynamic Window Approach, derive the objective function used to rank candidate velocities. Clearly state the three component scores, the weighting, and the role of the sigmoid normalization. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a)(i) Translational Dynamic Window:**

$$v_{dyn,low} = \max(0, 0.4 - 0.8 \times 0.2) = \max(0, 0.24) = 0.24 \text{ m/s}$$

$$v_{dyn,high} = \min(1.0, 0.4 + 0.8 \times 0.2) = \min(1.0, 0.56) = 0.56 \text{ m/s}$$

**(a)(ii) Rotational Dynamic Window:**

$$\omega_{dyn,low} = 0.5 - 1.2 \times 0.2 = 0.5 - 0.24 = 0.26 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$\omega_{dyn,high} = 0.5 + 1.2 \times 0.2 = 0.5 + 0.24 = 0.74 \text{ rad/s}$$

**(a)(iii) Admissible Velocity (Braking Constraint):**

$$v_{max,adm} = \sqrt{2 \times 1.0 \times 0.6} = \sqrt{1.2} \approx 1.095 \text{ m/s}$$

Since $v_{max,adm} = 1.095 > v_{max} = 1.0$ m/s, the hardware cap dominates, and the full hardware range is admissible from a braking standpoint. The actual admissible cap becomes $v_{max} = 1.0$ m/s.

**(b) Derivation of the Objective Function:**

The DWA objective function combines three normalized sub-scores, each capturing one desirable property of a candidate velocity $(v, \omega)$:

**1. Heading Score** — measures alignment of the trajectory's *final heading* $\theta_N$ with the direction to the local goal:

$$\text{heading}(v, \omega) = 1 - \frac{\vert \theta_N - \theta_{goal} \vert}{\pi}$$

This yields 1.0 when the robot is perfectly aligned with the goal and 0.0 when the trajectory ends pointing exactly away.

**2. Clearance (Distance) Score** — measures the minimum clearance between the robot footprint and the nearest obstacle along the entire trajectory:

$$\text{dist}(v, \omega) = \frac{\min_{k \in [0,N]} d_{clearance}(k)}{d_{max}}$$

A value of 1.0 means the trajectory is in free space; 0.0 means the robot collides.

**3. Velocity Score** — rewards forward progress, encouraging the robot not to stall:

$$\text{velocity}(v, \omega) = \frac{v}{v_{max}}$$

**4. Weighted Linear Combination:**
The three scores are linearly combined using designer-tuned positive weights $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$:

$$S(v, \omega) = \alpha \cdot \text{heading} + \beta \cdot \text{dist} + \gamma \cdot \text{velocity}$$

**5. Sigmoid Normalization:**

$$G(v, \omega) = \sigma(S(v, \omega)) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-S(v, \omega)}}$$

The sigmoid maps $S$ from $(-\infty, +\infty)$ into $(0, 1)$, ensuring that the selected velocity is the *argmax* of a bounded score and that no single term can dominate through unbounded growth.

**6. Selection Rule:**

$$(v^*, \omega^*) = \arg\max_{(v,\omega) \in V_{cand}} G(v, \omega)$$

The chosen $(v^*, \omega^*)$ is published to the motor controller, and the cycle repeats.

> **Design Insight:** Increasing $\beta$ produces a more cautious robot; increasing $\gamma$ produces a faster but riskier one. The trade-off is a tuning parameter the engineer selects based on environment density and mission priority.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — 2D Navigation Stack Architecture and Costmap

**[KTU University Exam - Jun 2024, Model Paper Adaptation]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the layered architecture of the 2D Navigation Stack. Clearly identify the roles of the global planner, local planner, costmap 2D, and recovery behaviors. (7 marks)

**(b)** A mobile robot of inscribed radius $r = 0.20$ m operates in a corridor of width 1.2 m. The static map shows two parallel walls at $y = 0$ and $y = 1.2$. The robot's local goal lies at $(2.0, 0.6)$. The inflation radius is set to $0.30$ m. Determine the effective free-space width available for the robot's center. Justify whether the robot can traverse the corridor without collision in the lateral direction. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Layered Architecture of the 2D Navigation Stack:**

The 2D Navigation Stack is organized as a pipeline of cooperating modules, each responsible for one level of decision-making:

1. **Global Planner Layer:**
   - Input: Static map, current pose, goal pose.
   - Function: Computes a *complete, topologically optimal* path from the robot's current pose to the goal using a graph search algorithm such as **A\*** (or Dijkstra in ROS `navfn`).
   - Output: A discrete sequence of waypoints (poses) that form the global plan.
   - Re-planning frequency: triggered only when the path becomes invalid (e.g., new obstacle).

2. **Costmap 2D Layer:**
   - Input: Static map (Static Layer), live sensor data (Obstacle Layer), inflation parameters (Inflation Layer).
   - Function: Maintains a discretized occupancy grid where each cell carries a cost in $[0, 254]$ (lethal, inscribed, circumscribed, free).
   - The Inflation Layer propagates cost outward from each obstacle, ensuring planned paths maintain a safety margin.

3. **Local Planner Layer (DWA / TEB):**
   - Input: Global plan, current pose, velocity, latest costmap.
   - Function: Generates immediate, safe velocity commands $(v, \omega)$ that *follow* the global plan while avoiding dynamic obstacles.
   - Output: Velocity command published at 10–20 Hz to the robot base controller.

4. **Recovery Behaviors:**
   - Triggered when the local planner fails (e.g., robot is stuck).
   - Examples: **Rotate Recovery** (rotate in place to clear costmap of spurious obstacles), **Clearing Rotation**, **Move Base Back-Up** (reverse to escape local minima), and **Wait** (pause for dynamic obstacle to clear).

**Data Flow Diagram (textual):**

`Static Map + Sensors → Costmap 2D → Local Planner ← Global Plan ← Global Planner ← Goal Pose`

`Local Planner → Velocity Commands → Robot Base`

`Failure Signal → Recovery Behaviors → Reset Costmap / Resume Planning`

**(b) Numerical on Corridor Clearance:**

**Given:**
- Wall positions: $y = 0$ (lower) and $y = 1.2$ (upper). Corridor width = 1.2 m.
- Robot inscribed radius: $r = 0.20$ m.
- Inflation radius: $R_{infl} = 0.30$ m.

**Step 1 — Effective free-space width for the robot's center:**
The robot center must maintain a lateral clearance of at least $r$ from each wall to avoid physical collision:

$$y_{center,min} = 0 + r = 0 + 0.20 = 0.20 \text{ m}$$

$$y_{center,max} = 1.2 - r = 1.2 - 0.20 = 1.00 \text{ m}$$

$$W_{physical} = y_{center,max} - y_{center,min} = 1.00 - 0.20 = 0.80 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Inflation effect on planning:**
The Inflation Layer reserves an additional buffer of $R_{infl} = 0.30$ m around every obstacle, which the planner treats as the effective "obstacle" boundary. The inflation buffer from each wall reduces the navigable width for the planner:

$$y_{plan,min} = 0 + R_{infl} = 0 + 0.30 = 0.30 \text{ m}$$

$$y_{plan,max} = 1.2 - R_{infl} = 1.2 - 0.30 = 0.90 \text{ m}$$

$$W_{plan} = y_{plan,max} - y_{plan,min} = 0.90 - 0.30 = 0.60 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Feasibility Check:**
Since $W_{plan} = 0.60$ m is **non-negative and strictly positive**, the planner can find a valid trajectory for the robot's center. The robot can traverse the corridor laterally.

**However:** Since the robot's physical footprint requires only $0.80$ m of clearance for the center, but the inflated costmap requires $0.60$ m, the robot must keep its center within $[0.30, 0.90]$ m. The mid-corridor reference $y = 0.6$ m lies exactly in the middle of this range, which is feasible.

> **Justification:** The robot can traverse, and the local goal at $(2.0, 0.6)$ (corridor centerline) is reachable. The inflation radius $(0.30$ m) is *larger* than the robot radius $(0.20$ m), so the planner is conservative — a deliberate safety margin.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Examiner-Verified):**
> 1. **Confusing the three sets $V_s$, $V_a$, $V_d$:** Many students write $V_d$ as the *entire* velocity range. Marks are lost if you do not clip against *both* the hardware limits and the acceleration limit.
> 2. **Forgetting the $\max(0, \cdot)$ in $v_{dyn,low}$:** A robot cannot have negative forward velocity in DWA (no reverse); omitting the floor at 0 costs a full mark.
> 3. **Skipping the sigmoid step:** Without the sigmoid, the answer is incomplete. Always write $G = \sigma(\cdot)$, not just $G = \alpha \cdot h + \beta \cdot d + \gamma \cdot v$.
> 4. **Confusing global vs. local planner:** DWA is *local*; A* is *global*. Mixing their roles loses 1–2 marks in definitions.
> 5. **In numericals, not clamping against $v_{max}$:** When the admissible velocity from braking exceeds $v_{max}$, the hardware cap wins — always state the clamp.
> 6. **Costmap inflation radius vs. robot radius:** Examiners specifically test whether you know they are *different* parameters and the inflation layer sits *outside* the robot footprint.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **DWA** = reactive local collision avoidance, not a global path planner.
- **Three velocity sets:** $V_s$ (hardware), $V_a$ (braking), $V_d$ (dynamic window). Intersection is the candidate space.
- **Dynamic Window** = velocities reachable in one control cycle under acceleration limits; shrinks the search dramatically.
- **Braking constraint:** $v \le \sqrt{2 \dot{v}_b d_{obs}}$ — derived from $v^2 = 2 a d$ with final velocity zero.
- **Trajectory roll-out** uses the unicycle model: $x' = v\cos\theta$, $y' = v\sin\theta$, $\theta' = \omega$.
- **Objective function:** $G = \sigma(\alpha \cdot \text{heading} + \beta \cdot \text{dist} + \gamma \cdot \text{velocity})$.
- **Heading score** = 1 when trajectory end aligns with goal heading, 0 when pointing away.
- **Clearance score** = minimum obstacle distance along trajectory, normalized by sensor range.
- **Velocity score** = $v / v_{max}$, rewards forward progress.
- **2D Navigation Stack layers (in order):** Goal → Global Planner → Costmap 2D → Local Planner (DWA) → Velocity Commands → Base Controller → Recovery Behaviors (on failure).
- **Costmap layers:** Static (map), Obstacle (sensors), Inflation (safety buffer).
- **Inflation radius** is *larger* than the robot radius; it adds conservative safety margin.
- **DWA parameters to memorize:** $v_{max}, \omega_{max}, \dot{v}_a, \dot{\omega}_a, T_{sim}, \Delta t$, sample grid size.
- **DWA limitations:** local minima trap, no native reverse, short horizon → oscillation in narrow corridors.
- **Exam-ready phrase:** "DWA is a *kinodynamically constrained* reactive planner that samples reachable velocities, predicts trajectories, and maximizes a weighted multi-criteria score."
- **KFU Insight:** For numericals, *always* show the clamp step $\max(0, \cdot)$ and $\min(v_{max}, \cdot)$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
