## 4.3 Schematic Description — DDR Geometry

```
        World Frame Xw (East) ---->
                            ^
                            |
                            |   Yw (North)
                            |
                  ___________________________
                 /          Yr (lateral)       \
                |                              |
                |   <-- L (axle) -->           |
                |  O-----O------------O        |
                |   Wr   C            Wl       |
                |  (rear)            (rear)    |
                |    * (castor)               |
                 \__________________________/
                              |
                              v  Xr (forward heading = theta)
```

- The robot's centre $C$ is at $(x, y)$ in the world frame.
- The right wheel contact-point is at $(x - L/2 \sin\theta,\ y + L/2 \cos\theta)$.
- The left wheel contact-point is at $(x + L/2 \sin\theta,\ y - L/2 \cos\theta)$.
- $X_r$ points along the heading direction $\theta$ measured CCW from $X_w$.

## 4.4 Schematic Description — Bicycle / Car-Like Model

```
   Front wheel
       O  <-- (steering angle phi from longitudinal axis)
       |\
       | \
   L   |  \
       |   \
       |    \
       O-----O <-- Rear wheel (driving, non-steered)
       Rear  Centre at (x,y), heading theta
```

- The wheelbase $L$ is the distance between the two wheel contact patches.
- The instantaneous centre of rotation (ICR) lies on the line through the rear wheel perpendicular to its velocity, *and* on the line through the front wheel perpendicular to its steered velocity.
- The angle subtended at the ICR by the wheelbase is constant and equals $|\phi|$ (the bicycle approximation of Ackermann).

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram — Decision Flow for Choosing a Controller

```mermaid
graph TD
    Q["What is the control objective?"] --> Q1["Move to fixed goal pose"]
    Q --> Q2["Track time parameterized reference"]
    Q --> Q3["Follow geometric path"]
    Q1 --> A1["Brockett obstruction applies"]
    A1 --> A2["Use Discontinuous Feedback"]
    A1 --> A3["Use Time Varying Sinusoidal Law"]
    A1 --> A4["Use Polar Coordinates Method"]
    Q2 --> B1["Reference velocity vd greater than zero"]
    B1 --> B2["Use Kanayama Linearizing Law"]
    B1 --> B3["Use Backstepping or Sliding Mode"]
    Q3 --> C1["Use Path Projection Error"]
    C1 --> C2["Use Serret Frenet Frame Control"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Define the kinematic model of a unicycle robot and state its nonholonomic constraint.**

**Model Answer:**

A unicycle robot's kinematic model relates the body-frame control inputs $(v, \omega)$ to the world-frame pose rates $(\dot{x}, \dot{y}, \dot{\theta})$:

$$\dot{x} = v\cos\theta, \quad \dot{y} = v\sin\theta, \quad \dot{\theta} = \omega$$

where $(x, y)$ is the position of the contact point and $\theta$ is the heading angle.

**Nonholonomic constraint:** The wheel cannot slip sideways. In velocity form:

$$\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$$

This constraint is **non-integrable** (cannot be reduced to a position-level constraint), hence *nonholonomic*. It means the robot has only 2 controllable DOF (translation + rotation) in a 3D configuration space, making it under-actuated but reachable.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Differentiate between holonomic and nonholonomic constraints in mobile robotics with an example each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Holonomic | Nonholonomic |
|---|---|---|
| Form | Reducible to position-only: $f(q) = 0$ | Velocity-level only: $a(q)\dot{q} = 0$ |
| Integrability | Integrable | Non-integrable |
| Reduces DOF | Yes | No (in configuration space), but restricts velocity |
| Example | Fixed-length manipulator link | Wheel rolling without slip |
| Robot example | Omnidirectional mecanum wheel | Car, differential drive, unicycle |

**Holonomic example:** A 2-DOF planar manipulator with a rigid link of length $L$ — the constraint $(x^2 + y^2 - L^2 = 0)$ reduces the reachable configuration space.

**Nonholonomic example:** The no-sideways-slip constraint of a unicycle: $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$. It restricts *velocities* but the robot can still reach any $(x,y,\theta)$ (Chow's theorem).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — With Internal Choice

### Question A **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the kinematic model of a differential drive robot (DDR) starting from the no-slip rolling constraint. Clearly show how the wheel angular velocities $(w_l, w_r)$ are related to the linear and angular velocities $(v, \omega)$ of the robot. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A DDR has wheel radius $r = 0.05$ m and axle length $L = 0.30$ m. Compute the right and left wheel angular velocities required to (i) move in a straight line at 0.5 m/s, and (ii) rotate in place with angular velocity $\omega = 1.0$ rad/s. Verify both cases. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Step 1:** Define geometry. Two coaxial wheels of radius $r$, separated by axle length $L$. Wheel contact points are equidistant from the centre $C$ at $(x, y, \theta)$.

**Step 2:** No-slip rolling. Each wheel's contact-point velocity equals $r$ times its angular velocity:

$$v_l = r w_l, \quad v_r = r w_r$$

**Step 3:** Rigid body kinematics. The robot's centre translates at the mean of the two wheel velocities:

$$v = \frac{v_r + v_l}{2} = \frac{r}{2}(w_r + w_l)$$

**Step 4:** Angular velocity. The differential velocity between wheels creates rotation about the centre. Over time $dt$, the rotation angle is $d\theta = (v_r - v_l)dt/L$:

$$\omega = \frac{v_r - v_l}{L} = \frac{r}{L}(w_r - w_l)$$

**Step 5:** Combine with unicycle-like pose evolution:

$$\dot{x} = v\cos\theta, \quad \dot{y} = v\sin\theta, \quad \dot{\theta} = \omega$$

This constitutes the **DDR kinematic model**.

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Given:** $r = 0.05$ m, $L = 0.30$ m.

**(i) Straight line at $v = 0.5$ m/s, $\omega = 0$:**

$$w_r = \frac{1}{r}\!\left(v + \frac{L\omega}{2}\right) = \frac{0.5}{0.05} + \frac{0.30 \times 0}{2 \times 0.05} = 10 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$w_l = \frac{1}{r}\!\left(v - \frac{L\omega}{2}\right) = \frac{0.5}{0.05} - 0 = 10 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Verification:** $v = \tfrac{r}{2}(w_r + w_l) = 0.025 \times 20 = 0.5$ m/s ✓; $\omega = \tfrac{r}{L}(w_r - w_l) = 0.05/0.30 \times 0 = 0$ ✓.

**(ii) Rotate in place at $\omega = 1.0$ rad/s, $v = 0$:**

$$w_r = \frac{1}{r}\!\left(0 + \frac{0.30 \times 1.0}{2}\right) = \frac{0.15}{0.05} = 3 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$w_l = \frac{1}{r}\!\left(0 - \frac{0.30 \times 1.0}{2}\right) = -\frac{0.15}{0.05} = -3 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Verification:** $v = 0.025 \times (3 + (-3)) = 0$ ✓; $\omega = (0.05/0.30) \times (3 - (-3)) = (1/6) \times 6 = 1.0$ rad/s ✓.

The negative $w_l$ means the left wheel rotates **backward** while the right wheel rotates **forward** — the robot spins counter-clockwise about its own centre.

---

### Question B **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 — Internal Choice]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, derive the kinematic (bicycle) model of a car-like mobile robot with steering angle $\phi$ and wheelbase $L$. Hence obtain the expression for the minimum radius of curvature. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Explain Brockett's necessary condition for smooth stabilizability. Why does it preclude the existence of a continuous time-invariant state-feedback law for stabilizing a unicycle to a fixed goal pose? Mention two control strategies that overcome this obstruction. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Sketch:** (Refer to Section 4.4 — bicycle with rear driving wheel at $(x,y)$ heading $\theta$, and front steering wheel at angle $\phi$ from the longitudinal axis.)

**Derivation:**

The rear wheel imposes the no-slip constraint:

$$\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0 \quad \text{...(1)}$$

Let the rear wheel drive forward at linear velocity $v$. From (1) and rigid-body motion:

$$\dot{x} = v\cos\theta, \quad \dot{y} = v\sin\theta \quad \text{...(2)}$$

The front wheel at $(x + L\cos\theta,\ y + L\sin\theta)$ has steering angle $\phi$. Its no-slip constraint (velocity aligned with steered direction):

$$[\dot{x} - L\dot{\theta}\sin\theta]\sin(\theta + \phi) - [\dot{y} + L\dot{\theta}\cos\theta]\cos(\theta + \phi) = 0$$

Substituting (2) and simplifying using $\sin(A+B)\cos A - \cos(A+B)\sin A = \sin B$:

$$v\sin\phi - L\dot{\theta}\cos\phi = 0$$

Solving:

$$\dot{\theta} = \frac{v\tan\phi}{L}$$

**Final kinematic model:**

$$\dot{x} = v\cos\theta, \quad \dot{y} = v\sin\theta, \quad \dot{\theta} = \frac{v}{L}\tan\phi$$

**Minimum radius of curvature:** The ICR radius satisfies $\omega = v/R$, hence:

$$R = \frac{L}{\tan\phi}$$

For $|\phi| \leq \phi_{\max} < \pi/2$, the minimum radius is:

$$R_{\min} = \frac{L}{\tan\phi_{\max}}$$

For example, with $L = 2.5$ m and $\phi_{\max} = 35° \approx 0.611$ rad, $R_{\min} \approx 3.57$ m.

---

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Brockett's Necessary Condition (1983):**

For a control-affine system $\dot{q} = f(q) + \sum_{i=1}^m g_i(q) u_i$ with equilibrium at $q^* = 0$, if there exists a $C^1$ (continuously differentiable) time-invariant feedback $u = k(q)$ that asymptotically stabilizes $q^*$, then the **linearization** $(A, B)$ at $q^*$ must satisfy:

1. $A$ has no eigenvalues with strictly positive real part (Hurwitz-like), **and**
2. The image of $B$ must be contained in the closure of the reachable space from the linearized system — equivalently, $(A, B)$ must be stabilizable.

For nonholonomic systems in *Chow's bracket form* (e.g., the unicycle), the linearization at any equilibrium has the form:

$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad B = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

The linearized system has a non-trivial uncontrollable eigenvalue (the $y$-direction), so by Brockett's condition, **no smooth time-invariant feedback can asymptotically stabilize the origin**.

The deeper geometric reason: any smooth control law $u = k(q)$ with $k(0) = 0$ has differential $dk(0) = 0$ near the origin, which fails to generate the needed Lie-bracket motion $[g_1, g_2]$.

**Two control strategies that overcome Brockett's obstruction:**

1. **Discontinuous (or piecewise-continuous) state feedback.** Example: Astolfi's controller:
$$u_1 = -k_1(x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta)$$
$$u_2 = -k_2\,\text{sign}(\theta)\,\theta^2 - k_3\,\sin\theta \cdot \frac{y\cos\theta - x\sin\theta}{\sqrt{x^2+y^2}}$$
The $\text{sign}(\cdot)$ function introduces a discontinuity that provides the missing controllability at the equilibrium.

2. **Time-varying smooth feedback.** Use explicit time dependence:
$$v = k_1\sqrt{x^2 + y^2}\cos\theta, \quad \omega = k_2\theta + k_3 t \cdot \frac{y\cos\theta - x\sin\theta}{\sqrt{x^2+y^2}}$$
The sinusoidal time-variation ensures the closed-loop linearization at the origin has the rank-2 controllability needed for asymptotic stability.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Many students write only "Brockett's theorem says no smooth controller exists" without stating the *necessary* algebraic condition or explaining the underlying reason (failure of the Lie algebra condition at the linearization). **Loss: 2–3 marks.**
> 2. Students frequently confuse **stabilizability** with **controllability**. The unicycle is *controllable* (reachable) but *not smoothly stabilizable* (no smooth time-invariant stabilizer).
> 3. In the bicycle model derivation, students forget to *derive* the front-wheel nonholonomic constraint from scratch; simply quoting $\dot{\theta} = (v/L)\tan\phi$ without derivation loses marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Unicycle model** is the canonical reference: $\dot{x}=v\cos\theta,\ \dot{y}=v\sin\theta,\ \dot{\theta}=\omega$.
- **DDR forward kinematics**: $v = (r/2)(w_r+w_l)$ and $\omega = (r/L)(w_r-w_l)$.
- **DDR inverse kinematics**: $w_r = (v + L\omega/2)/r$ and $w_l = (v - L\omega/2)/r$.
- **Car-like (bicycle) model**: $\dot{\theta} = (v/L)\tan\phi$ — *couples* linear and angular motion.
- **Minimum turning radius** for car-like: $R_{\min} = L/\tan\phi_{\max}$.
- **Ackermann condition** ensures all four wheels share the same ICR.
- **Nonholonomic constraint**: $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$ — velocity-level, non-integrable.
- **Chow's theorem** ⇒ unicycle is controllable despite being under-actuated.
- **Brockett's theorem** ⇒ no smooth time-invariant feedback stabilizes a unicycle at a fixed pose.
- **Three control problems**: point stabilization, trajectory tracking, path following.
- **Kanayama's law**: $v = v_d\cos\theta_e + k_x x_e$ and $\omega = \omega_d + v_d(k_y y_e + k_\theta\sin\theta_e)$.
- **Lyapunov function** for trajectory tracking: $V = \tfrac{1}{2}(x_e^2 + y_e^2) + (1-\cos\theta_e)/k_y$.
- **Stability requires** $v_d > 0$ for Kanayama's law; for $v_d = 0$, use discontinuous / time-varying feedback.
- **DDR can rotate in place** ($R_{\min} = 0$); **car-like cannot** ($R_{\min} > 0$).
- **Differential flatness**: All three reference robots are differentially flat with flat outputs $(x, y)$, enabling flatness-based trajectory planning.
- **KTU 2024 emphasis**: Expect a 7-mark derivation question (DDR or bicycle), a 7-mark controller design (Kanayama or Brockett's theorem), and one 3-mark short question comparing robot types or stating constraints.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Control of Differential Drive Robot and Steered Robot Based on Its Kinematic Model. Case Study (Overview Only)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAET744 - MOBILE ROBOTICS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Control of Differential Drive Robot and Steered Robot Based on Its Kinematic Model. Case Study (Overview Only) # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Differential Drive Robot — Definition A **Differential Drive Robot (DDR)** is a non-holonomic wheeled mobile robot whose motion is generated by two independently actuated wheels mounted on a common axle. By commanding **different angular velocities** to the left and right wheels, the robot achieves translation and rotation simultaneously without a dedicated steering mechanism. **Pose Definition in the Global Frame:** $$\xi = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ \theta \end{bmatrix}$$ Where: - **x, y** = position of the robot's reference point (typically the center of the wheel axis) in the world frame - **θ** = orientation (heading angle) of the robot measured from the global X-axis > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The *kinematic model* of a DDR establishes a *non-holonomic constraint* — the robot cannot move sideways. Its instantaneous center of rotation (ICR) always lies on the wheel axis, making the model fundamentally different from omnidirectional drives. ## 1.2 Steered Robot (Car-Like Kinematic Model) — Definition A **steered (car-like) mobile robot** uses Ackermann steering geometry. It has at least **two fixed rear wheels** providing traction and **two front steered wheels** that pivot by an angle **δ** (steering angle). The robot moves along circular arcs of radius determined by the steering angle and wheelbase. > [!NOTE] > The classical mathematical abstraction used by KTU examiners is the **Bicycle Model**, where the two rear wheels are merged into one and the two front wheels are merged into one — yielding an equivalent 2-wheel planar representation that preserves the kinematic behavior. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition ### The Differential Drive — "Office Chair on Casters" > Imagine yourself sitting on an office chair with two free-rolling casters that can be **pushed forward or backward independently**. Push **both wheels equally** → you go straight. Push **only the left wheel forward** → the chair pivots to the right (the right wheel becomes the pivot). This is exactly how a DDR works — the differential in wheel speeds translates into both translation and rotation. ### The Steered Robot — "A Real Car at an Intersection" > When you turn a real car's steering wheel, the **front wheels angle inward**. The car does *not* pivot about its center; instead, it traces a smooth circular arc determined by how far you turn the wheel and the length of the car (wheelbase). The same physical intuition governs the **Bicycle Model** used in autonomous vehicle control. ## 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in KTU Board Problems | Metric | Symbol | Unit | Typical Range (Educational Robots) | |---|---|---|---| | Wheel radius | r | meters (m) | 0.03 – 0.10 m | | Wheel separation (track) | L | meters (m) | 0.15 – 0.50 m | | Wheelbase (steered robot) | L | meters (m) | 0.50 – 2.50 m | | Maximum linear velocity | v_max | m/s | 0.5 – 1.5 m/s | | Maximum angular velocity | ω_max | rad/s | 1.0 – 3.0 rad/s | | Maximum steering angle | δ_max | radians (rad) | ±0.5 rad (±30°) | | Sampling time (controller) | T_s | seconds (s) | 0.01 – 0.05 s | > [!TIP] > **KTU Examination Note:** All numerical values for **v_max**, **ω_max**, and **δ_max** are usually given in the question. You are expected to *derive the controller law symbolically first*, then substitute the values. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Differential Drive Robot — Forward Kinematic Model The DDR's two wheels rotate at angular velocities **ω_R** (right) and **ω_L** (left). Each wheel produces a linear velocity at its contact point: **v_R = r·ω_R** and **v_L = r·ω_L**. The robot's **rigid body motion** is the average of these two wheel velocities (translation) combined with a rotation produced by their difference. ### 2.1.1 Linear Velocity of Robot (v) $$v = \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}$$ ### 2.1.2 Angular Velocity of Robot (ω) $$\omega = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L}$$ ### 2.1.3 Posture Update Equations (Global Frame) $$\dot{x} = v\cos\theta$$ $$\dot{y} = v\sin\theta$$ $$\dot{\theta} = \omega$$ ## 2.2 Differential Drive Robot — Inverse Kinematic Model The **inverse kinematic problem** asks: *"Given a desired (v, ω) trajectory, what wheel speeds must I command?"* Solving for ω_R and ω_L: $$\omega_R = \frac{2v + \omega L}{2r}$$ $$\omega_L = \frac{2v - \omega L}{2r}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why this matters in control:** The reference trajectory generator outputs the desired **v_d(t)** and **ω_d(t)**. The *inverse kinematics block* converts these into motor reference speeds. The *low-level PID* then makes the actual motors track these references. This two-stage architecture appears in **every KTU board problem** on DDR control. ## 2.3 Non-Holonomic Constraint — The Kinematic Heart A DDR cannot translate sideways. Mathematically this is encoded as the **Pfaffian constraint**: $$\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$$ This equation has **NO temporal integration** — it is a constraint on *velocities*, not positions. Such constraints are called **non-holonomic** and they have profound consequences: - The robot is **controllable** despite the constraint (Brockett's theorem) - **Smooth time-invariant feedback** cannot stabilize a point posture (no continuous static-state-feedback exists for exact point stabilization) - **Discontinuous controllers** or **time-varying controllers** are required for point stabilization > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner Watch:** Questions frequently ask *"Why can't we use a simple P-controller to drive the robot to a point?"* The answer is rooted in **Brockett's necessary condition for smooth stabilization** — DDR fails this condition. ## 2.4 Steered Robot — Kinematic (Bicycle) Model The bicycle model represents the car-like robot with one virtual front wheel and one virtual rear wheel separated by wheelbase **L**. The front wheel steers by angle **δ**, and the rear wheel is aligned with the robot body. ### 2.4.1 Posture Update Equations $$\dot{x} = v\cos\theta$$ $$\dot{y} = v\sin\theta$$ $$\dot{\theta} = \frac{v\tan\delta}{L}$$ ### 2.4.2 Minimum Turning Radius $$R_{min} = \frac{L}{\tan\delta_{max}}$$ ### 2.4.3 Non-Holonomic Constraint for Car-Like Robot $$\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$$ > [!NOTE] > The car-like robot has **TWO** non-holonomic constraints: the no-side-slip constraint above **AND** the steering angle rate bound (δ̇ is also limited). This makes its control *harder* than the DDR despite the similar kinematic form. ## 2.5 Control Approaches — The High-Yield KTU Formula Sheet | Controller | Input Error | Core Equation | Best For | |---|---|---|---| | **Open-Loop (Feed-Forward)** | None | v, ω from trajectory | Pre-computed paths | | **PID Linear Velocity** | e_v = v_d - v | $u_v = K_{p,v}e_v + K_{i,v}\int e_v dt + K_{d,v}\dot{e_v}$ | Velocity tracking | | **PID Angular Velocity** | e_ω = ω_d - ω | $u_\omega = K_{p,\omega}e_\omega + K_{i,\omega}\int e_\omega dt + K_{d,\omega}\dot{e_\omega}$ | Heading tracking | | **Pose Error Feedback** | $e = \sqrt{(x_d-x)^2+(y_d-y)^2}$, $\alpha = \theta_d - \theta$ | $v = k_\rho e$, $\omega = k_\alpha \alpha + k_\rho\frac{\sin\alpha}{e}\beta$ | **Pose stabilization** | | **Lyapunov-Based Tracking** | $e = [e_x, e_y, e_\theta]^T$ | $\dot{V} = -k_1 e_x^2 - k_3 \frac{\sin^2 e_\theta}{e_\theta} \cdot e_y^2 < 0$ | Trajectory tracking | | **Backstepping (Car-Like)** | $\eta = [\eta_1, \eta_2, \eta_3]^T$ in error frame | $v = k_1\eta_1$, $\delta = \arctan(k_2 L \eta_2 / v) - \eta_3$ | Steered robot | | **Pure Pursuit (Car-Like)** | Look-ahead distance l_d | $\delta = \arctan(2L\sin\alpha / l_d)$ | Path following | | **Stanley (Car-Like)** | Cross-track e_fa, heading e_θ | $\delta = e_\theta + \arctan(k e_{fa} / v)$ | Autonomous cars | ## 2.6 The Lyapunov-Based Tracking Controller (Detailed in KTU Syllabus) For a DDR with pose error defined in the robot's local frame: $$e = \begin{bmatrix} e_x \\ e_y \\ e_\theta \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & \sin\theta & 0 \\ -\sin\theta & \cos\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_d - x \\ y_d - y \\ \theta_d - \theta \end{bmatrix}$$ The **control law** that ensures exponential convergence is: $$v = v_r \cos e_\theta + k_1 e_x$$ $$\omega = \omega_r + k_2 v_r e_y + k_3 v_r \sin e_\theta$$ Where **v_r, ω_r** are the reference trajectory velocities and **k_1, k_2, k_3 > 0** are design gains. ## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Differential drives** dominate indoor service robotics (TurtleBot, Pioneer, Roomba) due to mechanical simplicity and zero-turn radius capability. - **Steered / Ackermann robots** dominate outdoor autonomous vehicles (Stanley, Boss, Junior — all Stanford Racing winners) and warehouse AGVs because high-speed straight-line motion is stable. - **Mobile manipulation bases** (e.g., KUKA youBot, Fetch Freight) almost universally use DDR for in-factory navigation with the same kinematic model. > [!TIP] > When asked *"Give a real-world application"* in a KTU answer script, mention one specific robot platform (TurtleBot / Stanley) and one control algorithm — this demonstrates applied depth and earns full marks. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the DDR Forward Kinematic Model ### Step 1 — Visualize the Geometry Consider two wheels of radius **r**, separated by track **L**, rotating at angular velocities **ω_R** (right) and **ω_L** (left). The contact-point linear velocities are: $$v_R = r\omega_R \quad \text{(right wheel linear velocity at contact point)}$$ $$v_L = r\omega_L \quad \text{(left wheel linear velocity at contact point)}$$ ### Step 2 — Apply Rigid Body Rolling Constraint Since the robot is a single **rigid body**, the velocity of the midpoint M between the wheels is the arithmetic mean of the two wheel velocities: $$v = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2}$$ Substituting **v_R = r·ω_R** and **v_L = r·ω_L**: $$v = \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}$$ ### Step 3 — Derive Angular Velocity Using ICR Geometry The **Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR)** lies on the wheel axis. If the ICR is at distance **R** from M on the left side, then: $$v_R = \omega(R + L/2) \quad \text{and} \quad v_L = \omega(R - L/2)$$ Subtracting the second equation from the first eliminates R: $$v_R - v_L = \omega L$$ Substituting v_R, v_L: $$r\omega_R - r\omega_L = \omega L$$ $$\omega = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L}$$ ### Step 4 — Compose Global Frame Posture Kinematics The robot's planar motion has 3 DOF (x, y, θ). Projecting the body-frame velocities into the global frame: $$\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix}$$ Expanding component-wise: $$\dot{x} = v\cos\theta$$ $$\dot{y} = v\sin\theta$$ $$\dot{\theta} = \omega$$ ## 3.2 Derivation of the DDR Inverse Kinematics Starting from the forward kinematic equations: $$v = \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}$$ $$\omega = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L}$$ ### Step 1 — Multiply Both Equations by Appropriate Constants Multiply the **first** equation by 2: $$2v = r(\omega_R + \omega_L)$$ Multiply the **second** equation by L: $$\omega L = r(\omega_R - \omega_L)$$ ### Step 2 — Add the Two Equations Adding eliminates ω_L: $$2v + \omega L = 2r\omega_R$$ Therefore: $$\omega_R = \frac{2v + \omega L}{2r}$$ ### Step 3 — Subtract the Two Equations Subtracting eliminates ω_R: $$2v - \omega L = 2r\omega_L$$ Therefore: $$\omega_L = \frac{2v - \omega L}{2r}$$ ## 3.3 Derivation of the Bicycle Model Kinematics ### Step 1 — Geometry Setup The bicycle model has a rear wheel aligned with the body and a front wheel that can pivot by steering angle **δ**. The wheelbase is **L**. The rear wheel velocity is **v** (driving speed). ### Step 2 — Constrain the Rear Wheel to Roll Without Slipping The rear wheel direction equals the body direction θ: $$\dot{x} = v\cos\theta$$ $$\dot{y} = v\sin\theta$$ ### Step 3 — Relate Heading Rate to Steering Angle The vehicle rotates about the **Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR)** which lies on the line extending the rear axle, perpendicular to the rear wheel direction, and **also** on the line extending the front wheel direction. By geometry of two lines intersecting: The distance from rear axle to ICR is **R**, the turning radius. From the front wheel triangle: $$\tan\delta = \frac{L}{R}$$ The angular velocity of the vehicle body is: $$\omega = \frac{v}{R}$$ Substituting **R = L/tan δ**: $$\omega = \frac{v\tan\delta}{L}$$ Therefore: $$\dot{\theta} = \frac{v\tan\delta}{L}$$ ## 3.4 Derivation of the Pose-Error Tracking Controller ### Step 1 — Define Pose Error in Robot's Local Frame The **error vector** in the body-fixed frame is: $$\begin{bmatrix} e_x \\ e_y \\ e_\theta \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & \sin\theta & 0 \\ -\sin\theta & \cos\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_d - x \\ y_d - y \\ \theta_d - \theta \end{bmatrix}$$ ### Step 2 — Compute Error Dynamics Differentiating and using the chain rule, the error kinematics reduce to: $$\dot{e}_x = -v + v_r\cos e_\theta + \omega e_y$$ $$\dot{e}_y = v_r\sin e_\theta - \omega e_x$$ $$\dot{e}_\theta = \omega_r - \omega$$ ### Step 3 — Choose a Lyapunov Candidate Function $$V(e) = \frac{1}{2}(k_1 e_x^2 + k_2 e_y^2 + k_3 e_\theta^2), \quad k_1, k_2, k_3 > 0$$ This is positive-definite (V > 0 for e ≠ 0, V = 0 only at e = 0). ### Step 4 — Differentiate V Along Trajectories $$\dot{V} = k_1 e_x \dot{e}_x + k_2 e_y \dot{e}_y + k_3 e_\theta \dot{e}_\theta$$ Substituting the error dynamics and selecting control inputs: $$v = v_r \cos e_\theta + k_1 e_x$$ $$\omega = \omega_r + k_2 v_r e_y + k_3 v_r \sin e_\theta$$ ### Step 5 — Simplify the Derivative After substitution and algebraic simplification: $$\dot{V} = -k_1^2 e_x^2 - k_2 v_r^2 \sin^2 e_\theta \cdot \frac{e_y^2}{e_\theta^2} - k_3^2 v_r^2 \sin^2 e_\theta$$ Since each term is **negative semi-definite** and the system satisfies **LaSalle's invariance principle**, the origin is **asymptotically stable**. The robot converges to the desired trajectory. ## 3.5 Worked Example — KTU Style Numerical Problem **Problem Statement:** A differential drive robot has wheel radius **r = 0.05 m** and wheel separation **L = 0.30 m**. The robot must move forward at **v_d = 0.4 m/s** while turning left at **ω_d = 1.0 rad/s**. Calculate the required wheel angular velocities. ### Step 1 — Identify Given Quantities $$r = 0.05 \, \text{m}, \quad L = 0.30 \, \text{m}, \quad v_d = 0.4 \, \text{m/s}, \quad \omega_d = 1.0 \, \text{rad/s}$$ ### Step 2 — Apply the Inverse Kinematic Formula for Right Wheel $$\omega_R = \frac{2v_d + \omega_d L}{2r}$$ $$\omega_R = \frac{2(0.4) + (1.0)(0.30)}{2(0.05)}$$ $$\omega_R = \frac{0.8 + 0.3}{0.1} = \frac{1.1}{0.1} = 11.0 \, \text{rad/s}$$ ### Step 3 — Apply the Inverse Kinematic Formula for Left Wheel $$\omega_L = \frac{2v_d - \omega_d L}{2r}$$ $$\omega_L = \frac{2(0.4) - (1.0)(0.30)}{2(0.05)}$$ $$\omega_L = \frac{0.8 - 0.3}{0.1} = \frac{0.5}{0.1} = 5.0 \, \text{rad/s}$$ ### Step 4 — Physical Interpretation The **right wheel spins faster (11 rad/s)** than the **left wheel (5 rad/s)** because the robot is turning left — the right wheel must cover more distance on the outside of the turning arc. ## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Controller Selection Guide | Goal | Robot Type | Recommended Controller | Reason | |---|---|---|---| | Velocity tracking | DDR | PID (v, ω loops) | Decoupled SISO errors | | Point-to-point move | DDR | Pose-error feedback or Dynamic Window | Non-holonomic constraint | | Trajectory tracking | DDR | Lyapunov-based nonlinear control | Stability guarantee | | Path following | Steered | Pure Pursuit / Stanley | Computational efficiency | | Lane keeping | Steered | Stanley Controller | Heading + cross-track error | | Pose stabilization | Steered | Backstepping or Teel-style hybrid | Smooth feedback impossible | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 DDR Forward & Inverse Kinematic Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Reference Trajectory
x_d, y_d, theta_d] --> B[Trajectory Differentiator
Compute v_r, omega_r] B --> C[Inverse Kinematics Block
omega_R, omega_L equations] C --> D[PID Motor Controllers
Left and Right Loops] D --> E[DC Motors
Left and Right Wheels] E --> F[Robot Body
Differential Drive] F --> G[Wheel Encoders
Feedback Sensors] G --> D F --> H[Pose Estimator
x, y, theta from Odometry] H --> I[Error Computation Block
e_x, e_y, e_theta] I --> B ``` ## 4.2 DDR Lyapunov Tracking Controller Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph ReferencePath[Reference Path Generator] R1[x_d of t] R2[y_d of t] R3[theta_d of t] end subgraph RobotPlant[Robot Plant - Kinematic Model] P1[Inverse Kinematics] P2[DDR Body] P3[Odometry] end subgraph Controller[Nonlinear Tracking Controller] C1[Error Transformation
Rotation by theta] C2[Lyapunov Control Law
v and omega formulas] end R1 --> C1 R2 --> C1 R3 --> C1 P3 --> C1 C1 --> C2 C2 --> P1 P1 --> P2 P2 --> P3 ``` ## 4.3 Steered Robot Pure Pursuit Controller ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Global Path
Sequence of Waypoints] --> B[Lookahead Point Selection
Distance l_d ahead] B --> C[Compute alpha
Angle to Lookahead Point] C --> D[Steering Angle Law
delta equals arctan expression] D --> E[Steering Actuator
Front Wheels] E --> F[Car-Like Robot Body
Bicycle Kinematics] F --> G[Position Sensor
GPS or Encoder Fusion] G --> A ``` ## 4.4 Hierarchical Control Stack for Mobile Robots ```mermaid flowchart TB L1[Level 4 - Mission Planning
What is the goal] L2[Level 3 - Path Planning
Compute global path] L3[Level 2 - Trajectory Generation
Time-parameterized reference] L4[Level 1 - Kinematic Control
Compute v, omega or v, delta] L5[Level 0 - Motor Control
PID on wheel speeds] L1 --> L2 L2 --> L3 L3 --> L4 L4 --> L5 ``` ## 4.5 Case Study Mapping Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Indoor[Indoor Case Studies - DDR] I1[TurtleBot 2 - Burger] I2[Pioneer P3-DX] I3[Roomba 600 Series] end subgraph Outdoor[Outdoor Case Studies - Steered] O1[Stanley - Stanford DARPA 2005 Winner] O2[Boss - CMU Tartan Racing] O3[Junior - Stanford] end subgraph Industry[Industrial AGVs] AGV1[KIVA Amazon Robots] AGV2[Fetch Freight Base] end I1 --> K[DDR Kinematic Model] I2 --> K I3 --> K O1 --> S[Car-Like Kinematic Model] O2 --> S O3 --> S AGV1 --> K AGV2 --> K ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 — Differential Drive Kinematics **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]** **Q:** Derive the forward kinematic equations of a differential drive mobile robot. State the non-holonomic constraint clearly. **Model Answer:** The two wheels of radius **r** separated by distance **L** produce contact-point velocities **v_R = r·ω_R** and **v_L = r·ω_L**. By rigid body averaging, the linear velocity of the midpoint is: $$v = \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}$$ The angular velocity from the ICR geometry is: $$\omega = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L}$$ Posture update in the global frame: $$\dot{x} = v\cos\theta, \quad \dot{y} = v\sin\theta, \quad \dot{\theta} = \omega$$ The **non-holonomic constraint** is: $$\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$$ This expresses that the robot cannot move laterally — its velocity is always aligned with its heading axis. --- ### Question A2 — Steered Robot Steering Geometry **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]** **Q:** Explain why a car-like robot has a minimum turning radius. Express it in terms of wheelbase and maximum steering angle. **Model Answer:** A car-like robot with wheelbase **L** cannot pivot about its own center because the front wheels must rotate about an instantaneous center located on the perpendicular extension of the rear axle. The turning radius is geometrically: $$R = \frac{L}{\tan\delta}$$ The maximum value of **δ** is limited by the steering actuator. Therefore the **minimum turning radius** occurs at **δ = δ_max**: $$R_{min} = \frac{L}{\tan\delta_{max}}$$ For example, with **L = 2.5 m** and **δ_max = 0.5 rad**, R_min ≈ 4.53 m. This constraint is critical in path planning — paths with curvature exceeding **1/R_min** are infeasible. --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) ### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Full DDR Tracking Controller Derivation **[KTU University Exam — Model Question]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the inverse kinematic equations of a differential drive robot. A DDR with **r = 0.04 m** and **L = 0.20 m** must execute a maneuver with **v_d = 0.3 m/s** and **ω_d = 0.5 rad/s**. Compute the required wheel angular velocities and verify the direction of motion. **(b) [7 Marks]** Design a Lyapunov-based nonlinear tracking controller for a DDR tracking a reference trajectory. Define the pose error in the robot's local frame, write the control law, and prove asymptotic stability. #### Model Solution to (a) Starting from forward kinematics: $$v = \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}, \quad \omega = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L}$$ Multiply first by 2 and second by L, then add and subtract: $$\omega_R = \frac{2v + \omega L}{2r} = \frac{2(0.3) + (0.5)(0.20)}{2(0.04)} = \frac{0.6 + 0.10}{0.08} = \frac{0.70}{0.08} = 8.75 \, \text{rad/s}$$ $$\omega_L = \frac{2v - \omega L}{2r} = \frac{0.6 - 0.10}{0.08} = \frac{0.50}{0.08} = 6.25 \, \text{rad/s}$$ **Verification:** Both ω_R and ω_L are **positive** (forward motion). ω_R > ω_L means the right wheel is moving faster → the robot turns **left**. This matches the given ω_d = +0.5 rad/s (counter-clockwise positive). #### Model Solution to (b) **Step 1 — Error Definition:** $$e = \begin{bmatrix} e_x \\ e_y \\ e_\theta \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & \sin\theta & 0 \\ -\sin\theta & \cos\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_d - x \\ y_d - y \\ \theta_d - \theta \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 2 — Error Kinematics:** $$\dot{e}_x = -v + v_r \cos e_\theta + \omega e_y$$ $$\dot{e}_y = v_r \sin e_\theta - \omega e_x$$ $$\dot{e}_\theta = \omega_r - \omega$$ **Step 3 — Lyapunov Candidate:** $$V = \frac{1}{2}(k_1 e_x^2 + k_2 e_y^2 + k_3 e_\theta^2), \quad k_1, k_2, k_3 > 0$$ **Step 4 — Control Law:** $$v = v_r \cos e_\theta + k_1 e_x$$ $$\omega = \omega_r + k_2 v_r e_y + k_3 v_r \sin e_\theta$$ **Step 5 — Stability Proof:** Substituting the control law into the error dynamics and simplifying yields: $$\dot{V} = -k_1^2 e_x^2 - k_2^2 v_r^2 e_y^2 - k_3^2 v_r^2 \sin^2 e_\theta \leq 0$$ By **LaSalle's invariance principle**, the largest invariant set contained in the set where **V̇ = 0** is the origin **(e_x = e_y = e_θ = 0)**. Therefore the origin is **globally asymptotically stable** and the robot tracks the reference trajectory. --- ### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Steered Robot Pure Pursuit and Kinematics **[KTU University Exam — Model Question]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the bicycle kinematic model for a car-like mobile robot. Show that the minimum turning radius is **R_min = L/tan(δ_max)**. Calculate the minimum turning radius for **L = 2.0 m** and **δ_max = 30°**. **(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the Pure Pursuit path-following controller for a steered robot. Derive the steering law and discuss how the look-ahead distance affects tracking accuracy and stability. #### Model Solution to (a) **Step 1 — Setup:** Let the rear wheel be aligned with the body heading θ, and the front wheel steer by δ. The wheelbase is L. The instantaneous center of rotation lies on the perpendicular to both the rear axle line and the front-wheel direction line. **Step 2 — From the geometry of the triangle formed by the ICR, rear contact, and front contact:** $$\tan\delta = \frac{L}{R}$$ Therefore: $$R = \frac{L}{\tan\delta}$$ **Step 3 — Compute R_min for given values:** $$R_{min} = \frac{2.0}{\tan(30°)} = \frac{2.0}{0.5774} \approx 3.464 \, \text{m}$$ #### Model Solution to (b) **Step 1 — Concept:** The Pure Pursuit controller selects a **look-ahead point** on the global path at distance **l_d** ahead of the robot. The steering command is computed to make the robot's circular arc pass through this point. **Step 2 — Geometry:** Let α be the angle between the robot's heading and the look-ahead point. The turning radius **R** required to reach the point is found from the chord–angle relation: $$R = \frac{l_d}{2\sin\alpha}$$ **Step 3 — Steering Law:** Using the bicycle model relation **R = L/tan δ**, equate the two expressions: $$\frac{l_d}{2\sin\alpha} = \frac{L}{\tan\delta}$$ Solving for δ: $$\delta = \arctan\left(\frac{2L\sin\alpha}{l_d}\right)$$ **Step 4 — Effect of Look-ahead Distance:** - **Small l_d:** Sharp turning, accurate path following, but oscillatory / unstable at high speed. - **Large l_d:** Smooth, stable motion, but cuts corners and may deviate significantly from path. - **Practical tuning:** l_d ≈ 1.5 × wheelbase is a common starting point. --- ## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks (Module 4):** > > 1. **Forgetting the non-holonomic constraint** in the kinematic model — Examiners explicitly check if you have stated the constraint **A = [sin θ, -cos θ, 0] · ξ̇ = 0**. Missing it costs 1–2 marks immediately. > > 2. **Mixing up wheel radius r and wheel separation L** in the DDR inverse kinematics. Re-derive on the answer sheet — never memorize blindly. > > 3. **Incorrect orientation of the error transformation matrix** in Lyapunov tracking. The rotation matrix must be **R(θ)** not **R(θ_d)**. Wrong sign on **e_y** flips the entire convergence proof. > > 4. **Skipping the stability proof** in Part B questions. Examiners allocate 2–3 marks specifically for the **V̇ < 0 derivation** + LaSalle's principle invocation. Stating "the system is stable" without proof is insufficient. > > 5. **Units confusion:** Angular velocities must always be in **rad/s**, not degrees/s or RPM, when substituted into formulas. > > 6. **Forgetting the bicycle model is an approximation** — Ackermann geometry differs from true 4-wheel steering during sharp turns. --- ## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4** - **DDR Forward Kinematics:** $v = \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}$, $\omega = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L}$ - **DDR Inverse Kinematics:** $\omega_R = \frac{2v + \omega L}{2r}$, $\omega_L = \frac{2v - \omega L}{2r}$ - **Non-holonomic constraint:** $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$ — **always state this in your answer** - **Bicycle model:** $\dot{x} = v\cos\theta$, $\dot{y} = v\sin\theta$, $\dot{\theta} = v\tan\delta / L$ - **Min turning radius:** $R_{min} = L / \tan\delta_{max}$ - **Lyapunov tracking control law:** $v = v_r\cos e_\theta + k_1 e_x$, $\omega = \omega_r + k_2 v_r e_y + k_3 v_r \sin e_\theta$ with $k_i > 0$ - **Pure Pursuit steering law:** $\delta = \arctan(2L\sin\alpha / l_d)$ - **Stanley Controller:** $\delta = e_\theta + \arctan(k \cdot e_{fa} / v)$ - **Brockett's theorem:** DDR cannot be stabilized by any smooth time-invariant state feedback to a single point posture — use discontinuous or time-varying control - **Two-stage control architecture:** Trajectory Generator → Inverse Kinematics → Motor PID — this is the standard KTU answer framework - **Case studies:** TurtleBot / Pioneer (DDR), Stanley / Boss / Junior (steered), KIVA robots (industrial AGV using DDR) - **Always include units** in numerical answers: m, m/s, rad, rad/s - **Direction convention:** ω > 0 = counter-clockwise (left turn), ω < 0 = clockwise (right turn) - **For Part B stability questions:** Always (1) define V > 0, (2) show V̇ ≤ 0, (3) cite LaSalle's invariance principle, (4) conclude asymptotic stability

topics.title: Design and Implementation of a Differential Drive Robot Capable of Moving to a Point, Following a Line and Following a Path. Case Study
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET744 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Design and Implementation of a Differential Drive Robot Capable of Moving to a Point, Following a Line and Following a Path. Case Study

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Differential Drive Robot (DDR)** is a non-holonomic mobile robot whose motion is produced by two independently actuated wheels mounted on a common axle, where each wheel can be driven forward or backward at different speeds. The vehicle's planar motion (translation + rotation) emerges solely from the **velocity difference between the left and right wheels**, without any dedicated steering mechanism.

For the scope of Module 4, the design must exhibit **three primitive navigational competencies**:

| Competency | KTU Terminology | Description |
|------------|-----------------|-------------|
| **Goal-Seeking Behavior** | *Move to a Point* | Navigate from the current pose $(x, y, \theta)$ to a target goal $G = (x_g, y_g)$. |
| **Edge-Following Behavior** | *Line Following* | Track a 1-D path drawn on the ground using on-board reflectance sensors. |
| **Trajectory-Following Behavior** | *Path Following* | Stay as close as possible to a predefined geometric curve $\mathcal{C}(s)$. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Focus:** The question paper typically fuses the **kinematic model** with **behavioral control** and asks for a complete hardware-software case study. Memorizing only the equations without understanding the behavior state machine costs marks.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Two-Horse Carriage"

Imagine a chariot drawn by **two horses** (the left and right wheels). The reins are independent:

- **Both horses pull with the same force** → the chariot moves in a straight line.
- **Only the left horse pulls** → the chariot swerves to the right (because the left side advances while the right side stays).
- **Both horses pull in opposite directions** → the chariot spins in place about its own center.

This is exactly how a differential drive works in software. Instead of horses, we have **DC geared motors driven by an H-Bridge**, and instead of reins, we have **Pulse Width Modulation (PWM) duty cycles** sent from a microcontroller.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following values are the **standard textbook metrics** used in KTU valuation:

- **Wheel radius** $r$ — typically **$0.03$ m to $0.05$ m** for educational robots.
- **Wheel separation / track length** $L$ (also denoted $2b$ or $d$) — typically **$0.10$ m to $0.20$ m**.
- **Maximum linear velocity** $v_{max}$ — usually **$\le 0.5$ m/s** for indoor safety.
- **Maximum angular velocity** $\omega_{max}$ — usually **$\le 2$ rad/s** to prevent slip.
- **Sampling period** $\Delta t$ — typically **$10$ ms to $100$ ms** for closed-loop control.

> [!NOTE]
> Always quote units explicitly in KTU answer sheets. A formula without units attracts a **partial-mark penalty** under KTU's evaluation rubric.

---

## 1.4 The Three Behaviors at a Glance

### A. Move to a Point (Point-to-Point Navigation)

The robot computes the **Euclidean distance** and the **bearing angle** to the goal, then drives toward it while continuously correcting its heading. The behavior terminates when the robot enters a **goal-tolerance disk** of radius $\rho_{tol}$ around $G$.

### B. Line Following

An array of **infrared (IR) reflectance sensors** (typically 5 sensors in a line: S1, S2, S3, S4, S5) is mounted underneath the chassis. The line is detected as the position where the sensor reading drops (black line on white floor, or vice versa). The robot's job is to **maintain the line directly under the center sensor** by adjusting the relative wheel speeds.

### C. Path Following

The robot is given a **sequence of waypoints** or a **smooth curve** $y = f(x)$. The objective is to follow the curve while moving forward — not necessarily reaching a specific endpoint. The classical algorithm used here is the **Pure Pursuit** (carrot-following) algorithm.

---

## 1.5 Why These Three Behaviors Matter (Engineering Relevance)

- **Warehouse AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles)** like Amazon Kiva robots use line-following on magnetic tape.
- **Autonomous lawn mowers** use point-to-point navigation between lawn zones.
- **Self-driving cars** use path-following on lane curves.
- **Mars rovers (Perseverance, Curiosity)** use differential drive for terrain traversal.

> [!TIP]
> In a 14-mark KTU answer, always begin with a one-line statement of the **engineering application** before diving into equations. Examiners reward contextual awareness.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📐 SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Differential Drive Kinematic Model

### 2.1.1 Configuration Space

The pose of the robot in the 2-D world frame is fully described by:

$$q = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ \theta \end{bmatrix}$$

where $\theta$ is the **orientation angle** measured counter-clockwise from the positive X-axis.

> [!NOTE]
> A differential drive robot has **3 configuration variables** but only **2 control inputs** $(v, \omega)$. Hence it is **non-holonomic** — it cannot move sideways, regardless of wheel speeds.

### 2.1.2 Forward Kinematics — From Wheel Speeds to Body Twist

Let:
- $v_l$ = linear speed of the left wheel (m/s)
- $v_r$ = linear speed of the right wheel (m/s)
- $r$ = wheel radius
- $L$ = distance between wheel contact points (track length)
- $\omega_l, \omega_r$ = angular speeds of left and right wheels (rad/s)

The body twist (linear and angular velocity of the chassis center) is:

$$v = \frac{v_r + v_l}{2} = \frac{r(\omega_r + \omega_l)}{2}$$

$$\omega = \frac{v_r - v_l}{L} = \frac{r(\omega_r - \omega_l)}{L}$$

The **Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR)** lies on the line joining the two wheels at a distance:

$$R = \frac{v}{\omega} = \frac{L}{2} \cdot \frac{v_r + v_l}{v_r - v_l}$$

Special cases:
- If $v_r = -v_l$ → $v = 0$, $\omega \neq 0$ → **pure rotation in place**.
- If $v_r = v_l$ → $\omega = 0$ → **pure translation along a straight line**.

### 2.1.3 Inverse Kinematics — From Body Twist to Wheel Speeds

Given a desired $(v, \omega)$, solve for the wheel speeds:

$$v_r = v + \frac{L \cdot \omega}{2}$$

$$v_l = v - \frac{L \cdot \omega}{2}$$

This is the **inverse mapping** and is used inside the controller every control cycle.

### 2.1.4 World-Frame Pose Update (Euler Integration)

At each time step $\Delta t$, the pose is propagated forward:

$$x_{k+1} = x_k + v_k \cos(\theta_k) \, \Delta t$$

$$y_{k+1} = y_k + v_k \sin(\theta_k) \, \Delta t$$

$$\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \omega_k \, \Delta t$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Forgetting to use the **old** $\theta_k$ in the $\cos$ and $\sin$ terms. Using $\theta_{k+1}$ creates an implicit equation requiring iterative solution.

---

## 2.2 Behavior 1 — Move-to-a-Point (Goal-Seeking)

### 2.2.1 Geometric Quantities

Given goal $G = (x_g, y_g)$ and current pose $(x, y, \theta)$:

$$\rho = \sqrt{(x_g - x)^2 + (y_g - y)^2}$$

$$\alpha = \text{atan2}(y_g - y, \, x_g - x) - \theta$$

where $\rho$ is the **distance to goal** and $\alpha$ is the **heading error** (wrapped to $(-\pi, \pi]$).

### 2.2.2 Control Law

A standard proportional control law:

$$v = k_\rho \cdot \rho$$

$$\omega = k_\alpha \cdot \alpha + k_\beta \cdot \rho \cdot \sin(\alpha)$$

where the third term $k_\beta \rho \sin(\alpha)$ is **feed-forward** and prevents the robot from circling the goal.

> [!NOTE]
> The $\text{atan2}$ function (not $\arctan$) is mandatory in KTU solutions because $\arctan$ cannot distinguish between diametrically opposite quadrants.

### 2.2.3 Termination Condition

The behavior halts when $\rho \le \rho_{tol}$ (typically $\rho_{tol} = 0.05$ m).

---

## 2.3 Behavior 2 — Line Following

### 2.3.1 Sensor Geometry

A 5-sensor array (e.g., **TCRT5000** modules) is mounted with **equal spacing** $s$. Each sensor outputs a value $S_i \in [0, 1023]$ (after ADC). Black line → low value; White floor → high value.

### 2.3.2 Line Position Calculation (Weighted Centroid)

$$e = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{5} (i - 3) \cdot \bar{S}_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{5} \bar{S}_i}$$

where $\bar{S}_i = 1 - \frac{S_i}{1023}$ inverts the white-floor convention so that the line has a positive weight.

The error $e$ is in sensor-units. To convert to meters: $e_{meters} = e \cdot s$.

### 2.3.3 PID Control Law

$$\omega(t) = K_p \cdot e(t) + K_i \int e(\tau) d\tau + K_d \frac{de}{dt}$$

The forward speed is held constant: $v = v_{const}$.

> [!TIP]
> In KTU 14-mark answers, **mention tuning heuristics**: start with $K_p$, add $K_d$ to reduce oscillation, and add $K_i$ only if a steady-state offset is observed.

---

## 2.4 Behavior 3 — Path Following (Pure Pursuit)

### 2.4.1 Algorithm Outline

Given a path made of waypoints $\{(x_1, y_1), (x_2, y_2), \dots, (x_n, y_n)\}$ and a **look-ahead distance** $L_d$:

1. Find the **closest point** $P_{cl}$ on the path to the robot.
2. Move forward by $L_d$ along the path → this is the **look-ahead point** $P_{la}$.
3. Compute the curvature $\kappa$ that drives the robot from its current pose to $P_{la}$.
4. Set $(v, \omega)$ accordingly.

### 2.4.2 Curvature Equation

Let $\alpha$ = angle between the robot's forward axis and the line joining robot to $P_{la}$. Let $L_d$ = distance from robot to $P_{la}$.

$$\kappa = \frac{2 \sin(\alpha)}{L_d}$$

$$\omega = v \cdot \kappa = \frac{2v \sin(\alpha)}{L_d}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pure Pursuit** is **not** stable for very small $L_d$ (because $\omega \to \infty$). The standard mitigation is to clamp $\omega \le \omega_{max}$ and to **blend** Pure Pursuit with a Stanley-style heading correction for high-speed applications.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $v = \dfrac{r(\omega_r + \omega_l)}{2}$ | Forward velocity of chassis | m/s |
| 2 | $\omega = \dfrac{r(\omega_r - \omega_l)}{L}$ | Angular velocity of chassis | rad/s |
| 3 | $v_r = v + \dfrac{L\omega}{2}$ | Inverse kinematic (right wheel) | m/s |
| 4 | $v_l = v - \dfrac{L\omega}{2}$ | Inverse kinematic (left wheel) | m/s |
| 5 | $R = \dfrac{L}{2} \cdot \dfrac{v_r + v_l}{v_r - v_l}$ | ICR radius | m |
| 6 | $x_{k+1} = x_k + v_k \cos\theta_k \, \Delta t$ | Pose update X | m |
| 7 | $y_{k+1} = y_k + v_k \sin\theta_k \, \Delta t$ | Pose update Y | m |
| 8 | $\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \omega_k \, \Delta t$ | Pose update heading | rad |
| 9 | $\rho = \sqrt{(x_g - x)^2 + (y_g - y)^2}$ | Distance to goal | m |
| 10 | $\alpha = \text{atan2}(y_g - y,\, x_g - x) - \theta$ | Heading error | rad |
| 11 | $v = k_\rho \rho, \;\; \omega = k_\alpha \alpha + k_\beta \rho \sin\alpha$ | Go-to-point control | m/s, rad/s |
| 12 | $e = \dfrac{\sum (i-3)\bar{S}_i}{\sum \bar{S}_i}$ | Line error (sensor units) | — |
| 13 | $\omega(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e \, dt + K_d \dot e$ | PID line-following law | rad/s |
| 14 | $\kappa = \dfrac{2 \sin\alpha}{L_d}$ | Pure Pursuit curvature | 1/m |

> [!NOTE]
> Vertical bars $\vert x \vert$ were intentionally avoided in the table; the **distance formula** (row 9) already encodes magnitude as a positive square root.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Behavior Used | Industrial Example |
|--------|---------------|---------------------|
| Hospital drug delivery | Move-to-point | Aethon TUG robot |
| Warehouse inventory | Line-following | Amazon Kiva / Heriot-Watt LGV |
| Autonomous highway driving | Path-following | Tesla Autopilot lane-keeping |
| Mars exploration | All three combined | NASA Perseverance rover |
| Agricultural weeding | Line-following | Naio Technologies Oz |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🧮 SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> **Mandatory Rule:** Every algebraic transition is expanded. No equation is skipped.

---

## 3.1 Derivation of Forward Kinematics

### Starting Point — Wheel Speeds

The linear speed at the contact patch of each wheel is the **circumferential speed**:

$$v_l = r \cdot \omega_l$$

$$v_r = r \cdot \omega_r$$

### Geometric Reasoning

The robot's chassis center $C$ moves along a circular arc of radius $R$ around the ICR. Two constraints hold simultaneously:

**(i)** The center $C$ lies midway between the two wheels. Therefore its linear speed $v$ is the **average** of the two wheel speeds (since the wheel on the outside of the arc moves faster):

$$v = \frac{v_r + v_l}{2}$$

**(ii)** The two wheels lie on a circle of radius $R \pm L/2$ around the ICR. Their speeds satisfy the no-slip rigid-body rotation relation:

$$v_r = (R + L/2) \cdot \omega$$

$$v_l = (R - L/2) \cdot \omega$$

Subtracting the two:

$$v_r - v_l = L \cdot \omega$$

$$\boxed{\omega = \frac{v_r - v_l}{L}}$$

Combining:

$$\boxed{v = \frac{r(\omega_r + \omega_l)}{2}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Inverse Kinematics

Given desired $v$ and $\omega$, solve the linear system:

$$v_r + v_l = 2v$$

$$v_r - v_l = L\omega$$

Add and subtract:

$$v_r = v + \frac{L\omega}{2}$$

$$v_l = v - \frac{L\omega}{2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> These two equations are the **bridge between the AI planner** (which outputs $v, \omega$) and the **motor driver** (which accepts $v_l, v_r$). They are the most-frequently-quoted formulas in KTU exams.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Go-to-Goal Control Law

### Step 1 — Express heading error $\alpha$

Let $\phi_g = \text{atan2}(y_g - y, x_g - x)$ be the world-frame bearing of the goal. The robot is pointing along $\theta$. The angular error is:

$$\alpha = \phi_g - \theta$$

It must be wrapped to $(-\pi, \pi]$:

$$\alpha \leftarrow \text{wrap}(\alpha) = \text{atan2}(\sin\alpha, \cos\alpha)$$

### Step 2 — Distance $\rho$

From the Pythagorean theorem:

$$\rho = \sqrt{(x_g - x)^2 + (y_g - y)^2}$$

### Step 3 — Saturate velocity

To prevent overshoot when far away:

$$v = \min(k_\rho \rho, \, v_{max})$$

### Step 4 — Saturate angular velocity

$$\omega = \min\!\big(\max(k_\alpha \alpha + k_\beta \rho \sin\alpha, -\omega_{max}), \, \omega_{max}\big)$$

> [!TIP]
> The **wrap function** `wrap(alpha)` is essential. Without it, $\alpha$ can accumulate to $10$ rad and the robot will spin endlessly. Examiners frequently test this.

---

## 3.4 Derivation of Pure Pursuit Curvature

### Setup

Let the robot be at $C = (x, y)$ pointing along $\theta$. The look-ahead point $P_{la} = (x_{la}, y_{la})$ lies on the path at distance $L_d$ ahead along the trajectory.

### Step 1 — Vector from robot to look-ahead point

$$\vec{d} = (x_{la} - x, \, y_{la} - y)$$

### Step 2 — Angle between robot heading and $\vec{d}$

$$\alpha = \text{atan2}(y_{la} - y, \, x_{la} - x) - \theta$$

### Step 3 — Geometry of circular arc

A circle passing through $C$ and $P_{la}$ with chord length $L_d$ subtends an angle $2\alpha$ at its center. By the inscribed-angle theorem, the radius of curvature is:

$$R = \frac{L_d}{2 \sin\alpha}$$

### Step 4 — Curvature is the reciprocal of radius

$$\kappa = \frac{1}{R} = \frac{2 \sin\alpha}{L_d}$$

### Step 5 — Angular velocity

$$\omega = v \cdot \kappa = \frac{2v \sin\alpha}{L_d}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The look-ahead distance $L_d$ must satisfy $L_d \ge 0.5 v$ to avoid oscillation. A common KTU heuristic: $L_d \approx 0.5$ to $1.0$ m for indoor robots.

---

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example — Pure Pursuit

**Given:**
- Robot pose: $(0, 0, 0)$
- Look-ahead point: $P_{la} = (1.0, 0.5)$ m
- Forward speed: $v = 0.3$ m/s

**Find:** Required $\omega$.

**Step 1:** Compute $\alpha$

$$\phi_{la} = \text{atan2}(0.5, 1.0) = 0.4636 \text{ rad}$$

$$\alpha = 0.4636 - 0 = 0.4636 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 2:** Compute $L_d$

$$L_d = \sqrt{(1.0)^2 + (0.5)^2} = \sqrt{1.25} = 1.118 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3:** Curvature

$$\kappa = \frac{2 \sin(0.4636)}{1.118} = \frac{2 \times 0.4472}{1.118} = 0.800 \text{ m}^{-1}$$

**Step 4:** Angular velocity

$$\omega = 0.3 \times 0.800 = 0.240 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 5:** Verify against saturation

$$\vert \omega \vert = 0.240 \le \omega_{max} = 2.0 \; ✓$$

The robot will turn left at **0.24 rad/s** to intercept the look-ahead point.

---

## 3.6 Numerical Worked Example — Forward Kinematics

**Given:** $r = 0.04$ m, $L = 0.15$ m, $\omega_l = 5$ rad/s, $\omega_r = 7$ rad/s.

**Step 1:** Wheel linear speeds

$$v_l = 0.04 \times 5 = 0.20 \text{ m/s}$$

$$v_r = 0.04 \times 7 = 0.28 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2:** Body twist

$$v = \frac{0.28 + 0.20}{2} = 0.24 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\omega = \frac{0.28 - 0.20}{0.15} = 0.533 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 3:** ICR radius

$$R = \frac{0.24}{0.533} = 0.45 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4:** Pose update over $\Delta t = 0.1$ s

$$x_{new} = 0 + 0.24 \cos(0) \times 0.1 = 0.024 \text{ m}$$

$$y_{new} = 0 + 0.24 \sin(0) \times 0.1 = 0 \text{ m}$$

$$\theta_{new} = 0 + 0.533 \times 0.1 = 0.0533 \text{ rad} \approx 3.05°$$

The robot moves forward **24 mm** and rotates left by **3.05°** in one tick.

---

## 3.7 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Aspect | Go-to-Point | Line Following | Path Following |
|--------|-------------|----------------|----------------|
| **Sensor input** | Encoders + IMU + GPS (optional) | IR reflectance array | Encoders + IMU + map |
| **Output control** | $(v, \omega)$ pair | Constant $v$, variable $\omega$ | Constant $v$, variable $\omega$ |
| **Reference signal** | Fixed goal $(x_g, y_g)$ | Sensor error $e(t)$ | Look-ahead point $P_{la}$ |
| **Termination** | $\rho \le \rho_{tol}$ | Continuous (infinite loop) | Continuous (until $s = s_{final}$) |
| **Stability risk** | Overshoot / oscillation | Sensor noise → jitter | Small $L_d$ → spin-out |
| **Computation cost** | Low (2 ops + atan2) | Low (PID) | Moderate (path lookup) |
| **KTU exam frequency** | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★★ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🖼️ SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hardware Block Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph POWER["POWER SUBSYSTEM"]
        BAT1["11.1V LiPo Battery"]
        REG5["5V Regulator LM7805"]
        REG33["3.3V Regulator AMS1117"]
    end

    subgraph SENSE["SENSOR SUBSYSTEM"]
        IR5["5-Channel IR Array TCRT5000"]
        ENC2["Quadrature Encoders x2"]
        IMU1["MPU6050 IMU 6-Axis"]
        USON["HC-SR04 Ultrasonic"]
    end

    subgraph CTRL["CONTROL SUBSYSTEM"]
        MCU1["Arduino Mega 2560 / STM32"]
        PID1["PID Controller Firmware"]
        FSM1["Behavior State Machine"]
    end

    subgraph DRIVE["DRIVE SUBSYSTEM"]
        MOT1["Left DC Geared Motor 12V"]
        MOT2["Right DC Geared Motor 12V"]
        DRV1["L298N Dual H-Bridge"]
        WHLL["Left Wheel r=4cm"]
        WHLR["Right Wheel r=4cm"]
    end

    subgraph COMMS["COMMUNICATION SUBSYSTEM"]
        BT1["HC-05 Bluetooth Module"]
        XBEE["XBee S2C Telemetry"]
        LCD1["16x2 LCD Display"]
    end

    BAT1 --> REG5
    BAT1 --> REG33
    BAT1 --> DRV1

    IR5 --> MCU1
    ENC2 --> MCU1
    IMU1 --> MCU1
    USON --> MCU1

    MCU1 --> PID1
    MCU1 --> FSM1
    PID1 --> DRV1
    FSM1 --> PID1

    DRV1 --> MOT1
    DRV1 --> MOT2
    MOT1 --> WHLL
    MOT2 --> WHLR

    MCU1 --> BT1
    MCU1 --> XBEE
    MCU1 --> LCD1
```

---

## 4.2 Behavior State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> INIT
    INIT --> IDLE : Sensors calibrated
    IDLE --> GO_TO_POINT : Goal received
    IDLE --> LINE_FOLLOW : Line detected
    IDLE --> PATH_FOLLOW : Path uploaded
    GO_TO_POINT --> IDLE : Reached goal
    LINE_FOLLOW --> IDLE : E-Stop pressed
    PATH_FOLLOW --> IDLE : Path complete
    GO_TO_POINT --> LINE_FOLLOW : Line found during transit
    LINE_FOLLOW --> GO_TO_POINT : Line lost, goal still active
    PATH_FOLLOW --> GO_TO_POINT : Obstacle detected
    IDLE --> [*]
```

---

## 4.3 Closed-Loop Control Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Read sensors at time k] --> B[Compute error e_k]
    B --> C[Compute rho and alpha if go-to-point]
    B --> D[Compute line error if line-follow]
    B --> E[Compute look-ahead if path-follow]
    C --> F[Compute control v, omega]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G[Inverse kinematics v_r, v_l]
    G --> H[Apply PWM to H-Bridge]
    H --> I[Motors actuate wheels]
    I --> J[Robot moves and pose changes]
    J --> A
```

---

## 4.4 Differential Drive Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph "WORLD FRAME"
        X["+X axis"]
        Y["+Y axis"]
    end
    subgraph "ROBOT FRAME"
        THETA["theta = heading angle"]
        R["ICR (Instantaneous Center of Rotation)"]
        L["Left wheel at distance L/2"]
        CTR["Center C"]
        RR["Right wheel at distance L/2"]
    end
    R --- L
    R --- RR
    L --- CTR
    CTR --- RR
    CTR --> THETA
```

---

## 4.5 Software Layer Architecture

| Layer | Function | KTU-Exam Equivalent |
|-------|----------|---------------------|
| **Application Layer** | Goal / path storage, user commands | High-level planning |
| **Behavior Layer** | FSM selecting Move / Line / Path | Behavior arbitration |
| **Control Layer** | PID and Pure Pursuit algorithms | Closed-loop control |
| **Kinematic Layer** | Forward & inverse kinematics | $(v,\omega) \leftrightarrow (v_r, v_l)$ |
| **Hardware Abstraction Layer** | PWM, ADC, GPIO drivers | Motor driver interface |
| **Physical Layer** | Motors, wheels, sensors | Hardware |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📝 SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between the three navigational behaviors: Move-to-a-Point, Line Following, and Path Following. State one real-world application of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Behavior | Description | Reference Signal | Application |
|----------|-------------|------------------|-------------|
| **Move-to-a-Point** | Robot navigates from current pose to a single goal coordinate using pose-based feedback. | Goal $(x_g, y_g)$ | Hospital drug delivery robot |
| **Line Following** | Robot tracks a 1-D painted or magnetic line using on-board reflectance sensors. | Line error $e(t)$ from sensor array | Amazon warehouse AGV |
| **Path Following** | Robot follows a predefined trajectory (sequence of waypoints or smooth curve) while maintaining forward motion. | Look-ahead point on the path | Self-driving car lane-keeping |

> Distribution: 1 mark definition + 1 mark distinction + 1 mark application example.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**With a neat diagram, derive the forward kinematics equations of a differential drive robot.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

For wheel radius $r$, track length $L$, and wheel angular speeds $\omega_l, \omega_r$:

**Linear velocity of chassis center:**

$$v = \frac{r(\omega_r + \omega_l)}{2}$$

**Angular velocity of chassis:**

$$\omega = \frac{r(\omega_r - \omega_l)}{L}$$

**Derivation essence (2 marks):** Linear speed is the **average** of the two wheel speeds because the chassis center lies midway. Angular speed arises because the two wheels describe arcs of radii $R \pm L/2$, and their velocity difference equals $L\omega$.

> Distribution: 1 mark equations + 1 mark derivation + 1 mark neat diagram.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Rule (ESE):** Students must answer **one full question** per module with internal choice. Each 14-mark question typically splits into **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

### ✅ Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Design the kinematic model of a differential drive robot. Derive the forward and inverse kinematics. Given $r = 0.05$ m, $L = 0.20$ m, compute the wheel speeds required to achieve a forward velocity of $0.4$ m/s and an angular velocity of $0.5$ rad/s. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the Pure Pursuit algorithm for path following with a neat block diagram. Derive the curvature expression $\kappa = \frac{2 \sin\alpha}{L_d}$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Q.A(a) — Design and Kinematics (7 marks)

**Step 1: Configuration space (1 mark)**

The robot pose in the world frame is:

$$q = [x, y, \theta]^T$$

where $(x,y)$ is the chassis center and $\theta$ is the orientation.

**Step 2: Forward kinematics (2 marks)**

The wheel contact speeds are $v_l = r\omega_l$ and $v_r = r\omega_r$. The chassis center speed is the **average** of the two wheel speeds:

$$v = \frac{v_r + v_l}{2} = \frac{r(\omega_r + \omega_l)}{2}$$

The angular speed of the chassis arises from the velocity difference across the track:

$$\omega = \frac{v_r - v_l}{L} = \frac{r(\omega_r - \omega_l)}{L}$$

**Step 3: Inverse kinematics (2 marks)**

Given $(v, \omega)$, solve the linear system:

$$v_r = v + \frac{L\omega}{2}$$

$$v_l = v - \frac{L\omega}{2}$$

**Step 4: Numerical evaluation (2 marks)**

Substitute $r = 0.05$ m, $L = 0.20$ m, $v = 0.4$ m/s, $\omega = 0.5$ rad/s:

$$v_r = 0.4 + \frac{0.20 \times 0.5}{2} = 0.4 + 0.05 = 0.45 \text{ m/s}$$

$$v_l = 0.4 - 0.05 = 0.35 \text{ m/s}$$

Corresponding wheel angular speeds:

$$\omega_r = \frac{0.45}{0.05} = 9 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$\omega_l = \frac{0.35}{0.05} = 7 \text{ rad/s}$$

> **Answer:** Right wheel must turn at $9$ rad/s, left wheel at $7$ rad/s.

---

#### Solution to Q.A(b) — Pure Pursuit Algorithm (7 marks)

**Step 1: Algorithm outline (2 marks)**

The Pure Pursuit algorithm operates as follows:
1. Find the point $P_{cl}$ on the path closest to the robot.
2. Advance $L_d$ along the path from $P_{cl}$ to obtain the **look-ahead point** $P_{la}$.
3. Compute the angle $\alpha$ between the robot's forward axis and the vector $\vec{CP_{la}}$.
4. Compute the curvature $\kappa$ of the arc joining $C$ to $P_{la}$.
5. Apply $\omega = v \kappa$ and $v_r, v_l$ via inverse kinematics.

**Step 2: Derivation (3 marks)**

Let $L_d = \vert \vec{CP_{la}} \vert$. The chord length of the desired arc equals $L_d$. By the inscribed-angle theorem, the chord $L_d$ subtends angle $2\alpha$ at the center of the arc of radius $R$:

$$L_d = 2R \sin\alpha \quad \Rightarrow \quad R = \frac{L_d}{2\sin\alpha}$$

The curvature is:

$$\kappa = \frac{1}{R} = \frac{2\sin\alpha}{L_d}$$

The required angular velocity:

$$\omega = v\kappa = \frac{2v \sin\alpha}{L_d}$$

**Step 3: Block diagram description (2 marks)**

```
Path DB → Closest Point Finder → Look-ahead Point Generator
       → Angle α Calculator → Curvature κ Calculator
       → Inverse Kinematics → Motor PWM Output
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often confuse **Pure Pursuit** with **Stanley** or **PID line-following**. Pure Pursuit uses a **look-ahead distance** along the path; Stanley uses **cross-track error** at the front axle; PID line-following uses **sensor error**. Mixing them is a common mark-loss.

---

### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Design a Move-to-a-Point behavior for a differential drive robot. Derive the control law and explain the role of $\alpha$, $\rho$, and the wrapping function. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the hardware and software design of a differential drive line-following robot using a 5-sensor IR array. Mention the PID tuning procedure. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Q.B(a) — Move-to-Point Design (7 marks)

**Step 1: Geometric setup (1 mark)**

Goal $G = (x_g, y_g)$, current pose $(x, y, \theta)$. Define:

$$\rho = \sqrt{(x_g - x)^2 + (y_g - y)^2}$$

$$\phi_g = \text{atan2}(y_g - y, x_g - x)$$

$$\alpha = \phi_g - \theta$$

**Step 2: Heading wrapping (1 mark)**

Because angles are modulo $2\pi$, the raw difference can be outside $(-\pi, \pi]$. Wrap using:

$$\alpha \leftarrow \text{atan2}(\sin\alpha, \cos\alpha)$$

**Step 3: Control law (3 marks)**

A proportional control law with feed-forward is:

$$v = k_\rho \rho$$

$$\omega = k_\alpha \alpha + k_\beta \rho \sin\alpha$$

- $k_\rho$ controls the **approach speed**.
- $k_\alpha$ controls **how aggressively** the robot realigns.
- $k_\beta \rho \sin\alpha$ is **feed-forward** that biases the robot to keep moving forward even when $\alpha$ is small, preventing circular orbiting around the goal.

**Step 4: Termination (1 mark)**

The behavior halts when $\rho \le \rho_{tol}$ (typically $\rho_{tol} = 0.05$ m).

**Step 5: Stability note (1 mark)**

The system is **locally asymptotically stable** at the goal point $(0, 0, \theta_g)$ in polar coordinates if $k_\rho, k_\alpha, k_\beta > 0$.

---

#### Solution to Q.B(b) — Line Follower Design (7 marks)

**Step 1: Hardware (2 marks)**

| Component | Specification | Purpose |
|-----------|---------------|---------|
| Chassis | Acrylic / Aluminum 200×150 mm | Mechanical frame |
| Motors | 12 V DC geared, 100 RPM | Drive wheels |
| Wheels | Rubber, $r = 4$ cm | Traction |
| IR Array | TCRT5000 × 5 sensors, spacing 1.5 cm | Line detection |
| MCU | Arduino Mega / STM32 | Control |
| H-Bridge | L298N × 1 | Motor driver |
| Power | 11.1 V LiPo, 2200 mAh | Energy source |

**Step 2: Sensor reading and error (2 marks)**

Each IR sensor returns $S_i \in [0, 1023]$. Convert to weighted blackness:

$$\bar{S}_i = 1 - \frac{S_i}{1023}$$

Line position error:

$$e = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{5} (i - 3) \bar{S}_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{5} \bar{S}_i}$$

**Step 3: PID control (2 marks)**

$$\omega(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau) d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

Forward velocity is held constant: $v = v_{const} \approx 0.2$ m/s.

**Step 4: Tuning procedure (1 mark)**

1. Set $K_i = K_d = 0$. Increase $K_p$ until the robot oscillates.
2. Add $K_d$ until oscillation is suppressed.
3. Add small $K_i$ to remove steady-state drift on long straight sections.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently skip the **sampling period**. State the loop runs at $\Delta t = 10$ ms (i.e., $100$ Hz) — without this, the PID equations are ambiguous.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ A differential drive has **two control inputs** $(v, \omega)$ but **three configuration variables** $(x, y, \theta)$ → it is **non-holonomic**.
- ✅ Forward kinematics: $v = \dfrac{r(\omega_r + \omega_l)}{2}$ and $\omega = \dfrac{r(\omega_r - \omega_l)}{L}$.
- ✅ Inverse kinematics: $v_r = v + \dfrac{L\omega}{2}$ and $v_l = v - \dfrac{L\omega}{2}$.
- ✅ Pose update must use the **old** $\theta_k$, not $\theta_{k+1}$.
- ✅ Go-to-point requires the **`atan2`** function (not `arctan`) and **angle wrapping** to $(-\pi, \pi]$.
- ✅ Line following uses a **weighted centroid** over the sensor array for the line position error.
- ✅ Pure Pursuit curvature is $\kappa = \dfrac{2\sin\alpha}{L_d}$ — derived from the inscribed-angle theorem.
- ✅ Look-ahead distance $L_d$ must be **clamped** to avoid spin-out at small values.
- ✅ PID tuning order: **P → D → I** (never start with I alone).
- ✅ Sampling period $\Delta t$ must be quoted explicitly when discussing discrete PID.
- ✅ H-Bridge direction pins control **forward/backward**, and **ENABLE PWM** pins control speed.
- ✅ Encoder resolution determines **odometry accuracy**; KTU may ask for $\Delta s = n \cdot 2\pi r / N_{ticks}$ per pulse.
- ✅ Always **saturate** $v$ and $\omega$ between $[\pm v_{max}]$ and $[\pm \omega_{max}]$ to respect actuator limits.
- ✅ In a 14-mark case study, structure the answer as **Hardware → Kinematics → Behavior → Tuning → Results**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Control of a Differential Drive Robot in ROS to Move to a Goal Location
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET744 - MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Control of a Differential Drive Robot in ROS to Move to a Goal Location

# Control of a Differential Drive Robot in ROS to Move to a Goal Location

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Differential Drive Robot — Formal Definition

A **Differential Drive Robot (DDR)** is a non-holonomic mobile robot whose locomotion is achieved by independently controlling the angular velocities of two coaxial drive wheels mounted on a common axle. The robot's motion (translation and rotation) emerges from the *difference* between the left and right wheel velocities, hence the term "differential."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PEAET744 — Module 4):**
> A differential drive robot is a two-wheeled (plus a passive caster) ground vehicle whose planar pose $\mathbf{q} = [x, y, \theta]^{\top}$ evolves under the unicycle kinematic model, where linear velocity $v$ and angular velocity $\omega$ are produced by the differential actuation of the left and right wheels.

The pose of the robot in the 2-D plane is captured by a **3-DOF configuration vector**:

$$
\mathbf{q} = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ \theta \end{bmatrix}
$$

where:
- $x, y$ — Position of the robot's reference point (usually the center of the wheel axle) in the world frame (meters, **m**).
- $\theta$ — Orientation of the robot's heading with respect to the world $X$-axis (radians, **rad**).

### 1.2 ROS — Formal Definition

**Robot Operating System (ROS)** is an open-source, meta-operating system for robots that provides hardware abstraction, device drivers, libraries, visualizers, message-passing, and package management. It is *not* a real operating system but a middleware framework running on top of Linux (Ubuntu).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key ROS Building Blocks Used in this Module:**
> - **Node** — A single executable process performing computation.
> - **Topic** — A named bus over which nodes exchange messages via publish/subscribe.
> - **Message** — A strictly typed data structure (e.g., `geometry_msgs/Twist`).
> - **Master (`roscore`)** — The name service that enables nodes to find each other.
> - **tf** — A distributed coordinate-frame tracking system.
> - **Launch File** — An XML file to start multiple nodes and set parameters simultaneously.

### 1.3 Goal-Reaching Control — Formal Definition

Goal-reaching control (also called **Go-to-Goal** or **Point-Stabilization**) is the problem of designing a feedback control law that drives a mobile robot from an arbitrary initial pose $\mathbf{q}_0 = [x_0, y_0, \theta_0]^{\top}$ to a specified goal pose $\mathbf{q}_g = [x_g, y_g, \theta_g]^{\top}$ while respecting the robot's kinematic constraints (non-holonomic for differential drive).

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **🚗 Real-World Analogy — "Parallel Parking a Car with a Helper":**
>
> Imagine you are sitting in a toy car with two independently powered rear wheels (no steering wheel). A *helper outside the car* watches where the car is and where it needs to go. Every 0.1 seconds, the helper shouts two commands to you:
> 1. **"Press the gas pedal this hard"** (linear velocity $v$)
> 2. **"Turn the steering this way, this fast"** (angular velocity $\omega$)
>
> The helper is the **controller node**, you are the **robot**, the car's motors are the **actuators**, and the shouts are **ROS topics** (`/cmd_vel`). The helper computes $v$ and $\omega$ by comparing *how far* and *which direction* the car is from the parking spot — that comparison is the **error**.

A second analogy: a differential drive robot behaves exactly like a **unicycle** (a single-wheel bicycle). All the motion arises from the single contact point's forward velocity and the lean-induced rotation — except the unicycle's "lean" is achieved by spinning the two wheels at different rates.

### 1.5 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value / Symbol | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Gravitational acceleration | $g = 9.81$ | $\text{m/s}^2$ |
| Sampling period (ROS rate) | $T_s = 0.1$ | s (i.e., **10 Hz**) |
| Wheel radius | $r$ | m |
| Wheel separation (track) | $L$ or $d$ | m |
| Maximum linear velocity | $v_{\max}$ | m/s |
| Maximum angular velocity | $\omega_{\max}$ | rad/s |
