# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Unicycle Kinematic Model

The differential drive robot is kinematically equivalent to the **unicycle model**. The continuous-time state evolution is governed by:

$$
\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:**
> - The robot can *only* move forward along its own heading direction $\theta$.
> - Sideways motion is **impossible** instantaneously (non-holonomic constraint: $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$).
> - The robot must therefore *rotate* to align with a goal before translating.

## 2.2 Forward Kinematics — Wheel Velocities → Robot Motion

Given the angular velocities of the left and right wheels, $\omega_L$ and $\omega_R$ (rad/s), the robot's linear and angular velocities are:

$$
v = \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}, \qquad \omega = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L}
$$

where:
- $r$ — radius of each drive wheel (m)
- $L$ — distance between the two wheel contact points (m)

### Worked Reasoning
- **Why averaging for $v$?** Each wheel contributes $r\omega_i$ at the contact patch. The robot's center moves at the *average* of the two wheel speeds (both wheels push the chassis forward together).
- **Why subtraction for $\omega$?** When wheels spin in opposite directions, the robot *rotates in place*. When they differ, the robot follows a circular arc with radius $R = v/\omega$.

## 2.3 Inverse Kinematics — Robot Motion → Wheel Velocities

Solving the above equations for the wheel angular velocities:

$$
\omega_R = \frac{2v + L\omega}{2r}, \qquad \omega_L = \frac{2v - L\omega}{2r}
$$

This is the equation used by the **motor driver / microcontroller** to translate high-level commands $(v, \omega)$ into low-level wheel commands.

## 2.4 Goal-Reaching Control Law (Proportional Controller)

Let the goal be $\mathbf{q}_g = [x_g, y_g]^{\top}$ (heading at goal is often ignored in basic go-to-goal, or handled separately).

**Step 1: Compute position error in the world frame.**

$$
\Delta x = x_g - x, \qquad \Delta y = y_g - y
$$

**Step 2: Transform error into the robot's local frame.**

$$
\rho = \sqrt{(\Delta x)^2 + (\Delta y)^2}, \qquad \alpha = \operatorname{atan2}(\Delta y, \Delta x) - \theta
$$

where:
- $\rho \geq 0$ — Euclidean distance to goal (m)
- $\alpha \in (-\pi, \pi]$ — heading error (rad), wrapped to the principal value
- $\operatorname{atan2}(\cdot)$ — the four-quadrant inverse tangent, returns the signed angle from the world $X$-axis to the vector pointing from robot to goal

**Step 3: Apply the proportional control law.**

$$
v = K_\rho \, \rho, \qquad \omega = K_\alpha \, \alpha
$$

where $K_\rho$ and $K_\alpha$ are positive scalar gains controlling the *aggressiveness* of translation and rotation, respectively.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Observation:**
> - When $\rho \to 0$ (robot is at the goal), $v \to 0$ naturally — **self-stabilizing**.
> - When $\alpha \neq 0$, $\omega \neq 0$ — robot rotates to align first.
> - The gains must satisfy $K_\rho > 0$ and $K_\alpha > 0$; otherwise the closed-loop system is **unstable**.

## 2.5 Velocity Saturation (Safety)

Real robots have actuator limits. The controller outputs must be clipped:

$$
v_{\text{sat}} = \operatorname{sgn}(v)\min(|v|, v_{\max}), \qquad \omega_{\text{sat}} = \operatorname{sgn}(\omega)\min(|\omega|, \omega_{\max})
$$

Also, as $\rho$ decreases, the robot may overshoot due to large $\omega$. A practical fix is to **scale down $v$** when the heading error is large:

$$
v = K_\rho \, \rho \, \cos(\alpha)
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why the $\cos(\alpha)$ factor?**
> If the robot is pointing *away* from the goal, $\cos(\alpha) < 0$, and so $v < 0$ — the robot reverses. If pointing tangentially, $v = 0$, forcing the robot to rotate first. This is the **De Luca-Oriolo smooth go-to-goal controller** commonly used in academic and KTU references.

## 2.6 ROS Message Types Used

| ROS Message | Used For | Key Fields |
|---|---|---|
| `geometry_msgs/Twist` | Velocity command to robot | `linear.x` ($v$), `angular.z` ($\omega$) |
| `nav_msgs/Odometry` | Robot pose + velocity from wheel encoders | `pose.pose.position`, `pose.pose.orientation` |
| `geometry_msgs/PoseStamped` | Goal pose with timestamp | `pose.position.x/y/z`, `pose.orientation` |
| `geometry_msgs/Pose2D` | Simplified 2D pose | `x, y, theta` |
| `tf2_msgs/TFMessage` | Coordinate transforms | `transforms[].transform.translation/rotation` |

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dot{x} = v\cos\theta$ | $X$-velocity of robot center | m/s |
| 2 | $\dot{y} = v\sin\theta$ | $Y$-velocity of robot center | m/s |
| 3 | $\dot{\theta} = \omega$ | Angular velocity of robot | rad/s |
| 4 | $v = \dfrac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}$ | Forward kinematics (linear) | m/s |
| 5 | $\omega = \dfrac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L}$ | Forward kinematics (angular) | rad/s |
| 6 | $\omega_R = \dfrac{2v + L\omega}{2r}$ | Inverse kinematics (right wheel) | rad/s |
| 7 | $\omega_L = \dfrac{2v - L\omega}{2r}$ | Inverse kinematics (left wheel) | rad/s |
| 8 | $\rho = \sqrt{(x_g-x)^2 + (y_g-y)^2}$ | Distance to goal | m |
| 9 | $\alpha = \operatorname{atan2}(y_g-y,\, x_g-x) - \theta$ | Heading error | rad |
| 10 | $v = K_\rho \rho \cos(\alpha)$ | Smooth linear control law | m/s |
| 11 | $\omega = K_\alpha \alpha$ | Proportional angular control law | rad/s |
| 12 | $\dot{\mathbf{q}}_{k+1} = \mathbf{q}_k + T_s \cdot \dot{\mathbf{q}}_k$ | Euler integration (odometry update) | mixed |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:**
> Always wrap $\alpha$ to $(-\pi, \pi]$ using `atan2` *before* applying the control law. Examiners specifically check whether the student correctly distinguishes $\operatorname{atan2}$ from $\arctan$.

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Warehouse AMRs (Autonomous Mobile Robots):** Amazon Kiva robots use variants of go-to-goal controllers to navigate between shelves and packing stations.
- **AGVs in factories:** Hyundai, BMW, and Toyota assembly-line bots use differential drive + go-to-goal + line-following hybrid control.
- **Domestic vacuum robots:** iRobot Roomba implements similar proportional control layered with behavior trees.
- **Agricultural robots:** Row-following bots use go-to-goal to traverse between plant rows.
- **ROS + Gazebo:** The de-facto academic and industrial prototyping environment for testing control laws before deployment.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Forward Kinematics (Wheel → Robot)

**Setup:** Consider a differential drive robot with two wheels of radius $r$ separated by distance $L$. The contact point of the left wheel with the ground is at position $(-L/2, 0)$ and the right wheel is at $(+L/2, 0)$ in the robot's local frame.

**Step 1 — Linear velocity of each wheel contact.**

The left wheel contact point moves at linear speed $v_L = r\omega_L$ and the right at $v_R = r\omega_R$.

**Step 2 — Velocity of the robot's center.**

The center of the axle is exactly midway between the two contact points. If the wheels rotate at the same speed, the robot translates straight with the average speed:

$$
v = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2} = \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Angular velocity of the robot.**

The instantaneous center of rotation (ICR) lies on the line joining the two wheel contacts. Let the ICR be at signed distance $R$ from the robot's center along the lateral axis. Then:

$$
v_R = \omega(R + L/2), \qquad v_L = \omega(R - L/2)
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $\omega$ and $R$.**

Subtracting the two equations:

$$
v_R - v_L = \omega \cdot L \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L} = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L}
$$

This is the standard result. The ICR distance is:

$$
R = \frac{L(v_R + v_L)}{2(v_R - v_L)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> When $v_R = v_L$, the denominator vanishes, $R \to \infty$ — the robot moves in a **straight line** (no rotation).

## 3.2 Derivation of Inverse Kinematics (Robot → Wheel)

**Step 1 — Start from forward kinematics.**

$$
v = \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2} \quad (A), \qquad \omega = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L} \quad (B)
$$

**Step 2 — Multiply (A) by 2 and (B) by $L$.**

$$
2v = r(\omega_R + \omega_L), \qquad L\omega = r(\omega_R - \omega_L)
$$

**Step 3 — Add the two equations.**

$$
2v + L\omega = 2r\omega_R \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \omega_R = \frac{2v + L\omega}{2r}
$$

**Step 4 — Subtract the two equations.**

$$
2v - L\omega = 2r\omega_L \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \omega_L = \frac{2v - L\omega}{2r}
$$

Verified.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Go-to-Goal Control Law

**Step 1 — Define the error vector in the world frame.**

$$
\mathbf{e}_w = \begin{bmatrix} x_g - x \\ y_g - y \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Rotate the error vector into the robot's body frame using $R(\theta)$.**

$$
\mathbf{e}_b = R(-\theta)\,\mathbf{e}_w = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & \sin\theta \\ -\sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_g - x \\ y_g - y \end{bmatrix}
$$

Computing the components:

$$
e_x = (x_g - x)\cos\theta + (y_g - y)\sin\theta, \qquad e_y = -(x_g - x)\sin\theta + (y_g - y)\cos\theta
$$

**Step 3 — Express in polar form.**

$$
\rho = \sqrt{e_x^2 + e_y^2} = \sqrt{(x_g - x)^2 + (y_g - y)^2}
$$

$$
\alpha = \operatorname{atan2}(e_y,\, e_x) = \operatorname{atan2}(y_g - y,\, x_g - x) - \theta
$$

(The $\operatorname{atan2}$ subtraction holds because rotation preserves angles up to a constant offset.)

**Step 4 — Propose a Lyapunov candidate function.**

A common choice to prove stability is:

$$
V = \frac{1}{2} K_\rho \rho^2 + \frac{1}{2} K_\alpha \alpha^2 \geq 0
$$

**Step 5 — Compute $\dot{V}$ and require $\dot{V} \leq 0$.**

$$
\dot{V} = K_\rho \rho \dot{\rho} + K_\alpha \alpha \dot{\alpha}
$$

Using $\dot{\rho} = -v\cos\alpha$ and $\dot{\alpha} = -\omega + v\frac{\sin\alpha}{\rho}$ (standard unicycle identities), and substituting $v = K_\rho\rho\cos\alpha$, $\omega = K_\alpha\alpha + K_\rho\sin\alpha\cos\alpha$:

$$
\dot{V} = -K_\rho^2 \rho^2 \cos^2\alpha - K_\alpha^2 \alpha^2 \leq 0
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Since $V \geq 0$ and $\dot{V} \leq 0$, by **Lyapunov's direct method**, the equilibrium $(\rho, \alpha) = (0, 0)$ is **asymptotically stable**. The robot converges to the goal.

## 3.4 Euler Integration for Odometry

At each control tick, the new pose is computed from the current pose and the commanded velocity:

$$
\mathbf{q}_{k+1} = \mathbf{q}_k + T_s \cdot \begin{bmatrix} v_k \cos\theta_k \\ v_k \sin\theta_k \\ \omega_k \end{bmatrix}
$$

where $T_s$ is the loop period (e.g., $0.1$ s for a 10 Hz controller).

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example

> [!TIP]
> **Sample KTU-style Problem:**
> A differential drive robot has $r = 0.05$ m, $L = 0.30$ m. It receives the command $v = 0.20$ m/s and $\omega = 0.50$ rad/s. Compute the wheel angular velocities.

**Solution:**

$$
\omega_R = \frac{2v + L\omega}{2r} = \frac{2(0.20) + (0.30)(0.50)}{2(0.05)} = \frac{0.40 + 0.15}{0.10} = \frac{0.55}{0.10} = 5.5 \text{ rad/s}
$$

$$
\omega_L = \frac{2v - L\omega}{2r} = \frac{0.40 - 0.15}{0.10} = \frac{0.25}{0.10} = 2.5 \text{ rad/s}
$$

> Both wheels spin in the same direction (forward), but the right wheel is faster, so the robot curves **leftward**.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 ROS Node-Topic Architecture for Go-to-Goal

This diagram shows the publish/subscribe communication between the controller node, the simulator/hardware, and the odometry source.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[goalPublisher]:::pub -- geometry_msgs/PoseStamped --> T1[/goalTopic/]:::topic
    T1 -- subscribes --> B[goToGoalControllerNode]:::ctrl
    C[odometrySourceNode]:::pub -- nav_msgs/Odometry --> T2[/odomTopic/]:::topic
    T2 -- subscribes --> B
    B -- geometry_msgs/Twist --> T3[/cmdVelTopic/]:::topic
    T3 -- subscribes --> D[robotActuatorDriver]:::drv
    D -- wheelRPM --> E[(DifferentialDriveRobot)]:::robot
    E -- wheelEncoders --> C
    F[tfBroadcaster]:::pub -- tf2_msgs/TFMessage --> T4[/tfTopic/]:::topic
    F -- reads pose from --> C
    T4 -- subscribes --> G[rvizVisualizer]:::viz

    classDef pub fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0050b3,color:#000
    classDef topic fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000,color:#000
    classDef ctrl fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#38761d,color:#000
    classDef drv fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450,color:#000
    classDef robot fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#9673a6,color:#000
    classDef viz fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#6c8ebf,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Control Loop

```mermaid
graph TB
    SP[Setpoint: Goal Pose xg yg]:::inp --> SUM1((Sum node)):::sum
    FB[Feedback: Current Pose x y theta]:::fb --> SUM1
    SUM1 -- error ex ey --> ERR[Error Computation Module: rho atan2 alpha]:::proc
    ERR --> CTRL[Proportional Controller: Krho Kalpha]:::proc
    CTRL -- v omega --> SAT[Velocity Saturation: vmax wmax]:::proc
    SAT -- vsat wsat --> INV[Inverse Kinematics: wR wL]:::proc
    INV --> ACT[Motor Driver PWM Signals]:::drv
    ACT --> ROBOT[(Differential Drive Robot)]:::robot
    ROBOT -- wheel encoders --> ODOM[Odometry / TF Broadcaster]:::proc
    ODOM --> FB

    classDef inp fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0050b3,color:#000
    classDef fb fill:#fce5cd,stroke:#b45f06,color:#000
    classDef proc fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#38761d,color:#000
    classDef sum fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000,color:#000
    classDef drv fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450,color:#000
```


## 4.4 Control Loop Flowchart (Discrete-Time Tick)

```mermaid
graph TB
    S([Start: initialize node subscribe topics]):::start --> R1[Read current pose from odom]:::proc
    R1 --> R2[Read goal pose from goal topic]:::proc
    R2 --> R3[Compute rho and alpha using atan2]:::proc
    R3 --> D{Is rho less than tolerance?}:::dec
    D -- Yes --> STOP[Publish zero Twist: stop robot]:::proc
    STOP --> END1([End: goal reached]):::end
    D -- No --> CTRL[Compute v and omega with Krho Kalpha]:::proc
    CTRL --> SAT[Clip v and omega to vmax and wmax]:::proc
    SAT --> PUB[Publish Twist to cmd vel]:::proc
    PUB --> SL[rospy sleep Ts seconds]:::proc
    SL --> R1
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Differential Drive Kinematics
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, PEAET744 Module 4]**

> Define the kinematic model of a differential drive robot. Derive the expressions for linear and angular velocities of the robot in terms of wheel angular velocities.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The differential drive robot is governed by the **unicycle kinematic model**:

$$
\dot{x} = v\cos\theta, \qquad \dot{y} = v\sin\theta, \qquad \dot{\theta} = \omega
$$

For a robot with wheel radius $r$ and axle length $L$, the linear and angular velocities produced by wheel angular velocities $\omega_L$ and $\omega_R$ are:

$$
v = \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}, \qquad \omega = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L}
$$

*(1 mark — definition, 2 marks — derivation)*

### Question 2: ROS Architecture
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, PEAET744 Module 4]**

> List the principal ROS message types used in a differential drive go-to-goal application and state the role of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. `geometry_msgs/Twist` — Used to publish velocity commands $v$ and $\omega$ on `/cmd_vel`. *(1 mark)*
2. `nav_msgs/Odometry` — Provides estimated robot pose $(x, y, \theta)$ from wheel encoders via `/odom`. *(1 mark)*
3. `geometry_msgs/PoseStamped` — Carries the goal pose $(x_g, y_g)$ to the controller via the goal topic. *(1 mark)*

---

## Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Model Question, PEAET744 Module 4]**

> **(a)** Derive the inverse kinematics of a differential drive robot, expressing the left and right wheel angular velocities in terms of commanded linear velocity $v$ and angular velocity $\omega$. Given $r = 0.04$ m and $L = 0.25$ m, compute $\omega_L$ and $\omega_R$ for $v = 0.30$ m/s and $\omega = 1.0$ rad/s. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Design a proportional go-to-goal controller for the robot. Derive the control law for $v$ and $\omega$ in terms of position error $\rho$ and heading error $\alpha$. Show that the chosen control law is asymptotically stable using Lyapunov analysis. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution to A(a)

**Step 1 — Starting point: forward kinematics.**

$$
v = \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}, \qquad \omega = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L}
$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 2 — Multiply first equation by 2 and second by $L$.**

$$
2v = r(\omega_R + \omega_L), \qquad L\omega = r(\omega_R - \omega_L)
$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 3 — Add and subtract to isolate each wheel.**

Adding:

$$
2v + L\omega = 2r\omega_R \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \omega_R = \frac{2v + L\omega}{2r}
$$

Subtracting:

$$
2v - L\omega = 2r\omega_L \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \omega_L = \frac{2v - L\omega}{2r}
$$

*(2 marks for derivation)*

**Step 4 — Numerical substitution.**

$$
\omega_R = \frac{2(0.30) + (0.25)(1.0)}{2(0.04)} = \frac{0.60 + 0.25}{0.08} = \frac{0.85}{0.08} = 10.625 \text{ rad/s}
$$

$$
\omega_L = \frac{2(0.30) - (0.25)(1.0)}{0.08} = \frac{0.60 - 0.25}{0.08} = \frac{0.35}{0.08} = 4.375 \text{ rad/s}
$$

*(3 marks for computation and units)*

#### Model Solution to A(b)

**Step 1 — Define error coordinates.**

Let the goal be $(x_g, y_g)$. The position error in the robot's local frame is obtained by rotating the world-frame error by $-\theta$:

$$
\rho = \sqrt{(x_g - x)^2 + (y_g - y)^2}
$$

$$
\alpha = \operatorname{atan2}(y_g - y,\, x_g - x) - \theta
$$

*(2 marks)*

**Step 2 — Propose the control law.**

$$
v = K_\rho \, \rho \cos\alpha, \qquad \omega = K_\alpha \, \alpha + K_\rho \sin\alpha \cos\alpha
$$

*(2 marks)*

**Step 3 — Lyapunov stability proof.**

Choose $V(\rho, \alpha) = \frac{1}{2}(K_\rho \rho^2 + K_\alpha \alpha^2)$. This is positive definite.

Using the unicycle identities $\dot{\rho} = -v\cos\alpha$ and $\dot{\alpha} = -\omega + v\frac{\sin\alpha}{\rho}$:

$$
\dot{V} = K_\rho \rho \dot{\rho} + K_\alpha \alpha \dot{\alpha} = -K_\rho^2 \rho^2 \cos^2\alpha - K_\alpha^2 \alpha^2 \leq 0
$$

*(2 marks)*

**Step 4 — Conclusion.**

Since $V \geq 0$ and $\dot{V} \leq 0$ with $\dot{V} = 0$ only at $(\rho, \alpha) = (0, 0)$, by Lyapunov's direct method, the equilibrium is **globally asymptotically stable**. *(1 mark)*

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 / Model Question, PEAET744 Module 4]**

> **(a)** With a clear block diagram, describe the ROS architecture for a differential drive robot performing go-to-goal navigation. Identify the topics, messages, and nodes involved. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Write the pseudo-code (or mathematical algorithm) that the controller node executes in every control tick. Show how the pose is obtained from the `/odom` topic, the goal is read, and the `Twist` command is published. Include velocity saturation. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution to B(a)

**Step 1 — Identify the nodes.**

1. **Goal Publisher Node** — Publishes the target pose to `/goal_topic`.
2. **Go-to-Goal Controller Node** — Subscribes to `/odom` and `/goal_topic`, publishes to `/cmd_vel`.
3. **Odometry Source Node** — Publishes `/odom` from wheel encoders or Gazebo plugin.
4. **Robot Driver Node** (`ros_control` / `diff_drive_controller`) — Subscribes to `/cmd_vel`, drives motors.
5. **TF Broadcaster** — Publishes the transform between `odom` and `base_link` frames.
6. **RViz Visualizer** — Subscribes to TF and topics for visualization. *(3 marks)*

**Step 2 — Identify the topics and messages.**

| Topic | Message Type | Direction |
|---|---|---|
| `/odom` | `nav_msgs/Odometry` | Published by odometry node |
| `/cmd_vel` | `geometry_msgs/Twist` | Published by controller |
| `/goal_topic` | `geometry_msgs/PoseStamped` | Published by goal publisher |
| `/tf` | `tf2_msgs/TFMessage` | Published by TF broadcaster |

*(2 marks)*

**Step 3 — Block diagram (verbal description).**

The chain is: **Goal Publisher → Goal Topic → Controller ← Odometry Topic ← Encoders**, and **Controller → /cmd_vel → Motor Driver → Wheels → Robot**. TF runs in parallel to keep the coordinate frames consistent. *(2 marks)*

#### Model Solution to B(b)

**Step 1 — Node initialization.**

```
Initialize ROS node 'go_to_goal_controller'
Subscribe to /odom (callback updates pose x, y, theta)
Subscribe to /goal_topic (callback updates goal xg, yg)
Publish to /cmd_vel (geometry_msgs/Twist)
Set control rate = 10 Hz, Ts = 0.1 s
Set gains K_rho, K_alpha and saturation v_max, w_max
```

*(2 marks)*

**Step 2 — Main control loop.**

```
While not shutdown:
    Wait for next tick (rate.sleep())
    If goal not received: continue
    
    Compute dx = xg - x
    Compute dy = yg - y
    Compute rho = sqrt(dx^2 + dy^2)
    Compute alpha = atan2(dy, dx) - theta
    Wrap alpha to (-pi, pi]
    
    If rho < tolerance:
        Publish Twist(0, 0, 0)
        Log 'Goal reached' and continue
    
    v_cmd = K_rho * rho * cos(alpha)
    w_cmd = K_alpha * alpha
    
    v_cmd = clip(v_cmd, -v_max, v_max)
    w_cmd = clip(w_cmd, -w_max, w_max)
    
    Publish Twist(v_cmd, 0, 0, 0, 0, w_cmd)
```

*(4 marks)*

**Step 3 — Notes on saturation.**

```
def clip(value, lo, hi):
    return max(lo, min(hi, value))
```

*(1 mark)*

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $\cos\alpha$ factor** in $v$ causes the robot to translate sideways or overshoot wildly. Examiners specifically check this.
> 2. **Not wrapping $\alpha$ to $(-\pi, \pi]$** leads to discontinuous $\omega$ at the $\pm\pi$ boundary — marks deducted for unstable control.
> 3. **Forgetting to clip $v$ and $\omega$** causes the controller to send physically impossible commands to motors. Always saturate.
> 4. **Confusing holonomic and non-holonomic constraints** — the differential drive CANNOT move sideways; only rotation can re-orient it. Marks heavily deducted if claimed otherwise.
> 5. **Mixing up `/odom` frame and `/map` frame** — the controller typically operates in the `odom` frame for short-term goals; long-term SLAM uses `map`.

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU ESE — Module 4**

- ✅ **Differential drive** = two independently driven coaxial wheels + passive caster. Non-holonomic; cannot move sideways instantaneously.
- ✅ **Pose vector** $\mathbf{q} = [x, y, \theta]^{\top}$ — 3 DOF in 2-D plane.
- ✅ **Forward kinematics:** $v = \dfrac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}$, $\omega = \dfrac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L}$.
- ✅ **Inverse kinematics:** $\omega_R = \dfrac{2v + L\omega}{2r}$, $\omega_L = \dfrac{2v - L\omega}{2r}$.
- ✅ **Polar error:** $\rho = \sqrt{(x_g - x)^2 + (y_g - y)^2}$, $\alpha = \operatorname{atan2}(y_g - y,\, x_g - x) - \theta$.
- ✅ **Proportional control law:** $v = K_\rho \rho \cos\alpha$, $\omega = K_\alpha \alpha + K_\rho \sin\alpha\cos\alpha$.
- ✅ **Always** wrap $\alpha$ to $(-\pi, \pi]$.
- ✅ **Always** saturate $v$ and $\omega$ to actuator limits $v_{\max}$, $\omega_{\max}$.
- ✅ **Lyapunov candidate:** $V = \frac{1}{2}(K_\rho \rho^2 + K_\alpha \alpha^2)$ gives $\dot{V} = -K_\rho^2 \rho^2\cos^2\alpha - K_\alpha^2 \alpha^2 \leq 0$ ⟹ asymptotically stable.
- ✅ **Key ROS messages:** `Twist` (output), `Odometry` (input), `PoseStamped` (goal).
- ✅ **Key topics:** `/cmd_vel` (controller → driver), `/odom` (driver → controller), `/goal_topic` (user → controller), `/tf` (transform broadcaster).
- ✅ **Key nodes:** Goal Publisher, Controller, Odometry Source, Motor Driver, TF Broadcaster, RViz.
- ✅ **Simulation stack:** ROS master + Gazebo + `ros_control` + `diff_drive_controller` plugin + URDF.
- ✅ **Control rate:** typically 10 Hz ($T_s = 0.1$ s).
- ✅ **At goal** ($\rho \to 0$): $v \to 0$, robot stops automatically — *self-stabilizing*.
- ✅ **Stability condition:** $K_\rho > 0$ AND $K_\alpha > 0$ — otherwise unstable.
- ✅ **Equation numbers** (KTU reference): memorize formulas **(1)–(12)** from the cheat sheet above.

> [!TIP]
> **One-line memory hook:** *"Average for translation, difference for rotation — atan2 for alignment, Lyapunov for assurance."*
